topics.title: Transmission Modes: Serial, Parallel, Asynchronous, Synchronous, Simplex, Half Duplex, Full Duplex
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT522 - COMPUTER COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Transmission Modes: Serial, Parallel, Asynchronous, Synchronous, Simplex, Half Duplex, Full Duplex

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Transmission Mode"?

In **Computer Communication**, *transmission mode* defines the **mechanism by which binary data (bits) is physically moved between a source (sender) and a destination (receiver)** over a communication channel. The KTU 2024 syllabus classifies transmission along **two orthogonal axes**:

| Axis | Classifies Based On | Options |
|------|--------------------|--------|
| **Axis 1 — Data Path Topology** | How bits travel across the wire | Serial vs Parallel |
| **Axis 2 — Timing & Control** | How sender/receiver stay in step | Asynchronous vs Synchronous |
| **Axis 3 — Direction of Flow** | Who can talk, and when | Simplex, Half-Duplex, Full-Duplex |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Insight:** Students frequently confuse *transmission mode* with *transmission medium*. **Mode** = the *logical* arrangement (serial, duplex, etc.). **Medium** = the *physical* path (copper, fiber, RF). Examiners love to trap students here.

## 1.2 Plain-English Intuition (Conceptual Analogy)

> [!NOTE]
> 🛣️ **The Highway Analogy**
> Imagine two cities connected by roads.
> - **Serial Transmission** = A **single-lane road** — cars (bits) travel one after another in a queue.
> - **Parallel Transmission** = A **multi-lane highway** — 8 cars (a byte) travel side-by-side, each in their own lane.
> - **Asynchronous** = Cars leave the source city *without* a fixed departure schedule; the receiver checks "who's there" each time.
> - **Synchronous** = Cars leave on a *strict timetable*; the receiver knows exactly when to expect them.
> - **Simplex** = A **one-way street** (e.g., a TV broadcast tower → your TV).
> - **Half-Duplex** = A **single-track railway with signals** — only one train moves at a time (e.g., walkie-talkies).
> - **Full-Duplex** = A **two-way highway** — traffic flows both ways simultaneously (e.g., a phone call).

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Definitions

- **Bit Duration ($T_b$):** Time taken to transmit **1 bit**. Measured in **seconds**.
- **Baud Rate (B):** Number of **signal units (symbols)** transmitted per second. Unit: **symbols/second (baud)**.
- **Bit Rate ($R_b$):** Number of **bits** transmitted per second. Unit: **bits/second (bps)**.
- Relationship: $R_b = B \times \log_2(M)$, where $M$ is the number of signal levels.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For a binary (2-level) signal, $M = 2$, so $R_b = B \times 1 = B$.** Baud rate = Bit rate only for binary encoding (NRZ).

## 1.4 Formal KTU Definitions

> [!NOTE]
> **1. Serial Transmission:** Data bits are transmitted **sequentially, one bit at a time, over a single communication channel**, using either one or two signal lines (one for data, one for ground/reference).
> *(KTU Module 1, Unit 1.1)*

> [!NOTE]
> **2. Parallel Transmission:** Data bits (typically grouped as a byte = **8 bits**) are transmitted **simultaneously** across **multiple parallel channels** (usually **8 or more wires**).
> *(KTU Module 1, Unit 1.2)*

> [!NOTE]
> **3. Asynchronous Transmission:** A transmission scheme where **each character (byte) is framed independently**, with **start and stop bits** at its boundaries. The receiver uses the start bit to **resynchronize its clock** for every character. **No shared long-term clock** between sender and receiver.

> [!NOTE]
> **4. Synchronous Transmission:** A transmission scheme where data is sent as a **continuous stream of bits** organized into **fixed-size frames**. Sender and receiver maintain **precisely synchronized clocks** (typically via a shared clock line or embedded clock recovery like PLL). **No per-character overhead.**

> [!NOTE]
> **5. Simplex Communication:** A **unidirectional** communication mode. The channel operates in **one direction only**. The receiver cannot transmit back. Example: **Radio broadcasting, TV transmission.**

> [!NOTE]
> **6. Half-Duplex Communication:** A **bidirectional** mode where both stations **can transmit, but only ONE at a time**. The channel is shared and must be toggled. Example: **Walkie-Talkie (Push-To-Talk).**

> [!NOTE]
> **7. Full-Duplex Communication:** A **simultaneous bidirectional** mode where both stations **transmit and receive at the same time** using the same channel. Requires either **two physical channels** or **channel-division** techniques (FDD/TDD). Example: **Telephone conversation, Ethernet full-duplex links.**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Serial vs Parallel Transmission

### 2.1.1 Serial Transmission

**Operational Logic (Step-by-Step):**
1. A **single data line** (plus a ground reference) is used between DTE and DCE.
2. The sender's **Parallel-to-Serial converter (e.g., UART)** shifts out bits LSB-first (or MSB-first, per protocol) at a fixed baud rate.
3. The receiver's **Serial-to-Parallel converter** samples the line and reassembles the byte.
4. Bits travel **one after another** — hence the name *serial*.

**Advantages:**
- **Lower cost**: Only 1 (or 2) wires needed → cheaper cabling.
- **Suitable for long-distance** communication (e.g., RS-232, USB, SATA, PCIe serial).
- **No skew problem** (since only one bit is on the line at a time).

**Disadvantages:**
- **Slower** than parallel at the same clock rate (1 bit per clock vs N bits per clock).
- Requires **serial-to-parallel conversion hardware** (UART/SERDES).

### 2.1.2 Parallel Transmission

**Operational Logic (Step-by-Step):**
1. **N parallel wires** (typically N = 8) carry N bits simultaneously.
2. Each wire is assigned a fixed bit position (e.g., D0, D1, ..., D7).
3. A common **clock/strobe line** signals when all N bits are valid on the bus.
4. The receiver latches all N bits at the rising/falling edge of the strobe.

**Advantages:**
- **N× faster** in theory (8 bits/cycle vs 1 bit/cycle).
- **Simpler hardware** at low speeds (no SERDES needed).

**Disadvantages:**
- **Expensive cabling** (8+ wires + shield + ground).
- **Skew problem**: At high frequencies, bits on different wires arrive at *slightly different times* due to differing propagation delays → bits are misaligned at the receiver. **This is why modern high-speed interconnects (PCIe, SATA, USB 3.x, 100 GbE) are all SERIAL despite needing high speed.**
- Limited to **short distances** (typically < 1–2 meters; e.g., internal computer bus, old IEEE-488, printer port).

### 2.1.3 KTU Comparison Table

| Parameter | Serial | Parallel |
|---|---|---|
| Wires needed | **1 (data) + ground** | **N (typically 8) + ground + strobe** |
| Speed @ same clock | $1 \times$ bit/cycle | $N \times$ bits/cycle |
| Cost | **Low** | **High** |
| Skew issue | **None** | **Severe at high speed** |
| Max practical distance | **Kilometers** (with drivers) | **Meters** (short bus) |
| Example | USB, SATA, RS-232, PCIe | Old printer port, internal PCI/ISA bus |

## 2.2 Asynchronous vs Synchronous Transmission

### 2.2.1 Asynchronous Transmission — Detailed Mechanics

A character (typically **7 or 8 bits**, plus optional parity) is wrapped in a **frame**:

```
[ IDLE (1) ] [ START (0) ] [ D0 ] [ D1 ] ... [ D7 ] [ PARITY? ] [ STOP (1) ] [ IDLE (1) ] ...
```

- **Idle line = MARK (logic 1)** — no transmission.
- **Start bit (1 bit, logic 0)** — falling edge triggers receiver's local clock.
- **Data bits (5–8 bits)** — sampled at the **center** of each bit period for noise immunity.
- **Parity bit (optional, 1 bit)** — even or odd error check.
- **Stop bit(s) (1, 1.5, or 2 bits, logic 1)** — guarantees next start bit's falling edge is detectable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Overhead Calculation:** For 8 data bits + 1 parity + 1 stop + 1 start = **11 bits** to send 8 data bits.
> **Efficiency** = $8/11 \approx \mathbf{72.7\%}$. This overhead is the price of asynchrony.

### 2.2.2 Synchronous Transmission — Detailed Mechanics

- No start/stop bits. Data is sent as a **continuous bitstream** framed into larger blocks.
- Frames bounded by **flag bytes / preamble** (e.g., HDLC flag = `01111110`) or **SYN characters** (e.g., Bisync).
- A **shared clock** is maintained via:
  - A **separate clock line** (e.g., SPI, I²C SCL).
  - **Clock recovery** from data transitions using a **Phase-Locked Loop (PLL)** (e.g., Ethernet, SONET).
- Bits are grouped into frames/blocks typically of **hundreds to thousands of bytes**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Efficiency** ≈ **100%** (negligible overhead per bit; only per-frame headers). This is why synchronous links dominate backbone networks.

### 2.2.3 KTU Comparison Table

| Parameter | Asynchronous | Synchronous |
|---|---|---|
| Clock | **Independent at each end** | **Shared / Recovered** |
| Framing | **Per-character** (start/stop) | **Per-block** (flag/SYN) |
| Overhead | **~20–30%** (start/stop) | **< 1%** (frame headers only) |
| Hardware cost | **Low** (simple UART) | **High** (PLL, buffers) |
| Speed | **Low–Medium** (up to ~115.2 kbps classic UART) | **High** (Mbps → Gbps) |
| Error probability | Higher (re-sync each char) | Lower |
| Example | RS-232 terminal, keyboard | HDLC, Ethernet, SONET, PCIe |

## 2.3 Directional Modes — Simplex, Half-Duplex, Full-Duplex

### 2.3.1 Simplex
- **One direction only.** A → B is allowed; B → A is **physically impossible** (no return path).
- Channel bandwidth is fully dedicated to one direction.
- Example: **FM radio broadcast**, TV, paging systems, telemetry from a satellite.

### 2.3.2 Half-Duplex (HDX)
- **Both directions, but NEVER simultaneously.** The channel must be "turned around."
- Requires a **direction-control protocol** (e.g., RTS/CTS in RS-485, Push-To-Talk).
- Effective throughput = **half** of full-duplex (because each direction only gets a slice of time).
- Example: **Walkie-Talkie**, CB radio, classic Ethernet (10BASE-T using CSMA/CD), Wi-Fi (half-duplex RF).

### 2.3.3 Full-Duplex (FDX)
- **Both directions simultaneously.** Sender transmits while receiver transmits.
- Implementation requires either:
  - **Two separate physical channels** (e.g., 4-wire telephone: 2 for each direction), OR
  - **Frequency-Division Duplex (FDD)**: split bandwidth into two sub-bands (e.g., upstream/downstream cable), OR
  - **Time-Division Duplex (TDD)**: alternate very fast in time (used in 4G/5G, Wi-Fi).
- Example: **Landline telephone, full-duplex Ethernet (100BASE-TX, switched), modern cellular voice.**

### 2.3.4 KTU Comparison Table

| Mode | Direction | Simultaneous? | Channel Use | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Simplex** | A → B only | N/A | 100% one-way | TV broadcast |
| **Half-Duplex** | A ↔ B (alternating) | **No** | 50% each direction (avg) | Walkie-Talkie |
| **Full-Duplex** | A ↔ B (concurrent) | **Yes** | 100% each direction | Telephone |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Asynchronous Efficiency | $\eta_{async} = \dfrac{D}{D + S + P + St}$ | $D$=data bits, $S$=start, $P$=parity, $St$=stop |
| 2 | Default Async Frame (8-N-1) | $\eta = \dfrac{8}{8+1+0+1} = 72.7\%$ | Most serial ports |
| 3 | Sync Efficiency | $\eta_{sync} = 1 - \dfrac{H}{L + H} \approx 1$ | $H$=header, $L$=payload |
| 4 | Bit Rate ↔ Baud | $R_b = B \times \log_2(M)$ | M-ary signaling |
| 5 | Bit Duration | $T_b = \dfrac{1}{R_b}$ | Time per bit |
| 6 | Frame Transfer Time | $T_f = \dfrac{N_{bits}}{R_b}$ | $N_{bits}$ = total bits incl. overhead |
| 7 | Half-Duplex Throughput | $R_{HDX} = \dfrac{R_{full}}{2}$ | Ideal, no guard time |
| 8 | Parallel Speedup (ideal) | $R_{\parallel} = N \times R_{serial}$ | N-wire bus |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineer's Real-World Utility:** Mastering transmission modes is the foundation for selecting the right **UART/USART chip** for embedded design (asynchronous), the right **SERDES** for high-speed board-to-board links (serial-synchronous), and the right **duplexer** for RF front-ends (FDD vs TDD). Every IoT device, every 5G base station, every data-center switch makes these design choices.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Asynchronous Transmission Efficiency (8-N-1)

> **Problem:** For a standard UART configuration (8 data bits, No parity, 1 stop bit, 1 start bit — abbreviated **8-N-1**), compute the transmission efficiency when sending 1 character.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify each component in the frame.**

For the standard 8-N-1 frame:
- Start bits = **1**
- Data bits = **8**
- Parity bits = **0** (None)
- Stop bits = **1**

**Step 2 — Compute total bits transmitted per character.**

$$
N_{total} = S + D + P + St
$$
$$
N_{total} = 1 + 8 + 0 + 1 = 10 \text{ bits per character}
$$

*Reasoning:* Every character sent requires 1 start bit (to wake up the receiver), 8 actual data bits, and 1 stop bit (to ensure the line returns to idle before the next character). Even though we only "care" about 8 bits, all 10 must be transmitted.

**Step 3 — Define efficiency.**

Efficiency is the **ratio of useful (information-bearing) bits to total bits transmitted**.

$$
\eta = \frac{\text{Data bits}}{\text{Total bits transmitted}} = \frac{D}{N_{total}}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute values.**

$$
\eta = \frac{8}{10} = 0.8 = 80\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note for KTU 2024:** Many textbooks (and the older syllabus) quote the asynchronous overhead as **"1 start + 1 stop" for 8 data bits = 10-bit frame → 80% efficiency.** Earlier sections of this note quoted 11 bits / 8 data because they included parity. **Always read the configuration string carefully!**

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Synchronous Frame Efficiency

> **Problem:** A synchronous link uses HDLC framing. Each frame contains **1500 bytes of payload** and **6 bytes of header/flag/FCS overhead**. Compute the efficiency.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Convert everything to bits (canonical units).**

$$
\text{Payload} = 1500 \text{ bytes} = 1500 \times 8 = 12000 \text{ bits}
$$
$$
\text{Overhead} = 6 \text{ bytes} = 6 \times 8 = 48 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute total frame size.**

$$
N_{total} = 12000 + 48 = 12048 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply efficiency formula.**

$$
\eta_{sync} = \frac{L}{L + H} = \frac{12000}{12048}
$$
$$
\eta_{sync} \approx 0.99602 = 99.60\%
$$

**Step 4 — Compare with asynchronous (8-N-1, sending 1500 bytes character-by-character).**

$$
N_{total, async} = 1500 \times 10 = 15000 \text{ bits (one byte becomes 10 bits)}
$$
$$
\eta_{async} = \frac{12000}{15000} = 80.00\%
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the absolute overhead saved per 1500-byte transfer.**

$$
\text{Bits saved} = 15000 - 12048 = 2952 \text{ bits}
$$
$$
\text{Percentage saved} = \frac{2952}{15000} \times 100\% = 19.68\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight for Examiners:** Synchronous transmission **scales beautifully** with large payloads. For short messages (e.g., a few bytes from a sensor), the per-frame overhead can dominate — sometimes making asynchrony competitive. This is why **UARTs** are still ubiquitous in low-speed sensor links.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Total Time to Transmit a File

> **Problem:** A 1 MB file (1,048,576 bytes) is to be transmitted over each of the following links at $R_b = 115200$ bps. Compute the time required:
> (a) Asynchronous 8-N-1
> (b) Synchronous HDLC with 6-byte overhead per frame, payload 1500 bytes/frame

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step (a) — Asynchronous:**

$$
N_{bits} = 1{,}048{,}576 \text{ bytes} \times 10 \text{ bits/byte} = 10{,}485{,}760 \text{ bits}
$$
$$
T_a = \frac{N_{bits}}{R_b} = \frac{10{,}485{,}760}{115{,}200} \approx 91.03 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step (b) — Synchronous:**

$$
\text{Number of frames} = \left\lceil \frac{1{,}048{,}576}{1500} \right\rceil = \lceil 699.05 \rceil = 700 \text{ frames}
$$
$$
\text{Last frame payload} = 1{,}048{,}576 - (699 \times 1500) = 1{,}048{,}576 - 1{,}048{,}500 = 76 \text{ bytes}
$$
$$
\text{Frame sizes in bits: 699 frames} \times (1500 + 6) \times 8 \text{ bits} + 1 \text{ frame} \times (76 + 6) \times 8 \text{ bits}
$$
$$
= 699 \times 12048 + 1 \times 656
$$
$$
= 8{,}421{,}552 + 656 = 8{,}422{,}208 \text{ bits}
$$
$$
T_b = \frac{8{,}422{,}208}{115{,}200} \approx 73.11 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Comparison:**
$$
\Delta T = T_a - T_b = 91.03 - 73.11 = 17.92 \text{ seconds saved}
$$
$$
\text{Speedup} = \frac{T_a}{T_b} = \frac{91.03}{73.11} \approx 1.245 \times
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Even on a slow 115.2 kbps link, synchronous framing saves ~17.9 s on a 1 MB file — a ~24.5% speedup with **zero hardware speed change**. This is precisely why backbone telecom is universally synchronous.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Half-Duplex vs Full-Duplex Effective Throughput

> **Problem:** A 1 Mbps full-duplex link is being used in half-duplex mode (alternating equal directions). What is the effective one-way throughput?

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — In FDX**, both directions can use the full 1 Mbps simultaneously:
$$
R_{FDX, \rightarrow} = 1 \text{ Mbps}, \quad R_{FDX, \leftarrow} = 1 \text{ Mbps}
$$

**Step 2 — In HDX**, only one direction transmits at a time. Assume equal time allocation:
$$
T_{\rightarrow} = T_{\leftarrow} = \frac{T_{total}}{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Effective one-way throughput**:
$$
R_{HDX, \rightarrow} = \frac{T_{\rightarrow}}{T_{total}} \times R_{link} = \frac{1}{2} \times 1 \text{ Mbps} = 0.5 \text{ Mbps}
$$

**Step 4 — Real-world guard time penalty** (TX/RX switch takes $\Delta t$ per turn):
$$
R_{HDX, real} = \frac{T_{total}/2 - \Delta t}{T_{total}} \times R_{link}
$$
For $T_{total} = 1$ s and $\Delta t = 5$ ms:
$$
R_{HDX, real} = \frac{0.500 - 0.005}{1} \times 1 \text{ Mbps} = 0.495 \text{ Mbps}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner Tip:** Don't forget to mention that real half-duplex systems suffer from **propagation delay, turnaround time, and CSMA/CD collision overhead** — the simple 1/2 factor is an upper bound.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Transmission Mode Selection

| Use-Case | Recommended Mode | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Keyboard → PC | **Asynchronous Serial** | Low speed, simple, intermittent |
| CPU ↔ RAM | **Parallel Synchronous** | Short distance, ultra-high speed |
| PCIe GPU slot | **Serial Synchronous (SERDES)** | High speed + long distance within chassis |
| Walkie-Talkie | **Half-Duplex** | Single RF channel, manual PTT |
| Landline Telephone | **Full-Duplex** | 2-wire with hybrid + echo canceller, OR 4-wire |
| TV Broadcast | **Simplex** | One-way content distribution |
| 5G Smartphone | **Full-Duplex (TDD/FDD)** | Bidirectional voice + data simultaneously |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: Serial vs Parallel Transmission (Bit-Level View)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph SERIAL["SERIAL TRANSMISSION (1 wire, sequential)"]
        direction LR
        S1["Byte: 10110011"]
        S2["T0: 1"]
        S3["T1: 0"]
        S4["T2: 1"]
        S5["T3: 1"]
        S6["T4: 0"]
        S7["T5: 0"]
        S8["T6: 1"]
        S9["T7: 1"]
        S10["RCV (re-assembled)"]
        S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6 --> S7 --> S8 --> S9 --> S10
    end

    subgraph PARALLEL["PARALLEL TRANSMISSION (8 wires, simultaneous)"]
        direction LR
        P1["Byte: 10110011"]
        P2["Wire D0: 1"]
        P3["Wire D1: 1"]
        P4["Wire D2: 0"]
        P5["Wire D3: 0"]
        P6["Wire D4: 1"]
        P7["Wire D5: 1"]
        P8["Wire D6: 0"]
        P9["Wire D7: 1"]
        P10["RCV (latched at strobe)"]
        P1 --> P2
        P1 --> P3
        P1 --> P4
        P1 --> P5
        P1 --> P6
        P1 --> P7
        P1 --> P8
        P1 --> P9
        P2 --> P10
        P3 --> P10
        P4 --> P10
        P5 --> P10
        P6 --> P10
        P7 --> P10
        P8 --> P10
        P9 --> P10
    end
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: Asynchronous vs Synchronous Frame Structure

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph ASYNC["ASYNCHRONOUS FRAME (per-character overhead)"]
        direction LR
        A1["IDLE 1"]
        A2["START 0"]
        A3["D0"]
        A4["D1"]
        A5["D2"]
        A6["D3"]
        A7["D4"]
        A8["D5"]
        A9["D6"]
        A10["D7"]
        A11["PARITY"]
        A12["STOP 1"]
        A13["IDLE 1"]
        A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> A4 --> A5 --> A6 --> A7 --> A8 --> A9 --> A10 --> A11 --> A12 --> A13
    end

    subgraph SYNC["SYNCHRONOUS FRAME (block-level overhead)"]
        direction LR
        B1["FLAG 01111110"]
        B2["ADDR 8b"]
        B3["CTRL 8b"]
        B4["PAYLOAD 1000s of bytes"]
        B5["FCS 16b CRC"]
        B6["FLAG 01111110"]
        B1 --> B2 --> B3 --> B4 --> B5 --> B6
    end
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: Simplex / Half-Duplex / Full-Duplex Directional Flow

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph SIMPLEX["SIMPLEX"]
        direction LR
        SX_A["Station A SENDER ONLY"]
        SX_B["Station B RECEIVER ONLY"]
        SX_A -- "Data one way ONLY" --> SX_B
    end

    subgraph HDX["HALF-DUPLEX"]
        direction LR
        HD_A["Station A"]
        HD_B["Station B"]
        HD_A -- "Time Slot 1: A to B" --> HD_B
        HD_B -- "Time Slot 2: B to A" --> HD_A
    end

    subgraph FDX["FULL-DUPLEX"]
        direction LR
        FD_A["Station A"]
        FD_B["Station B"]
        FD_A -- "Channel 1: A to B continuously" --> FD_B
        FD_B -- "Channel 2: B to A continuously" --> FD_A
    end
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4: UART Transmit State Machine (Asynchronous Engine)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle: IDLE (Line = 1)
    StartBit: Transmit START (0)
    DataBit0: Transmit D0
    DataBit1: Transmit D1
    DataBit2: Transmit D2
    DataBit3: Transmit D3
    DataBit4: Transmit D4
    DataBit5: Transmit D5
    DataBit6: Transmit D6
    DataBit7: Transmit D7
    ParityBit: Transmit PARITY
    StopBit: Transmit STOP (1)
    Idle --> StartBit: TX triggered
    StartBit --> DataBit0: 1 bit-time
    DataBit0 --> DataBit1: 1 bit-time
    DataBit1 --> DataBit2: 1 bit-time
    DataBit2 --> DataBit3: 1 bit-time
    DataBit3 --> DataBit4: 1 bit-time
    DataBit4 --> DataBit5: 1 bit-time
    DataBit5 --> DataBit6: 1 bit-time
    DataBit6 --> DataBit7: 1 bit-time
    DataBit7 --> ParityBit: 1 bit-time
    ParityBit --> StopBit: 1 bit-time
    StopBit --> Idle: 1 bit-time
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram 5: Functional Block Diagram — Complete Transmission System

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph TX["TRANSMITTER"]
        T1["Source Data (Parallel Bytes)"]
        T2["Parallel to Serial Converter (UART/SERDES)"]
        T3["Line Encoder (NRZ, Manchester)"]
        T4["Line Driver"]
        T1 --> T2 --> T3 --> T4
    end

    subgraph CH["CHANNEL"]
        C1["Physical Medium Copper / Fiber / RF"]
    end

    subgraph RX["RECEIVER"]
        R1["Line Receiver / Equalizer"]
        R2["Clock Recovery PLL"]
        R3["Line Decoder"]
        R4["Serial to Parallel Converter"]
        R5["Destination Data"]
        R1 --> R3 --> R4 --> R5
        R1 --> R2
        R2 --> R3
    end

    T4 -- "TX Signal" --> C1
    C1 -- "RX Signal" --> R1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** This functional block diagram highlights that *all* transmission modes (serial/parallel, sync/async, simplex/duplex) ultimately share a common architectural skeleton. The *mode* only differs in **which blocks are present and how they are configured** — e.g., async uses a *start-bit detector* instead of a *PLL-based clock recovery*.

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1]**
**Differentiate between Serial and Parallel transmission. Mention one real-world example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Serial | Parallel |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Bits sent **one after another** on a single channel | Bits sent **simultaneously** on multiple channels |
| **Wires needed** | 1 data wire (+ ground) | N data wires (e.g., 8) + ground + strobe |
| **Speed** | Lower per clock | Higher per clock (N bits/cycle) |
| **Distance** | **Long** (km range) | **Short** (< few m, due to skew) |
| **Cost** | Lower | Higher |
| **Example** | **USB, SATA, RS-232** | **Old printer port (LPT), internal CPU-memory bus** |

*(Examiner's note: Full marks for clear table + correct examples.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 1]**
**Explain the difference between Half-Duplex and Full-Duplex communication with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Half-Duplex:** Both stations can transmit, but **NOT simultaneously**. The channel must be turned around. Example: **Walkie-Talkie (Push-To-Talk)**.
- **Full-Duplex:** Both stations can transmit **simultaneously**, either via two physical channels (4-wire telephone) or channel-division (FDD/TDD in cellular). Example: **Modern mobile phone call**.
- **Key contrast:** Half-Duplex effective one-way throughput ≈ **half** of Full-Duplex; FDX needs more bandwidth or hardware (duplexer).

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> *(Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern: each Module typically contains one 14-mark question with internal choice.)*

---

### **🔹 Question A (14 Marks) — Serial vs Parallel + Asynchronous Framing**

#### **Q-A(a). [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**Compare Serial and Parallel transmission. Explain why modern high-speed interfaces (PCIe, SATA, USB 3.x) use serial links despite parallel links being theoretically faster.**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Comparison Table [3 Marks]:**

| Parameter | Serial | Parallel |
|---|---|---|
| Wires | 1 + ground | N + ground + strobe |
| Speed/clock | 1 bit/cycle | N bits/cycle |
| Skew | None | **Severe at high freq** |
| Max distance | Long (km) | Short (m) |
| Cost | Low | High |
| Example | PCIe, SATA | ISA, old LPT |

**(ii) Why Modern High-Speed Links Use Serial [4 Marks]:**

1. **Skew Problem:** In a parallel bus at high clock rates, signals on different wires travel at *slightly different velocities* due to manufacturing tolerances, dielectric variation, and crosstalk. The receiver sees bits **misaligned** beyond a tolerable window → bit errors. **Serial eliminates skew** because only one bit is on the wire at a time.

2. **Clock Frequency Headroom:** Without skew, the SERializer/DESerializer (SERDES) can be clocked at **multi-GHz rates** on a single differential pair, achieving aggregate bandwidth exceeding any practical parallel bus.

3. **Cost & Pin Count:** A single differential pair (2 wires) replaces 8+ parallel wires + strobe + ground. Fewer pins → smaller connectors, cheaper cables, easier routing on PCBs.

4. **EMI & Crosstalk:** A differential serial pair can be tightly coupled, rejecting common-mode noise far better than a wide parallel bus.

> **Conclusion:** Serial + high clock rate > Parallel + low clock rate, because parallel is bounded by skew. *(Examples: PCIe Gen 5 = 32 GT/s serial; SATA 6 Gbps; USB 3.2 = 20 Gbps.)*

---

#### **Q-A(b). [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
**With a neat diagram, explain the frame format of Asynchronous Serial Transmission. A system uses 8 data bits, even parity, and 2 stop bits. Compute the efficiency for sending one ASCII character.**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Asynchronous Frame Format [4 Marks]:**

```
| IDLE (1) | START (0) | D0 | D1 | D2 | D3 | D4 | D5 | D6 | D7 | PARITY | STOP1 (1) | STOP2 (1) | IDLE (1) ...
```

- **Idle:** Line held at logic 1 (MARK) when no data.
- **Start Bit (1 bit, logic 0):** Falling edge alerts receiver; receiver resets its local clock.
- **Data Bits (8 bits):** LSB-first typically; sampled at center of each bit time.
- **Parity Bit (1 bit):** Even parity → total number of 1s (data + parity) is even.
- **Stop Bits (2 bits, logic 1):** Returns line to idle; guarantees next start bit is detectable.
- Receiver resynchronizes on every start bit — no long-term clock dependency.

**(ii) Efficiency Calculation [3 Marks]:**

Configuration: **8 data, 1 parity, 2 stop, 1 start = 8-E-2**

$$
N_{total} = S + D + P + St = 1 + 8 + 1 + 2 = 12 \text{ bits}
$$

$$
\eta = \frac{D}{N_{total}} = \frac{8}{12} = \frac{2}{3} \approx 66.67\%
$$

> **Note:** This is the *least efficient* standard async format; this is why 8-N-1 (80%) is far more common.

---

### **🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — Synchronous Transmission + Duplex Modes**

#### **Q-B(a). [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Explain Synchronous Transmission in detail. Compare it with Asynchronous Transmission in a tabular form (at least 6 points).**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Synchronous Transmission Explanation [4 Marks]:**

Synchronous transmission sends data as a **continuous stream of bits**, grouped into **fixed-size blocks/frames** (typically hundreds to thousands of bytes). Sender and receiver maintain a **shared, precisely aligned clock** using either:
- A **dedicated clock line** (e.g., SPI SCL, I²C), OR
- **Clock recovery from the data stream** using a **Phase-Locked Loop (PLL)** (e.g., Ethernet, SONET, PCIe).

Each frame is bounded by **flags/preambles** (e.g., HDLC flag `01111110`) and contains an **address field, control field, payload, and Frame Check Sequence (FCS)** for error detection. Because there are no per-character start/stop bits, **efficiency approaches 100%**.

**(ii) Comparison Table (6 points minimum) [3 Marks]:**

| Parameter | Asynchronous | Synchronous |
|---|---|---|
| Clock | Independent at each end | Shared / PLL-recovered |
| Framing unit | Per-character (start/stop bits) | Per-frame (flag bytes) |
| Overhead | ~20–30% | < 1% (frame headers) |
| Hardware | Simple (UART) | Complex (PLL, large buffers) |
| Speed | Low (≤ 115.2 kbps classic) | Very high (Mbps → Gbps) |
| Typical use | Terminals, sensors | Backbone networks, PCIe |

---

#### **Q-B(b). [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Explain the three communication modes: Simplex, Half-Duplex, and Full-Duplex. For each, draw the channel model and give two real-world examples. Mention one advantage and one disadvantage of Full-Duplex over Half-Duplex.**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Simplex [1.5 Marks]:**
- **Definition:** Unidirectional; data flows only from Station A to Station B. Station B cannot reply.
- **Diagram:** A ────► B
- **Examples:** (1) **FM Radio broadcast**, (2) **TV transmission from tower to TV set**.
- **Pros:** Simple, no contention. **Cons:** No feedback/acknowledgement.

**(ii) Half-Duplex [1.5 Marks]:**
- **Definition:** Bidirectional but **non-simultaneous**. Both stations share one channel; only one transmits at a time.
- **Diagram:** A ◄────► B (with turn-around)
- **Examples:** (1) **Walkie-Talkie (Push-To-Talk)**, (2) **Old shared-bus Ethernet with CSMA/CD**.
- **Pros:** Uses single channel. **Cons:** Effective throughput halved.

**(iii) Full-Duplex [1.5 Marks]:**
- **Definition:** Bidirectional and **simultaneous**. Both stations transmit and receive at the same time.
- **Diagram:** A ═══════► B (channel 1), A ◄═══════ B (channel 2)
- **Examples:** (1) **Landline telephone** (with hybrid/echo canceller), (2) **Switched full-duplex Ethernet (100BASE-TX, 1 Gbps)**, (3) **5G cellular with FDD**.
- **Pros:** Double throughput. **Cons:** Needs **duplexer** (for FDD) or tight **synchronization** (for TDD); echo cancellation required.

**(iv) FDX vs HDX — One Pro, One Con of FDX [2.5 Marks]:**

| Aspect | Full-Duplex Advantage over Half-Duplex | Full-Duplex Disadvantage vs Half-Duplex |
|---|---|---|
| Throughput | **Doubles** effective throughput (both directions concurrent) | Requires **2× channel capacity** or complex duplexing |
| Latency | Lower — no need to wait for turn-around | Needs **echo cancellation** (significant DSP cost) |
| Hardware | — | **More expensive** (duplexer, two transceivers) |
| Protocol | Simpler MAC (no collision avoidance needed in switched FDX) | **More complex RF frontend** design |

---

> [!WARNING]
> ⚠️ **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing "mode" with "medium"** — Serial is a mode, copper is a medium. Marks lost for mixing terminology.
> 2. **Forgetting start bit** in async frame diagrams — examiners count it as a separate entity from data.
> 3. **Computing efficiency wrong** — using total bits as denominator is correct; mixing bits/bytes loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Drawing only arrows for duplex modes** — examiners expect **explicit labels** ("simultaneous" vs "alternating") and **two separate channels** for FDX.
> 5. **Stating "synchronous is always better"** — wrong; for short bursty traffic (sensors), async overhead is amortized differently and async can be simpler/cheaper.
> 6. **Confusing half-duplex with simplex** — half-duplex **does** allow both directions, just not at the same time.
> 7. **No mention of PLL/clock recovery** in sync answers — this is a favorite follow-up question worth 2 marks.

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Transmission mode** describes **how bits flow**, not the medium. Three independent axes: **Path (Serial/Parallel)**, **Timing (Async/Sync)**, **Direction (Simplex/HDX/FDX)**.
- **Serial = 1 bit at a time on 1 wire** (cheap, long-distance, skew-free). **Parallel = N bits at a time on N wires** (fast in theory, skew-limited, expensive).
- **Modern high-speed I/O is SERIAL** because parallel hits a **skew wall**; SERDES + multi-GHz clocks win.
- **Asynchronous** = per-character **start + stop bits**, **independent clocks**, ~**20–30% overhead**, simple UARTs.
- **Synchronous** = per-frame **flags/headers**, **shared/PLL-recovered clock**, **>99% efficiency**, used in backbone networks.
- **Standard async frame 8-N-1**: 10 bits per byte → **80% efficiency**.
- **Standard sync (HDLC)**: ~1500 B payload + ~6 B overhead → **~99.6% efficiency**.
- **Simplex** = one-way only (TV, radio). **Half-Duplex** = both ways, but **not at once** (walkie-talkie). **Full-Duplex** = both ways **simultaneously** (phone, switched Ethernet).
- **Half-Duplex throughput ≈ ½ of FDX** (ideal case, ignoring turn-around overhead).
- **FDX needs two channels OR FDD/TDD** to achieve simultaneity — additional hardware/dsp cost.
- **Asynchronous works without clock lines** — cheap, but resyncs every character → higher error probability.
- **Synchronous needs clock recovery (PLL)** or a clock line — more complex hardware, but reliable at Gbps speeds.
- **Key formulas to memorize:**
  - $\eta_{async} = D/(S+D+P+St)$
  - $R_b = B \cdot \log_2(M)$
  - $T_f = N_{bits}/R_b$
- **Real-world examples to drop in answers for full marks:** RS-232 (async serial), USB (sync serial), SATA (sync serial), SPI (sync serial with clock line), I²C (sync serial), PCIe (sync serial), Walkie-Talkie (HDX), Telephone (FDX), TV (Simplex), 5G (FDX with FDD/TDD).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Switching: Circuit Switching and Packet Switching
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT522 - COMPUTER COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Switching: Circuit Switching and Packet Switching

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Switching in a Communication Network?

**Switching** is the mechanism by which data is routed from a source to a destination across a network comprising multiple intermediate nodes (switches/routers). Since a direct physical link between every pair of devices is physically impossible and economically infeasible, intermediate switching nodes forward the data hop-by-hop until it reaches the intended receiver.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Switching is the foundational concept of Module 1. Every higher-layer protocol (TCP, IP, ATM) is built upon one of the switching paradigms discussed here.

A **Switch** is a physical or logical device that accepts incoming data, makes a routing decision, and forwards the data on an appropriate outgoing link. The strategy used to make this decision — whether a dedicated path is reserved or whether each packet is independently routed — defines the **switching technique**.

---

## 1.2 The Three Classical Switching Techniques

The literature classifies switching into three fundamental families:

1. **Circuit Switching** — A dedicated physical path is established *before* data transfer begins.
2. **Message Switching** — The entire message is stored at each intermediate node and forwarded as a whole (Store-and-Forward). Largely obsolete today.
3. **Packet Switching** — The message is broken into smaller units called *packets*; each packet is independently routed through the network.

```
              Switching Techniques (Module 1 Focus)
              ─────────────────────────────────────
                       │
       ┌───────────────┼────────────────┐
       │               │                │
  Circuit         Message            Packet
  Switching       Switching          Switching
       │                               │
       │                       ┌───────┴───────┐
       │                       │               │
       │                  Datagram      Virtual Circuit
       │                  Service       (VC) Service
       │                                 │
       │                            ┌────┴────┐
       │                            │         │
       │                       PVC (Permanent)  SVC (Switched)
       │
       └── Three phases: Setup → Data Transfer → Teardown
```

---

## 1.3 Circuit Switching — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Standard):** *Circuit switching is a switching technique in which a dedicated physical communication path (a circuit) is established between the source and destination nodes **before** any data is transmitted. This path forms a continuous chain of dedicated links that remains reserved for the entire duration of the communication session, regardless of whether data is actively being transmitted.*

**Conceptual Analogy — The Railway Reservation Analogy 🚆**

Imagine you want to travel from Kochi to Delhi by train. You book an entire train compartment (a "circuit") for your exclusive use, even if you plan to sleep through most of the journey. Once booked, no one else can use that compartment until you cancel the reservation, even if it's empty 70% of the time. You pay for the *reservation of the path*, not for the *amount of data moved*. This perfectly mirrors how circuit-switched networks behave — the bandwidth is paid for and reserved whether or not the line carries data.

**Real-world instance:** The legacy Public Switched Telephone Network (PSTN) — when you dial a number and the other party picks up, a physical circuit is held open for the entire call duration.

---

## 1.4 Packet Switching — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Standard):** *Packet switching is a switching technique in which the source message is divided into smaller, fixed-or-variable-sized blocks called **packets**. Each packet carries a header containing destination addressing and control information, and is independently routed through the network using the Store-and-Forward principle. The shared links are allocated on a per-packet basis, and idle capacity can be utilized by packets from other users.*

**Conceptual Analogy — The Postcard Analogy ✉️**

Suppose you want to send a 100-page report to a friend in another city. Instead of hiring an entire truck (the equivalent of circuit switching), you tear out every page, write your friend's address on the back of each one, and drop them in separate envelopes (packets). The post office (the network) may deliver envelope #5 before envelope #2, and they may even travel by completely different routes — but as long as the friend reassembles them in order, the message arrives intact. Crucially, **the truck carrying someone else's parcels can also carry yours**, which dramatically improves efficiency.

**Two Flavours of Packet Switching:**
- **Datagram Service (Connectionless):** Each packet is treated as an independent unit (e.g., the Internet's IP).
- **Virtual Circuit Service (Connection-Oriented):** A logical path is established first; all packets of the session follow it (e.g., ATM, Frame Relay, X.25).

---

## 1.5 Key Terminology — At a Glance

| Term | Meaning | Used In |
|---|---|---|
| **Dedicated Path** | A reserved, exclusive end-to-end channel | Circuit Switching |
| **Store-and-Forward** | Node fully receives a packet before forwarding it | Packet Switching |
| **Packet** | A small, addressable unit of data with header + payload | Packet Switching |
| **Datagram** | An independently-routed packet (no setup phase) | Datagram Service |
| **VC** | Virtual Circuit — a logical pre-defined route | Virtual-Circuit Service |
| **PVC** | Permanent Virtual Circuit — pre-configured by operator | Frame Relay / ATM |
| **SVC** | Switched Virtual Circuit — established per session on demand | X.25 / ATM |
| **Setup Delay** | Time to establish the path before transmission | Both (more in circuit) |
| **Propagation Delay** | Time for a signal to traverse a physical medium | Both |
| **Transmission Delay** | Time to push all bits of a packet onto the link | Both |

---

## 1.6 Why These Two Switching Modes Matter in Practice

| Domain | Switching Mode Used | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Traditional Telephone (PSTN) | **Circuit Switching** | Constant bit rate voice; latency must be bounded |
| Internet (IP) | **Packet Switching (Datagram)** | Bursty traffic; resilience to node failure |
| ATM / Frame Relay (legacy WAN) | **Packet Switching (VC)** | Mix of guaranteed QoS with statistical multiplexing |
| Optical Backbone (SONET/SDH) | **Circuit Switching** | High-bandwidth TDM circuits with strict guarantees |
| 4G/5G Data Plane | **Packet Switching** | IP-based core networks |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When a question asks *"Why is the Internet not circuit-switched?"* the answer is rooted in **statistical multiplexing** — packet switching exploits the bursty nature of data traffic to serve more users with the same physical bandwidth.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Phases of Circuit Switching

A circuit-switched session has a strict life-cycle of **three sequential phases**. Every textbook answer on circuit switching must explicitly reference all three to score full marks.

### Phase 1 — Circuit Establishment (Setup Phase)

1. The source station sends a *setup request* to the destination via a signalling protocol (e.g., SS7 in PSTN).
2. Each intermediate switch examines the request and **reserves a free outgoing trunk** along the path.
3. If a free path exists all the way to the destination, an acknowledgement is returned.
4. **Total Setup Time** = sum of signalling propagation + processing at each node.

If no path can be found, the call is **blocked** (call blocking probability — modelled by **Erlang B** formula, but that's outside Module 1 scope).

### Phase 2 — Data Transfer

- A **continuous, dedicated path** exists end-to-end.
- Data is transmitted as a steady bit stream at a constant rate equal to the link's capacity.
- No addressing is needed per bit — the path itself is the identifier.
- Throughput is **deterministic and constant** = full link bandwidth.

### Phase 3 — Circuit Teardown

1. After transmission completes, either station sends a *teardown signal*.
2. Each switch releases the reserved links, returning them to the free pool.
3. Acknowledgement is sent back to confirm release.

---

## 2.2 Phases of Packet Switching (Datagram Model)

The datagram flavour of packet switching has **no setup or teardown phases**. Each packet is processed independently:

### Step A — Segmentation & Encapsulation
The message of length $L$ bits is broken into $N$ packets, each of length $P$ bits. Each packet gets a header of $H$ bits, so the total on-wire size of one packet is $P + H$ bits.

### Step B — Independent Routing
Every packet carries the full destination address in its header. Each router makes a forwarding decision **independently**, based on its current routing table. Two packets of the same message may therefore take **different paths** and arrive **out of order**.

### Step C — Reassembly
The destination node re-sequences the packets using the sequence numbers embedded in the headers before delivering the message to the upper layer.

---

## 2.3 Phases of Virtual-Circuit Packet Switching

A hybrid that uses **both** a connection phase and independent per-packet forwarding:

1. **VC Setup:** A logical path is chosen; each switch creates a **Virtual Circuit Identifier (VCI)** entry in its table.
2. **Data Transfer:** Each packet carries only the short VCI (not the full address). Switches swap VCI values at each hop. All packets follow the same logical path.
3. **VC Teardown:** VCI entries are deleted from switch tables.

---

## 2.4 Detailed Comparison — Circuit vs Packet Switching

| Parameter | Circuit Switching | Packet Switching (Datagram) | Packet Switching (VC) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Dedicated path | Yes (physical) | No (logical per packet) | Yes (logical, not physical) |
| Setup phase | Required | Not required | Required |
| Resource reservation | End-to-end bandwidth | Per-packet, statistical | Per-VC bandwidth |
| Addressing | Once (during setup) | In every packet header | Short VCI in every packet |
| Packet ordering | Preserved naturally | May arrive out of order | Preserved along the VC |
| Effect of node failure | Entire call fails | Packets routed around failure | All VCs through that node fail |
| Delay | Uniform, predictable | Variable (jitter) | Less variable than datagram |
| Suitability | Voice, real-time | Bursty data | Mixed traffic |
| Bandwidth utilization | Low (wasted if silent) | High (statistical mux) | High |
| Congestion control | Done at setup (call blocking) | Per-packet queueing | Per-VC queueing |

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulae are **the most frequently tested** in KTU ESE questions on this topic. Memorize them with their boundary conditions.

### Notation Used

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $L$ | Total message length | bits |
| $P$ | Payload size per packet | bits |
| $H$ | Header size per packet | bits |
| $N$ | Number of packets in message | dimensionless |
| $d$ | Distance of one hop (link length) | meters |
| $s$ | Propagation speed of the medium | m/s (≈ $2 \times 10^8$ in fiber) |
| $R$ | Transmission rate (bandwidth) of the link | bits/second (bps) |
| $k$ | Number of hops (links) between source and destination | dimensionless |
| $t_{prop}$ | Propagation delay over one link | seconds |
| $t_{trans}$ | Transmission delay | seconds |
| $t_{proc}$ | Per-node processing delay | seconds |
| $t_{queue}$ | Queuing delay at a node | seconds |

### Core Equations

**Number of packets generated:**
$$
N = \left\lceil \dfrac{L}{P} \right\rceil
$$

**On-wire size of one packet:**
$$
S_{wire} = P + H \quad \text{[bits]}
$$

**Transmission delay (pushing bits onto the link):**
$$
t_{trans} = \dfrac{S_{wire}}{R} = \dfrac{P + H}{R} \quad \text{[seconds]}
$$

**Propagation delay (signal travel across one link):**
$$
t_{prop} = \dfrac{d}{s} \quad \text{[seconds]}
$$

**Total delay — Circuit Switching (one-way):**
$$
t_{circuit} = t_{setup} + k \cdot t_{prop} + \dfrac{L}{R} + t_{teardown}
$$

**Total delay — Packet Switching with Store-and-Forward (last packet arrival):**
$$
t_{packet} = k \cdot (t_{trans} + t_{prop} + t_{proc}) + (N-1) \cdot t_{trans}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The $(N-1) \cdot t_{trans}$** term captures the **pipelining effect**: while packet $i+1$ is being transmitted on the first link, packets $1$ through $i$ are simultaneously propagating/being transmitted on later links. This is the key advantage of packet switching.

**Total delay including header overhead (effective):**
$$
t_{packet}^{eff} = k \cdot \left( \dfrac{P+H}{R} + t_{prop} \right) + (N-1) \cdot \left( \dfrac{P+H}{R} \right)
$$

**Average packet delay across the network (simplified, no queuing):**
$$
\overline{D} = k \cdot t_{trans} + k \cdot t_{prop} + t_{queue}^{avg}
$$

**End-to-end throughput (goodput) for packet switching:**
$$
\eta = \dfrac{P}{P + H} \times 100\% \quad \text{[header efficiency]}
$$

**Condition under which packet switching becomes faster than circuit switching:**
$$
k \cdot t_{setup} > (N-1) \cdot t_{trans} + k \cdot t_{trans}
$$

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Concept | Engineering Domain | Application |
|---|---|---|
| Circuit Switching | Telecom (PSTN, ISDN, leased lines) | Voice calls, video conferencing (legacy) |
| Datagram Switching | Internet (IPv4 / IPv6) | Web, email, file transfer, streaming |
| VC Switching | ATM, Frame Relay, MPLS | Carrier WANs, VPNs |
| Statistical Multiplexing | 4G/5G core, ISP aggregation | Cost-effective bandwidth sharing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point — Statistical Multiplexing:** Packet switching is fundamentally enabled by *statistical multiplexing*. If $n$ users share a link of capacity $R$, and each is active only $p$ fraction of the time (typically $p \ll 1$), the required bandwidth is approximately $n \cdot p \cdot R$ instead of $n \cdot R$ — a savings of factor $1/p$. This is the **economic engine** behind the modern Internet.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Total Delay in Packet Switching (Pipelined Store-and-Forward)

We derive the canonical formula used by every KTU numerical on this topic. The setup is as follows:

- A message of $L$ bits is divided into $N$ packets, each of $P$ data bits.
- Each packet carries a header of $H$ bits, so on-wire size per packet is $S = P + H$ bits.
- The path from source to destination traverses $k$ links (so $k-1$ intermediate routers + 1 final link).
- Each link has transmission rate $R$ bps and propagation delay $t_{prop}$.
- Each router has negligible processing delay $t_{proc}$ per packet.

We wish to find: **the total time elapsed from the moment the source begins sending the first bit of packet 1 to the moment the destination receives the last bit of packet $N$.**

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Number of packets**

$$
N = \left\lceil \dfrac{L}{P} \right\rceil
$$

If $L$ is exactly divisible by $P$, we use $N = L/P$. (For exam simplification, assume exact divisibility unless stated otherwise.)

**Step 2 — Transmission delay for one packet onto the first link**

The time required to push all $S = P + H$ bits of one packet onto the link at rate $R$ is:

$$
t_{trans} = \dfrac{S}{R} = \dfrac{P + H}{R} \quad \text{[s]}
$$

**Step 3 — Time for packet 1 to travel end-to-end (Store-and-Forward)**

In store-and-forward, a router cannot begin transmitting packet $i$ until it has fully received packet $i$. So for a single packet traversing $k$ links:

$$
t_{one\_packet} = k \cdot t_{trans} + k \cdot t_{prop}
$$

$$
t_{one\_packet} = k \cdot \left( \dfrac{P+H}{R} \right) + k \cdot \left( \dfrac{d}{s} \right)
$$

Here $k \cdot t_{prop}$ is the cumulative propagation across all $k$ links (assuming equal link lengths $d$; otherwise sum individually).

**Step 4 — Pipelining effect across $N$ packets**

The clever bit: by the time packet 1 has cleared the *first* link (after $t_{trans}$ seconds), the source can immediately start sending packet 2 onto link 1. So each subsequent packet is "spaced" by $t_{trans}$ seconds on the first link. By the time packet $N$ finishes transmission onto link 1, packets $1$ through $N-1$ are already partway through the network.

So packet $N$ only needs to traverse the entire $k$-link path from scratch:

$$
t_{packet\_N} = k \cdot t_{trans} + k \cdot t_{prop}
$$

Packet 1 has a **head start** of $(N-1) \cdot t_{trans}$ seconds because it started transmitting $(N-1)$ packet-times earlier.

**Step 5 — Combine to get total end-to-end delay**

$$
t_{total} = \underbrace{(N-1) \cdot t_{trans}}_{\text{head start of packet 1}} + \underbrace{k \cdot t_{trans}}_{\text{transmission across all links}} + \underbrace{k \cdot t_{prop}}_{\text{propagation across all links}}
$$

**Step 6 — Final consolidated formula**

$$
\boxed{t_{total}^{packet} = \left(N + k - 1\right) \cdot t_{trans} + k \cdot t_{prop}}
$$

$$
\boxed{t_{total}^{packet} = \left(N + k - 1\right) \cdot \left( \dfrac{P+H}{R} \right) + k \cdot \left( \dfrac{d}{s} \right)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the formula:** $(N + k - 1)$ is the number of "transmission slots" the network must fill. The first $k$ slots fill the pipeline with packet 1; the remaining $(N-1)$ slots push the rest of the packets through the first link.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Total Delay in Circuit Switching

For circuit switching, after setup:

- The source transmits $L$ bits at full rate $R$ onto the first link → $L/R$ seconds.
- The signal then propagates across $k$ links → $k \cdot t_{prop}$.
- (No store-and-forward delay; the bits flow continuously through the established path.)

$$
t_{total}^{circuit} = t_{setup} + \dfrac{L}{R} + k \cdot t_{prop} + t_{teardown}
$$

For typical exam questions $t_{setup} + t_{teardown}$ are lumped into a single $t_{call}$ value.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical — Comprehensive KTU-Style Problem

**Problem Statement:**

A message of $L = 8000$ bits is to be transmitted from Station A to Station D along the path **A → B → C → D** (so $k = 3$ links). Each link has bandwidth $R = 1$ Mbps and propagation speed $s = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s. Each link is $d = 1000$ km long. The message is packetized with $P = 1000$ bits of payload and $H = 200$ bits of header per packet. Compute the total transmission delay for:

1. Circuit Switching (setup time $t_{setup} = 0.1$ s, ignore teardown)
2. Packet Switching (store-and-forward)

### Solution

**Step 1 — Common quantities**

Propagation delay per link:
$$
t_{prop} = \dfrac{d}{s} = \dfrac{1000 \times 10^3}{2 \times 10^8} = \dfrac{10^6}{2 \times 10^8} = 0.005 \text{ s} = 5 \text{ ms}
$$

Number of packets:
$$
N = \dfrac{L}{P} = \dfrac{8000}{1000} = 8 \text{ packets}
$$

On-wire size per packet:
$$
S = P + H = 1000 + 200 = 1200 \text{ bits}
$$

Transmission delay per packet:
$$
t_{trans} = \dfrac{S}{R} = \dfrac{1200}{10^6} = 1.2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 1.2 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 2 — Circuit Switching total delay**

$$
t_{circuit} = t_{setup} + \dfrac{L}{R} + k \cdot t_{prop}
$$

$$
t_{circuit} = 0.1 + \dfrac{8000}{10^6} + 3 \times 0.005
$$

$$
t_{circuit} = 0.1 + 0.008 + 0.015 = 0.123 \text{ s} = 123 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3 — Packet Switching total delay**

Using the derived formula:
$$
t_{packet} = (N + k - 1) \cdot t_{trans} + k \cdot t_{prop}
$$

$$
t_{packet} = (8 + 3 - 1) \times 1.2 \times 10^{-3} + 3 \times 0.005
$$

$$
t_{packet} = 10 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-3} + 0.015 = 0.012 + 0.015 = 0.027 \text{ s} = 27 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 4 — Comparison**

$$
\Delta t = t_{circuit} - t_{packet} = 123 - 27 = 96 \text{ ms}
$$

Packet switching is faster by 96 ms because the **120 ms of setup time** is amortized away by the pipelined transmission of 8 packets.

**Step 5 — Verdict**
$$
\boxed{\text{Packet Switching Wins: } 27 \text{ ms vs } 123 \text{ ms}}
$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When is Each Switching Technique Optimal?

| Scenario | Best Choice | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Long, continuous streaming (voice) | **Circuit Switching** | Constant bit rate; no statistical variance |
| Short, bursty data transfer (Web, email) | **Datagram Packet Switching** | No setup cost amortized over tiny flows |
| Real-time + reserved bandwidth (VoIP over ATM) | **Virtual-Circuit Packet Switching** | Combines QoS of circuit with efficiency of packet |
| Network prone to node/link failures | **Datagram** | Auto-reroutes around failures |
| Critical control signalling | **Circuit or VC** | Predictable latency required |
| Very short messages (few bytes) | **Datagram** | Circuit setup overhead dwarfs data |

---

## 3.5 Throughput Comparison — Analytical Derivation

For an end-to-end path of $k$ links each of rate $R$, transmitting $N$ packets of size $S = P+H$:

**Effective throughput (goodput) for packet switching:**
$$
\Theta_{pkt} = \dfrac{N \cdot P}{t_{total}^{packet}}
$$

Substituting the delay formula:
$$
\Theta_{pkt} = \dfrac{N \cdot P}{(N + k - 1) \cdot \frac{P+H}{R} + k \cdot t_{prop}}
$$

**Throughput for circuit switching (steady-state, ignoring setup):**
$$
\Theta_{cir} = \dfrac{L}{\frac{L}{R} + k \cdot t_{prop}} = R \cdot \dfrac{1}{1 + \frac{k \cdot t_{prop} \cdot R}{L}}
$$

As $L \to \infty$, $\Theta_{cir} \to R$. For small messages, the setup overhead dominates and $\Theta_{cir} \ll R$.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Circuit Switching — Logical Flow Diagram

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the complete lifecycle of a circuit-switched connection, from setup through teardown, including both success and blocking scenarios.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Source Station Initiates Call]) --> B[Send Setup Request to Local Switch]
    B --> C{Switch Finds Free Outgoing Trunk?}
    C -- No --> D[Return Blocking Signal to Source]
    D --> Z([Call Rejected - Busy Tone])
    C -- Yes --> E[Reserve Link, Forward Request to Next Switch]
    E --> F{All k Switches Have Free Path?}
    F -- No --> D
    F -- Yes --> G[Reserve All Links End-to-End]
    G --> H[Return Setup Acknowledgement to Source]
    H --> I([Phase 1: Setup COMPLETE])
    I --> J[Begin Continuous Data Transmission]
    J --> K([Phase 2: Data Transfer])
    K --> L{Source Finishes Data?}
    L -- No --> J
    L -- Yes --> M[Send Teardown Signal]
    M --> N[Each Switch Releases Reserved Link]
    N --> O[Send Teardown Acknowledgement]
    O --> P([Phase 3: Teardown COMPLETE])
    P --> Q([Circuit Free for New Calls])
```

## 4.2 Datagram Packet Switching — Independent Routing Diagram

The datagram model treats every packet as an autonomous unit. The diagram below shows how three packets of the **same message** can take **different physical paths** through the network.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S([Source Host S]) --> R1((Router R1))
    R1 -- Packet 1 --> R2((Router R2))
    R1 -- Packet 2 --> R3((Router R3))
    R1 -- Packet 3 --> R4((Router R4))
    R2 --> D([Destination D])
    R3 --> D
    R4 --> R5((Router R5))
    R5 --> D
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Packets 1, 2, 3 from the same message start at source S. Each carries the destination's IP address. At Router R1, the routing table may direct each packet along a different outgoing link based on current congestion — they reconverge at Destination D and are reassembled by sequence number.

## 4.3 Virtual Circuit Switching — VCI Label Swapping Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S([Source S]) -- VC:5 --> N1((Switch N1))
    N1 -- VC:12 --> N2((Switch N2))
    N2 -- VC:7 --> N3((Switch N3))
    N3 -- VC:3 --> D([Destination D])
```

**VCI Mapping Table (constructed during VC setup):**

| Switch | Incoming Port | Incoming VCI | Outgoing Port | Outgoing VCI |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| N1 | 1 | 5 | 2 | 12 |
| N2 | 1 | 12 | 3 | 7 |
| N3 | 1 | 7 | 2 | 3 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Notice that the **full destination address is NOT carried in each data packet**. Only the short VCI label (e.g., 5, 12, 7, 3) is needed at each hop. This is why virtual-circuit networks like ATM achieve very low per-packet overhead despite being connection-oriented.

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Switching Decision Engine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Incoming Packet] --> H[Parse Header]
    H --> C{Service Type?}
    C -- Connectionless Datagram --> A[Look up Destination Address in Routing Table]
    C -- Connection Oriented VC --> B[Look up Incoming VCI in VC Table]
    A --> Q[Apply Forwarding Decision to Output Queue]
    B --> VCI[Swap VCI Label]
    VCI --> Q
    Q --> T[Transmit on Selected Outgoing Link]
    T --> O([Packet Leaves the Switch])
```

## 4.5 Comparative Architecture — Side-by-Side Switching Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph CIRCUIT ["Circuit Switching Lifecycle"]
        C1([Idle]) --> C2[Setup Phase - Reserve Path]
        C2 --> C3[Data Transfer - Continuous Bit Stream]
        C3 --> C4[Teardown - Release Path]
        C4 --> C1
    end
    subgraph DATAGRAM ["Datagram Packet Switching Lifecycle"]
        D1([Idle]) --> D2[No Setup - Send Packet 1]
        D2 --> D3[Independent Routing Per Packet]
        D3 --> D4[Reassembly at Destination]
        D4 --> D5([Connection Closed After Last Packet])
    end
    subgraph VIRTUAL ["Virtual Circuit Switching Lifecycle"]
        V1([Idle]) --> V2[VC Setup - Establish Logical Path]
        V2 --> V3[Data Transfer - All Packets Use VC]
        V3 --> V4[VC Teardown - Delete VC Entries]
        V4 --> V1
    end
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — *[KTU University Exam - May 2024]*

**Differentiate between Circuit Switching and Packet Switching. List any two advantages of packet switching over circuit switching.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — keyword allocation):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
- **Circuit Switching:** A dedicated physical path is established between source and destination before data transfer; the path is reserved for the entire session.
- **Packet Switching:** The message is broken into packets; each packet is independently routed through the network using the Store-and-Forward principle.

**Differences (1 mark):**

| Aspect | Circuit Switching | Packet Switching |
|---|---|---|
| Path | Dedicated, pre-established | Shared, per-packet routing |
| Delay | Constant | Variable |

**Two Advantages of Packet Switching (1 mark):**
1. **Higher bandwidth utilization** through statistical multiplexing — idle capacity of one user can serve another's bursty traffic.
2. **Robustness to failures** — in datagram mode, packets can be re-routed around a failed node; circuit-switched calls drop entirely.

---

### Question A2 — *[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]*

**Explain the three phases of circuit switching with a neat diagram.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The three sequential phases of a circuit-switched connection are:

1. **Circuit Establishment (Setup Phase):** The source sends a setup request through the network. Each intermediate switch examines the request and reserves a free outgoing trunk. If a path to the destination exists, an acknowledgement returns to the source. *(1 mark)*

2. **Data Transfer Phase:** A continuous, dedicated physical path exists end-to-end. Data flows as a steady bit stream at constant rate equal to the link's full bandwidth. No per-packet addressing is needed. *(1 mark)*

3. **Circuit Teardown (Disconnect Phase):** After transmission ends, a teardown signal propagates through the path. Each switch releases its reserved links, returning them to the free pool. An acknowledgement confirms release. *(1 mark)*

**Diagram:** *[Draw three vertically-stacked boxes labelled Setup, Data Transfer, Teardown with arrows showing the flow.]*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### Question B-A — *[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]*  **(14 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**With a neat diagram, explain the working of Circuit Switching. Mention its advantages and disadvantages.**

**Model Answer:**

**Working of Circuit Switching (4 marks):**

A circuit-switched network operates in three sequential phases:

1. **Circuit Establishment:** When a source station wishes to communicate, it sends a setup request to its local switching node. This request carries the destination address. Each intermediate switch, upon receiving the request, examines its free-trunk table and reserves an outgoing link toward the destination. If a complete path can be reserved end-to-end, an acknowledgement signal is returned to the source.

2. **Data Transfer:** Once the circuit is established, the source and destination communicate over the dedicated path. Because the path is exclusive, the full bandwidth is available, and bits are delivered in order without per-packet addressing overhead.

3. **Circuit Teardown:** When either station finishes, it sends a teardown signal. Each switch releases its reserved links, and an acknowledgement confirms the release.

**Diagram (1 mark):**
*[Draw Source → Switch 1 → Switch 2 → Switch 3 → Destination, with labels indicating reserved links during the data transfer phase.]*

**Advantages (1 mark):**
- Guaranteed constant bandwidth throughout the session.
- No queuing delay at intermediate nodes; transmission delay is predictable.
- Suitable for real-time, delay-sensitive applications such as voice calls.

**Disadvantages (1 mark):**
- Inefficient for bursty traffic — reserved bandwidth is wasted during silent intervals.
- Long setup delay before actual data transfer begins.
- Failure of any intermediate switch disrupts the entire connection.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Explain the datagram packet switching technique. How does it differ from virtual circuit switching?**

**Model Answer:**

**Datagram Packet Switching (4 marks):**

In datagram packet switching, each packet (called a *datagram*) is treated as an **independent entity**. There is no call-setup phase. Each packet carries the **full destination address** in its header. At every intermediate router, the destination address is looked up in a routing table, and an independent forwarding decision is made based on the **current state of the network**. Consequently:

- Packets of the same message may take **different routes**.
- Packets may arrive at the destination **out of order**.
- The destination must use the **sequence number** in each packet's header to reassemble the message correctly.
- If a router or link fails, subsequent packets can simply be **re-routed around the failure**, making the network robust.

The classic example is the **Internet (IP)**.

**Comparison with Virtual Circuit Switching (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Datagram | Virtual Circuit |
|---|---|---|
| Setup phase | None | Required (logical path established) |
| Addressing in data packets | Full destination address in every packet | Short VCI label (swap at each hop) |
| Routing | Independent per packet | All packets follow same logical path |
| Ordering | Destination must re-sequence | Naturally preserved |
| Failure tolerance | High (auto-reroute) | Low (VC through failed node broken) |
| Header overhead | Higher | Lower |
| Example | IP, UDP | ATM, Frame Relay, X.25 |

---

### Question B-B — *[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]*  **(14 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**A message of 10,000 bits is to be sent from Source A to Destination D along the path A → B → C → D. Each link has a data rate of 1 Mbps. The propagation speed is $2 \times 10^8$ m/s and each link is 2000 km long. The message is broken into packets of 1000 bits each, with a header of 100 bits per packet. Calculate the total transmission time using (i) Circuit Switching, and (ii) Packet Switching. Assume setup time for circuit switching is 0.2 seconds and ignore teardown.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- $L = 10{,}000$ bits
- $k = 3$ links (A-B, B-C, C-D)
- $R = 1$ Mbps $= 10^6$ bps
- $d = 2000$ km $= 2 \times 10^6$ m per link
- $s = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s
- $P = 1000$ bits, $H = 100$ bits
- $t_{setup} = 0.2$ s

**Step 1 — Common quantities**

Propagation delay per link:
$$
t_{prop} = \dfrac{d}{s} = \dfrac{2 \times 10^6}{2 \times 10^8} = 0.01 \text{ s} = 10 \text{ ms}
$$

Number of packets:
$$
N = \dfrac{L}{P} = \dfrac{10{,}000}{1000} = 10 \text{ packets}
$$

On-wire packet size:
$$
S = P + H = 1000 + 100 = 1100 \text{ bits}
$$

Transmission delay per packet:
$$
t_{trans} = \dfrac{S}{R} = \dfrac{1100}{10^6} = 1.1 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 1.1 \text{ ms}
$$

**(i) Circuit Switching (4 marks):**

$$
t_{circuit} = t_{setup} + \dfrac{L}{R} + k \cdot t_{prop}
$$

$$
t_{circuit} = 0.2 + \dfrac{10{,}000}{10^6} + 3 \times 0.01
$$

$$
t_{circuit} = 0.2 + 0.01 + 0.03 = 0.24 \text{ s} = 240 \text{ ms}
$$

**(ii) Packet Switching (3 marks):**

Using the store-and-forward formula:
$$
t_{packet} = (N + k - 1) \cdot t_{trans} + k \cdot t_{prop}
$$

$$
t_{packet} = (10 + 3 - 1) \times 1.1 \times 10^{-3} + 3 \times 0.01
$$

$$
t_{packet} = 12 \times 0.0011 + 0.03 = 0.0132 + 0.03 = 0.0432 \text{ s} = 43.2 \text{ ms}
$$

**Conclusion:**
$$
\boxed{t_{circuit} = 240 \text{ ms}, \quad t_{packet} = 43.2 \text{ ms}}
$$

Packet switching is **5.56× faster** because the 200 ms setup overhead is bypassed and the pipelining effect accelerates transmission across 3 links.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Explain Virtual Circuit Switching with a suitable diagram. Compare it with Datagram Switching.**

**Model Answer:**

**Virtual Circuit Switching (4 marks):**

Virtual Circuit (VC) switching is a **hybrid technique** combining features of both circuit and packet switching:

1. **VC Setup Phase:** A logical path (the "virtual circuit") is established between source and destination through a signalling exchange. Each intermediate switch creates a **Virtual Circuit Identifier (VCI)** entry in its internal table, mapping the incoming VCI to the outgoing port and new VCI.

2. **Data Transfer Phase:** The message is divided into packets, each carrying only the **short VCI label** (not the full destination address). At every switch, the VCI is used as a lookup key, swapped to the outgoing VCI, and the packet is forwarded. All packets of the session traverse the same logical route, so **order is preserved**.

3. **VC Teardown Phase:** After transmission, the VCI entries are removed from all switches' tables.

**Diagram (1 mark):**
*[Draw S → Switch1 → Switch2 → Switch3 → D with VCI labels (e.g., 5, 12, 7, 3) on each link, plus a small VCI mapping table.]*

**Comparison with Datagram (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Virtual Circuit | Datagram |
|---|---|---|
| Connection | Connection-oriented | Connectionless |
| Header overhead | Low (short VCI) | High (full address) |
| Path | Same for all packets | May differ per packet |
| State in network | Per-VC state in switches | Stateless in core |
| Failure effect | VC must be re-established | Re-routing automatic |

**Examples:** ATM, Frame Relay (VC) vs. IP (Datagram).

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the header in transmission delay:** When computing $t_{trans}$ for packet switching, students frequently use only $P$ (the payload) instead of $P + H$. The header travels too! Loss: **up to 2 marks per sub-question**.
>
> 2. **Mixing up the $(N-1)$ vs $(N + k - 1)$:** The pipelining term is $(N-1) \cdot t_{trans}$ for the *first link only*. The full formula adds $k \cdot t_{trans}$ more for the remaining links. Get this wrong and you lose **2-3 marks**.
>
> 3. **Omitting propagation delay:** A surprisingly common error. Don't write the formula without the $k \cdot t_{prop}$ term.
>
> 4. **Confusing Virtual Circuit with Circuit Switching:** They are **different**. A VC is logical; a circuit is physical. Examiners love to ask *"Why is VC not the same as circuit switching?"* — answer: VC packets still share the physical link with other traffic; it is not a dedicated physical path.
>
> 5. **Missing "store-and-forward" in explanations:** Any answer about packet switching that fails to mention store-and-forward is **incomplete**. The phrase is a board-exam keyword.
>
> 6. **Units in numericals:** Always state units in intermediate steps (ms, s, bps). Examiners deduct marks for ambiguous units.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: Switching**

### Core Definitions
- ✅ **Circuit Switching** establishes a **dedicated physical path**; resources are reserved end-to-end.
- ✅ **Packet Switching** uses **store-and-forward** of independently-routed packets; resources are shared.
- ✅ **Datagram Service** = connectionless packet switching (e.g., IP).
- ✅ **Virtual Circuit Service** = connection-oriented packet switching using short VCIs (e.g., ATM, MPLS).

### The Three Phases of Circuit Switching
- ✅ **Setup → Data Transfer → Teardown.**
- ✅ Setup time is the primary overhead that penalizes short messages.

### The Three Phases of Virtual Circuit Switching
- ✅ **VC Setup → Data Transfer → VC Teardown** — same as circuit, but the path is logical, not physical.

### Critical Formulae to Memorize
- ✅ $N = L/P$
- ✅ $t_{trans} = (P+H)/R$
- ✅ $t_{prop} = d/s$
- ✅ **Packet Switching Delay:** $t_{packet} = (N + k - 1) \cdot t_{trans} + k \cdot t_{prop}$
- ✅ **Circuit Switching Delay:** $t_{circuit} = t_{setup} + L/R + k \cdot t_{prop} + t_{teardown}$
- ✅ **Header efficiency:** $\eta = P / (P + H)$

### Key Conceptual Differentiators
- ✅ Circuit = dedicated path, constant delay, no per-packet overhead, **poor for bursty traffic**.
- ✅ Packet = shared path, variable delay (jitter), header overhead, **good for bursty traffic**.
- ✅ VC = logical path with shared physical medium, **best of both worlds**.

### The Pipelining Insight (Most-Tested Concept)
- ✅ Because each router uses store-and-forward, packet $i+1$ can begin transmitting on link 1 **while** packet $i$ is being transmitted on link 2. This is why packet switching beats circuit switching for **short-to-medium messages**.

### Real-World Mapping
- ✅ **PSTN, ISDN** → Circuit Switching
- ✅ **Internet (IP)** → Datagram Packet Switching
- ✅ **ATM, Frame Relay, MPLS** → Virtual Circuit Packet Switching

### Common Pitfalls
- ❌ Don't equate VC with circuit switching.
- ❌ Don't forget header bits in $t_{trans}$.
- ❌ Don't omit propagation delay.
- ❌ Don't claim packet switching is *always* faster — for very large messages with no setup cost, the difference shrinks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Networks: Network Criteria, Physical Structures, Network Models, Categories of Networks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT522 - COMPUTER COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Networks: Network Criteria, Physical Structures, Network Models, Categories of Networks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Computer Network?

A **Computer Network** is a collection of two or more autonomous computing devices interconnected through a communication medium (wired or wireless) that allows them to share resources, exchange data, and coordinate actions using a standardized set of communication rules (protocols). In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PECPT522 – Computer Communication)**, a network is formally defined as a system of interconnected nodes (hosts, routers, switches, links) that uses both **hardware infrastructure** and **logical protocols** to deliver data from a source to one or more destinations.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The very first step in Computer Communication is to understand *what makes a network "good"*, *how it is physically wired*, *how its logic is layered*, and *what scales of network exist*. These four pillars form the conceptual base for every protocol (TCP/IP, Ethernet, Wi-Fi) studied in later modules.

## 1.2 The Four Sub-Topics at a Glance

| # | Sub-Topic | Core Question Answered |
|---|-----------|------------------------|
| 1 | Network Criteria | *What makes a network efficient, dependable, and safe?* |
| 2 | Physical Structures | *How are devices physically linked and what shape do they form?* |
| 3 | Network Models | *How is the communication logic layered so that hardware and software can work together?* |
| 4 | Categories of Networks | *How are networks classified based on geographic scale?* |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy – "The Postal System"

Imagine the entire Internet as a **national postal system**:

- **Network Criteria (Performance, Reliability, Security)** is like the postal department's commitment: *fast delivery (Performance), never losing your letter (Reliability), and sealed tamper-proof envelopes (Security)*.
- **Physical Structures (Topology)** is the *road map* of cities and highways connecting post offices (Mesh, Star, Bus, Ring).
- **Network Models (OSI / TCP-IP)** is the *operational rulebook* every postman, sorting hub, and delivery office follows, step by step, from picking up a letter to delivering it.
- **Categories of Networks (LAN, MAN, WAN)** is the *scale* of operation — a small local branch office (LAN), a regional state network (MAN), and the entire country-wide postal system (WAN).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A beginner often confuses **Topology (Physical Structure)** with **Network Model (Layered Architecture)**. Remember: **Topology = how cables/devices are physically arranged**. **Model = how communication tasks are logically divided into layers**. They are independent concepts.

## 1.4 Network Criteria – The Three Pillars

> [!NOTE]
> **Forouzan & Tanenbaum Standard Definition:** The three most fundamental metrics used to judge a network are **Performance**, **Reliability**, and **Security**.

### 1.4.1 Performance
Performance is measured by **Transit Time** (how long a message takes to travel from source to destination) and **Response Time** (the time between a request and the corresponding response). It depends on:
- Number of users
- Type of transmission medium
- Efficiency of hardware/software
- Latency and Throughput

### 1.4.2 Reliability
Reliability is measured by the **frequency of failure**, the **time it takes to recover** from a failure, and the **network's robustness** under catastrophic events. Higher reliability = more **uptime** = better **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)**.

### 1.4.3 Security
Security refers to the protection of data from **unauthorized access**, **unauthorized modification**, and ensuring **data authenticity**. It involves encryption, authentication, firewalls, and intrusion-detection mechanisms. A typical security model addresses **Data Safety**, **Privacy**, and **Integrity**.

## 1.5 Categories of Networks (Geographic Scale)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Networks are classified based on **geographical coverage** as PAN, LAN, CAN, MAN, WAN — each with distinct design rules, transmission technologies, and ownership models.

| Category | Full Form | Approximate Range | Typical Use |
|----------|-----------|-------------------|-------------|
| **PAN** | Personal Area Network | $\le 10$ **m** | Personal devices (Bluetooth) |
| **LAN** | Local Area Network | $\le 10$ **km** | Office, Lab, Building |
| **CAN** | Campus Area Network | $\le 100$ **km** | University campus |
| **MAN** | Metropolitan Area Network | $\le 100$ **km** | City, ISP backbone |
| **WAN** | Wide Area Network | $\gt 100$ **km** | Country, Continent, Globe |
| **SAN** | Storage Area Network | Variable | High-speed server storage |
| **WLAN** | Wireless LAN | $\le 100$ **m** | Wi-Fi hotspots |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Network Criteria – Expanded Theory

### 2.1.1 Performance Metrics in Detail
Performance is the most tangible criterion because users *feel* it directly. It is quantified using two principal metrics:
