- **Throughput** – The actual rate at which data is successfully delivered. It is measured in **bits per second (bps)**, **kilobits per second (kbps)**, **megabits per second (Mbps)**, or **gigabits per second (Gbps)**.
- **Latency (Delay)** – The total time taken for an entire message to travel from source to destination. Latency has four sub-components: **Transmission Delay**, **Propagation Delay**, **Processing Delay**, and **Queuing Delay**.

> [!NOTE]
> The **end-to-end delay** is the sum of all four delay components. KTU questions frequently ask to compute the *dominant* delay in a given scenario.

### 2.1.2 Reliability – The Three Sub-Metrics
1. **Accuracy of delivery** – Correct delivery to the intended destination.
2. **Fault tolerance** – Network continues operating despite link or node failure.
3. **Recovery time** – Time taken to restore service after a fault.

### 2.1.3 Security – The Three Goals
- **Confidentiality** – Only intended recipients can read the data.
- **Integrity** – Data is not altered in transit.
- **Authentication & Non-repudiation** – Sender's identity is verifiable.

## 2.2 Physical Structures – Topologies

### 2.2.1 Connection Types (Link Types)
- **Point-to-Point (P2P)**: A dedicated link connects exactly two devices. The entire capacity of the link is reserved for transmission between the two endpoints.
- **Multipoint (Multi-drop)**: Three or more devices share a single link. The channel capacity is shared either **spatially** or **temporally** (TDM/FDM).

### 2.2.2 Physical Topologies
The **physical topology** refers to the geometric arrangement of nodes and links.

| Topology | Description | Advantage | Disadvantage |
|----------|-------------|-----------|--------------|
| **Mesh** | Every node connects to every other node | Highest redundancy | $n(n-1)/2$ links expensive |
| **Star** | All nodes connect to a central hub/switch | Easy to install; single point of failure at hub | Hub failure brings down network |
| **Bus** | All nodes share a single backbone cable | Simple, cheap | Single cable failure cripples network |
| **Ring** | Each node connects to exactly two neighbours forming a closed loop | Equal access, no central server | One node failure breaks the ring |
| **Hybrid** | Combination of two or more topologies | Scalable and flexible | Complex design |

## 2.3 Network Models – Layered Architecture

### 2.3.1 The OSI 7-Layer Model
The **Open Systems Interconnection (OSI)** model, defined by the **International Organization for Standardization (ISO)**, is a 7-layer reference model that divides the communication process into discrete, manageable layers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU frequently tests the **mnemonic "Please Do Not Throw Sausage Pizza Away"** (PDNTSPA) — Physical, Data Link, Network, Transport, Session, Presentation, Application.

| # | Layer | Function | Data Unit (PDU) | Example |
|---|-------|----------|------------------|---------|
| 7 | Application | User interface, network services | Data | HTTP, FTP, SMTP, DNS |
| 6 | Presentation | Encryption, Compression, Encoding | Data | SSL, JPEG, ASCII |
| 5 | Session | Dialog control, synchronization | Data | NetBIOS, RPC |
| 4 | Transport | End-to-end delivery, flow & error control | Segment / Datagram | TCP, UDP |
| 3 | Network | Logical addressing, routing | Packet | IP, ICMP, OSPF |
| 2 | Data Link | Framing, MAC addressing, error detection | Frame | Ethernet, PPP, Wi-Fi (802.11) |
| 1 | Physical | Bit transmission over medium | Bit | Cables, Hubs, Connectors |

### 2.3.2 The TCP/IP (Internet) Model
The **TCP/IP model** (also called the **Internet Protocol Suite**) is the practical, real-world 4-layer model that powers the global Internet.

| Layer | Equivalent OSI Layers | Protocol Examples |
|-------|------------------------|-------------------|
| Application | 7, 6, 5 | HTTP, FTP, DNS, SMTP |
| Transport | 4 | TCP, UDP |
| Internet (Network) | 3 | IP, ICMP, ARP |
| Network Access (Link/Physical) | 2, 1 | Ethernet, Wi-Fi, PPP |

### 2.3.3 Layered Communication Concept
Each layer on the **sending host** adds its own header (encapsulation), and each layer on the **receiving host** removes the corresponding header (decapsulation). The **PDU (Protocol Data Unit)** at each layer has a different name — *Data, Segment, Packet, Frame, Bit*.

## 2.4 Categories of Networks – Ownership & Technology

Beyond geographic size, networks are also categorized by:
- **Ownership**: Private (Intranet) vs Public (Internet).
- **Switching technology**: Circuit-switched vs Packet-switched.
- **Transmission medium**: Wired (LAN) vs Wireless (WLAN).

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Description |
|---------|----------------|-------------|
| Number of links in a full Mesh with $n$ nodes | $L = \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$ | Used in Mesh topology link calculation |
| Number of ports per node in Mesh | $n - 1$ ports per node | Each node connects to all others |
| Maximum throughput (Theoretical) | $R_{\text{throughput}} \le C$ | $C$ = Channel capacity in **bps** |
| Total End-to-End Delay | $T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{trans}} + T_{\text{prop}} + T_{\text{proc}} + T_{\text{queue}}$ | All four sub-delays sum |
| Transmission Delay | $T_{\text{trans}} = \dfrac{\text{Frame Size (bits)}}{R \text{ (bps)}}$ | Time to push bits onto wire |
| Propagation Delay | $T_{\text{prop}} = \dfrac{\text{Distance (m)}}{\text{Propagation Speed (m/s)}}$ | Time for signal to travel |
| Maximum LAN range (10BASE-T) | $\le 100$ **m** | Common KTU constant |
| Maximum MAN range (typical) | $\le 100$ **km** | IEEE 802.6 standard |
| One-way speed of light in fibre | $\approx 2 \times 10^{8}$ **m/s** | Two-thirds of vacuum |
| One-way speed of light in copper | $\approx 2 \times 10^{8}$ **m/s** | KTU accepted value |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Number of Links in a Full Mesh Topology

> **Problem Statement:** Given a network with $n$ nodes connected in a full mesh topology, derive the formula for the total number of physical links.

### Step 1: Understand Full Mesh Property
In a **full mesh**, every node is directly connected to every other node. The graph is a *complete graph* $K_n$. This means for any two distinct nodes, there exists exactly one link between them.

### Step 2: Use Combinatorics (Combination Principle)
Choosing 2 distinct nodes from $n$ available nodes forms a unique link. The number of unordered pairs of nodes equals the number of edges in a complete graph.

$$L = \binom{n}{2} = \dfrac{n!}{2!(n-2)!}$$

### Step 3: Simplify the Expression
Expanding the factorial:

$$L = \dfrac{n \times (n-1) \times (n-2)!}{2 \times 1 \times (n-2)!}$$

The $(n-2)!$ terms cancel out:

$$L = \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$$

### Step 4: Verification with Small Cases
- If $n = 2$: $L = \dfrac{2 \times 1}{2} = 1$ link. ✓ (A single link connects two nodes.)
- If $n = 3$: $L = \dfrac{3 \times 2}{2} = 3$ links. ✓ (A triangle.)
- If $n = 4$: $L = \dfrac{4 \times 3}{2} = 6$ links. ✓ (A complete graph on 4 vertices.)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Rule:** Mesh link count is the *most asked* derivation in this module. Memorize the formula and the combination logic.

## 3.2 Derivation: Transmission Delay and Propagation Delay Relationship

> **Problem Statement:** A file of size $S$ bits is transmitted over a link of length $d$ meters and bandwidth $R$ bps. Derive the total time taken for the *first bit* to reach the receiver.

### Step 1: Identify the Two Delays
- The first delay is the time taken by the sender to push bits of the file onto the wire: **Transmission delay**.
- The second delay is the time for a bit to travel from sender to receiver across the physical medium: **Propagation delay**.

### Step 2: Express Transmission Delay
Time to push all bits of the file onto the medium:

$$T_{\text{trans}} = \dfrac{S \text{ (bits)}}{R \text{ (bps)}}$$

### Step 3: Express Propagation Delay
Time for a single bit to traverse the distance $d$ at speed $v$ (where $v$ is the signal propagation speed):

$$T_{\text{prop}} = \dfrac{d \text{ (m)}}{v \text{ (m/s)}}$$

### Step 4: Combine for First-Bit Arrival
The first bit must be transmitted (but only one bit worth of transmission time) *and then* propagate. However, in the most common KTU formulation, the *total time for the entire file to arrive* is:

$$T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{trans}} + T_{\text{prop}} = \dfrac{S}{R} + \dfrac{d}{v}$$

### Step 5: Example Numerical Evaluation
Suppose $S = 10{,}000$ bits, $R = 10^3$ bps, $d = 2000$ m, $v = 2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s.

$$T_{\text{trans}} = \dfrac{10{,}000}{10^3} = 10 \text{ s}$$

$$T_{\text{prop}} = \dfrac{2000}{2 \times 10^{8}} = 10^{-5} \text{ s} = 10 \; \mu\text{s}$$

$$T_{\text{total}} = 10 + 10^{-5} \approx 10 \text{ s}$$

This shows that for low-bandwidth, long-distance scenarios, **transmission delay dominates**.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix – Topology Comparison

| Criterion | Mesh | Star | Bus | Ring | Hybrid |
|-----------|------|------|-----|------|--------|
| **No. of links** | $n(n-1)/2$ | $n$ | $1$ | $n$ | Depends on sub-topology |
| **Cost** | Very High | Low | Very Low | Moderate | Moderate–High |
| **Reliability** | Excellent | Hub failure = fatal | Cable break = fatal | Node break = fatal | High |
| **Scalability** | Poor | Good | Poor | Moderate | Excellent |
| **Ease of troubleshooting** | Hard | Easy | Hard | Moderate | Moderate |
| **Best for** | Backbone (WAN) | Office LAN | Legacy Ethernet | Token Ring LAN | Enterprise campus |

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix – OSI vs TCP/IP Models

| Aspect | OSI Model | TCP/IP Model |
|--------|-----------|--------------|
| Number of layers | 7 | 4 (or 5 with Session) |
| Origin | ISO Standard | DARPA / Internet |
| Usage | Reference / teaching | Real-world implementation |
| Session & Presentation layers | Explicit | Merged into Application |
| Development | Theoretical first | Protocol-first |
| Adoption | Conceptual | Universal on Internet |

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix – Network Categories

| Network | Range | Speed (typical) | Protocol Examples | Ownership |
|---------|-------|------------------|--------------------|-----------|
| PAN | $\le 10$ **m** | $\le 1$ **Mbps** | Bluetooth, Infrared | Personal |
| LAN | $\le 10$ **km** | $10$–$10{,}000$ **Mbps** | Ethernet 802.3, Wi-Fi 802.11 | Private |
| CAN | $\le 100$ **km** | $\le 10{,}000$ **Mbps** | Ethernet, FDDI | University/Corporate |
| MAN | $\le 100$ **km** | $\le 100$ **Mbps** | DQDB, ATM, Metro-Ethernet | ISP / Telco |
| WAN | $\gt 100$ **km** | $\le 1$ **Gbps** | MPLS, Frame Relay, Satellite | Public/Private |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 OSI 7-Layer Model – Vertical Data Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    node7["LAYER 7: APPLICATION\nHTTP FTP DNS SMTP"]
    node6["LAYER 6: PRESENTATION\nSSL TLS JPEG ASCII"]
    node5["LAYER 5: SESSION\nNetBIOS RPC SIP"]
    node4["LAYER 4: TRANSPORT\nTCP UDP Segments"]
    node3["LAYER 3: NETWORK\nIP ICMP Packets"]
    node2["LAYER 2: DATA LINK\nEthernet PPP Frames"]
    node1["LAYER 1: PHYSICAL\nBits over Copper Fibre RF"]
    node7 --> node6
    node6 --> node5
    node5 --> node4
    node4 --> node3
    node3 --> node2
    node2 --> node1
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the diagram above, data flows **top-down on the sending host** (encapsulation, where each layer adds a header). On the receiving host, data flows **bottom-up** (decapsulation, where each layer removes its header). The **peer layers** (e.g., Transport ↔ Transport) communicate using logical, not physical, channels.

## 4.2 PDU Encapsulation – Frame Structure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    appData["APPLICATION DATA\nUser Message"]
    segBlock["SEGMENT\nHeader + Data\nTCP or UDP"]
    pktBlock["PACKET\nIP Header + Segment"]
    frmBlock["FRAME\nMAC Header + Packet + Trailer"]
    bitBlock["BITS\n0s and 1s on wire"]
    appData --> segBlock
    segBlock --> pktBlock
    pktBlock --> frmBlock
    frmBlock --> bitBlock
```

## 4.3 Physical Topologies – Block-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph mesh["MESH TOPOLOGY n=4"]
        m1((Node A)) <--> m2((Node B))
        m1 <--> m3((Node C))
        m1 <--> m4((Node D))
        m2 <--> m3
        m2 <--> m4
        m3 <--> m4
    end
    subgraph star["STAR TOPOLOGY"]
        s1((Node A)) --- hub((HUB SWITCH))
        s2((Node B)) --- hub
        s3((Node C)) --- hub
        s4((Node D)) --- hub
    end
    subgraph bus["BUS TOPOLOGY"]
        b1((Node A)) --- backbone["Backbone Cable"]
        b2((Node B)) --- backbone
        b3((Node C)) --- backbone
        b4((Node D)) --- backbone
    end
    subgraph ring["RING TOPOLOGY"]
        r1((Node A)) --- r2((Node B))
        r2 --- r3((Node C))
        r3 --- r4((Node D))
        r4 --- r1
    end
```

## 4.4 TCP/IP 4-Layer Model vs OSI 7-Layer

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph osi["OSI REFERENCE MODEL"]
        osi7["Application"]
        osi6["Presentation"]
        osi5["Session"]
        osi4["Transport"]
        osi3["Network"]
        osi2["Data Link"]
        osi1["Physical"]
        osi7 --> osi6 --> osi5 --> osi4 --> osi3 --> osi2 --> osi1
    end
    subgraph tcpip["TCP IP MODEL"]
        tcp4["Application Layer"]
        tcp3["Transport Layer"]
        tcp2["Internet Layer"]
        tcp1["Network Access Layer"]
        tcp4 --> tcp3 --> tcp2 --> tcp1
    end
    osi7 -.-> tcp4
    osi6 -.-> tcp4
    osi5 -.-> tcp4
    osi4 -.-> tcp3
    osi3 -.-> tcp2
    osi2 -.-> tcp1
    osi1 -.-> tcp1
```

## 4.5 Categories of Networks – Geographic Scale

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pan["PAN\nLess than 10 m\nBluetooth Body Network"]
    lan["LAN\nLess than 10 km\nOffice Lab Building"]
    can["CAN\nLess than 100 km\nUniversity Campus"]
    man["MAN\nLess than 100 km\nCity ISP Backbone"]
    wan["WAN\nGreater than 100 km\nCountry Continent Globe"]
    pan --> lan
    lan --> can
    can --> man
    man --> wan
```

## 4.6 Network Criteria – The Three Pillars

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    crit["NETWORK CRITERIA\nEvaluation of a Good Network"]
    perf["PERFORMANCE\nThroughput Delay Latency"]
    rel["RELIABILITY\nFault Tolerance MTBF Recovery"]
    sec["SECURITY\nConfidentiality Integrity Authentication"]
    crit --> perf
    crit --> rel
    crit --> sec
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the three primary criteria used to evaluate a computer network. *(Module 1, KTU 2024 Pattern)*
`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
The three primary criteria used to evaluate a computer network are:

1. **Performance** – Measured by *throughput* (successful bits delivered per second) and *delay / latency* (time taken for a message to travel end-to-end). Higher throughput and lower delay indicate better performance.
2. **Reliability** – Measured by *frequency of failure*, *time to recover*, and *robustness* under fault conditions. Networks with high *MTBF* (Mean Time Between Failures) and low *MTTR* (Mean Time To Repair) are highly reliable.
3. **Security** – Refers to protection against *unauthorized access*, *data modification*, and *impersonation*. Achieved through encryption, firewalls, authentication, and digital signatures.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** KTU awards 1 mark for each criterion's *definition* and 0.5 marks for the *metric/example* of each.

### Question 2: Differentiate between LAN and WAN. *(Module 1, KTU 2024 Pattern)*
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | LAN | WAN |
|---------|-----|-----|
| Geographic range | $\le 10$ **km** (Room, Building, Campus) | $\gt 100$ **km** (Country, Continent) |
| Ownership | Private (Single organization) | Public/Private (Telco/ISP) |
| Speed | High ($10$ **Mbps** – $10$ **Gbps**) | Moderate ($< 1$ **Gbps**) |
| Error rate | Very low | Higher |
| Maintenance | Easy | Difficult |
| Example | Office Ethernet, College Wi-Fi | Internet, MPLS, Satellite links |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each – Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks):**
*(a)* Explain the OSI 7-layer reference model in detail with the function of each layer, PDU name, and at least one protocol example per layer. *(7 Marks)*
*(b)* Describe the four main physical topologies – Mesh, Star, Bus, and Ring – with neat diagrams and discuss their relative advantages and disadvantages. *(7 Marks)*
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) – OSI 7-Layer Model (7 Marks)**

| Layer # | Layer Name | Function | PDU | Example Protocol |
|---------|------------|----------|-----|------------------|
| 7 | Application | Provides network services to user applications | Data | HTTP, FTP, SMTP, DNS |
| 6 | Presentation | Handles data translation, encryption, compression | Data | SSL/TLS, JPEG, ASCII |
| 5 | Session | Manages sessions between applications (dialog control) | Data | NetBIOS, RPC, SIP |
| 4 | Transport | Provides end-to-end connection, flow & error control | Segment (TCP), Datagram (UDP) | TCP, UDP |
| 3 | Network | Logical addressing and routing of packets | Packet | IP, ICMP, OSPF, BGP |
| 2 | Data Link | Framing, MAC addressing, error detection | Frame | Ethernet, Wi-Fi (802.11), PPP |
| 1 | Physical | Transmits raw bits over a physical medium | Bit | Cables, Connectors, Hubs |

**Peer-to-Peer Communication:** Each layer on the sending host communicates logically with its peer layer on the receiving host. The data is encapsulated at the sender (header added) and decapsulated at the receiver (header removed) as it moves through the layers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Pattern:** Award 0.5 marks per layer's function, 0.25 marks for the PDU name, and 0.25 marks for an example. Total = $7 \times 1 = 7$ marks.

**Part (b) – Physical Topologies (7 Marks)**

1. **Mesh Topology** – Every node connects to every other node. The number of links for $n$ nodes is $n(n-1)/2$. Used in WAN backbones and critical infrastructure. **Advantage:** Maximum reliability and redundancy. **Disadvantage:** Very expensive (high cabling cost).

2. **Star Topology** – All nodes connect to a central device (hub or switch). **Advantage:** Easy to install, add, and troubleshoot. One link failure affects only one node. **Disadvantage:** Central device is a single point of failure.

3. **Bus Topology** – All nodes share a single backbone cable terminated at both ends. **Advantage:** Simple and inexpensive for small networks. **Disadvantage:** Cable failure brings down the entire network; performance degrades with heavy traffic.

4. **Ring Topology** – Each node connects to exactly two neighbours, forming a closed loop. Data travels in one direction (or both in a dual ring). **Advantage:** Equal access for all nodes, no need for a central server. **Disadvantage:** Failure of one node can break the entire ring unless dual-ring or fault-recovery is used.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall – Common Mistake #1:** Students confuse **Physical Topology** with **Logical Topology**. Example: A 10BASE-T Ethernet wired in **star** (physical) still operates as a logical **bus** (CSMA/CD shared medium). Always clarify the *physical* vs *logical* distinction when the question is ambiguous.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall – Common Mistake #2:** When asked to compare topologies, students often write only advantages. KTU explicitly asks for **both advantages AND disadvantages** for full marks. Missing either side attracts a 1.5-mark penalty.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) – ALTERNATIVE CHOICE:**
*(a)* With a neat diagram, explain the TCP/IP 4-layer model. Compare it with the OSI 7-layer model. *(7 Marks)*
*(b)* What is a computer network? Explain the criteria used to evaluate a network's performance, reliability, and security. *(7 Marks)*
`[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) – TCP/IP 4-Layer Model & Comparison (7 Marks)**

The **TCP/IP (Internet Protocol Suite)** is a 4-layer practical model that forms the basis of the global Internet.

| TCP/IP Layer | Function | Equivalent OSI Layers | Example Protocol |
|--------------|----------|------------------------|------------------|
| Application | High-level protocols, end-user services | 7, 6, 5 (Application, Presentation, Session) | HTTP, FTP, DNS, SMTP, Telnet |
| Transport | End-to-end delivery, multiplexing | 4 (Transport) | TCP, UDP |
| Internet | Logical addressing, routing | 3 (Network) | IP, ICMP, ARP |
| Network Access / Link | Framing, physical transmission | 2, 1 (Data Link, Physical) | Ethernet, Wi-Fi, PPP, Frame Relay |

**Comparison Table (already in Section 3.4).** The key differences are:
- OSI is a 7-layer theoretical model; TCP/IP is a 4-layer practical model.
- OSI separates Session, Presentation, and Application clearly; TCP/IP combines them.
- TCP/IP protocols were developed *first*; OSI was developed later as a *reference model*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Pattern:** 3.5 marks for TCP/IP layers (0.5 per layer function + 0.5 per protocol example). 3.5 marks for the comparison (5 differences, 0.7 each).

**Part (b) – Network Criteria (7 Marks)**

**Definition:** A computer network is a collection of autonomous computing devices interconnected via communication media, exchanging data through standardized protocols.

**Three Evaluation Criteria:**

1. **Performance** – Evaluated by *throughput* and *delay*. Throughput is the bits-per-second successfully delivered. Delay has four components: transmission, propagation, processing, and queuing.

2. **Reliability** – Evaluated by *uptime*, *mean time between failures (MTBF)*, and *mean time to repair (MTTR)*. A reliable network has low failure rate and quick recovery. Redundant links and fault-tolerant protocols improve reliability.

3. **Security** – Evaluated by the strength of *confidentiality* (encryption), *integrity* (checksums, hashes), and *authentication* (digital certificates, passwords). Security failures can be passive (eavesdropping) or active (modification, impersonation).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall – Common Mistake #3:** Students often write generic answers for security like "*uses passwords*". For 7 marks, the examiner expects **three security goals** (CIA triad: Confidentiality, Integrity, Authentication) and at least **one mechanism** (e.g., AES for confidentiality, SHA-256 for integrity, RSA for authentication).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall – Common Mistake #4:** When asked "What is a computer network?", students sometimes skip the *autonomous* part. The word **autonomous** is critical — it rules out a master-slave configuration from being called a "network" in the formal KTU sense. Always include it in the definition.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Must-Memorize Before KTU Exam):**

- **Network Criteria = 3 Pillars** → Performance (Throughput, Delay), Reliability (MTBF, MTTR), Security (CIA Triad).
- **Connection Types** → Point-to-Point (dedicated) vs Multipoint (shared).
- **Five Physical Topologies** → Mesh, Star, Bus, Ring, Hybrid. Know link count, cost, reliability for each.
- **Mesh link count formula** → $L = n(n-1)/2$. Ports per node = $n-1$. **Must memorize.**
- **OSI 7 Layers (PDU names):** Application = **Data**, Presentation = **Data**, Session = **Data**, Transport = **Segment/Datagram**, Network = **Packet**, Data Link = **Frame**, Physical = **Bit**.
- **OSI Mnemonic (Top → Bottom):** **A**ll **P**eople **S**eem **T**o **N**eed **D**ata **P**rocessing → Application, Presentation, Session, Transport, Network, Data Link, Physical.
- **TCP/IP = 4 layers** → Application (7+6+5), Transport (4), Internet (3), Network Access (2+1).
- **Categories of Networks (geographic scale):** PAN (<10m) < LAN (<10km) < CAN (<100km) $\approx$ MAN (<100km) < WAN (>100km).
- **Transmission Delay formula:** $T = S/R$ where $S$ = bits, $R$ = bps.
- **Propagation Delay formula:** $T = d/v$ where $d$ = meters, $v \approx 2 \times 10^8$ **m/s** in copper/fibre.
- **Total Delay** = Transmission + Propagation + Processing + Queuing.
- **LAN typical speed:** $10$ **Mbps** to $10$ **Gbps**.
- **WAN typical speed:** up to $1$ **Gbps**.
- **Difference between physical and logical topology** — must clarify in any comparison answer.
- **The CIA Triad** for Security: **C**onfidentiality, **I**ntegrity, **A**uthentication.
- **PDU Encapsulation Order** (top to bottom): Data → Segment → Packet → Frame → Bit.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interconnection of Networks: Internetwork
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT522 - COMPUTER COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Interconnection of Networks: Internetwork

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Internetwork

An **Internetwork** (often shortened to *Internet*) is a collection of two or more distinct computer networks (which may use different protocols, technologies, and addressing schemes) interconnected through intermediate devices such as **routers** and **gateways** so that they function logically as a single, unified network capable of exchanging information transparently.

The formal term used in the KTU syllabus is **"Internetwork"** — the noun representing the *network of networks*. When prefixed with "the" and capitalized as **"the Internet"**, it specifically refers to the global, public, TCP/IP-based system of interconnected networks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PECPT522 - Module 1):**
> *Interconnection of Networks – Internetwork* refers to the mechanisms, devices, and protocols required to seamlessly bridge heterogeneous networks (LANs, MANs, WANs) into a unified communication system, enabling end-to-end data delivery between any two hosts regardless of their underlying network technology.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Highway System of Networks

Imagine each computer network as a **private city road system** (a LAN). Cities (networks) use their own road designs, speed limits, and address schemes. To travel from City A to City B, you cannot simply teleport; you must:

1. **Exit** City A's road network at a designated point.
2. **Enter a national highway** (the internetwork/internet backbone).
3. **Travel along the highway**, passing through **interchange hubs** (routers).
4. **Exit** at the correct highway junction for City B.
5. **Enter** City B's local road network and reach your destination.

The **routers** act exactly like highway interchanges — they receive a packet, look at its "destination address," and decide which "highway lane" (outgoing interface/next-hop) to forward it along. The key insight: **no single city needs to know how every other city is built internally** — only the highway rules and addresses must be standardized.

> [!NOTE]
> The genius of internetworking is **transparency**. A user in a Token-Ring LAN in Thiruvananthapuram can exchange emails with a user in a Wi-Fi LAN in New York without either user knowing — or caring — that the underlying physical media, framing, or MAC protocols differ.

### 1.3 Key Terms (KTU High-Yield Vocabulary)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Internetwork** | Generic term for any collection of interconnected networks |
| **Internet** | The global, public internetwork using the TCP/IP protocol suite |
| **Intranet** | A private internetwork accessible only within an organization |
| **Extranet** | A private internetwork accessible to selected external partners via the Internet |
| **Heterogeneous Network** | A network using different protocols, hardware, or addressing schemes |
| **Homogeneous Network** | A network using the same protocols and technologies throughout |
| **Tunneling** | Encapsulating one protocol's packets inside another for transit across an incompatible network |
| **Fragmentation** | Breaking a large packet into smaller pieces to traverse a network with a smaller MTU |

### 1.4 The Three Critical Engineering Questions

When two networks must be interconnected, exactly three engineering questions must be answered:

1. **What addressing scheme will be used across the combined network?** → Solved by a common network-layer protocol (typically **IP**).
2. **How will packets be forwarded between networks?** → Solved by **routing protocols** and **routing tables**.
3. **What happens when the underlying networks use incompatible frame formats, MTUs, or physical media?** → Solved by **encapsulation, fragmentation, and tunneling**.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for the Three Questions — A.R.E.:**
> **A**ddressing (IP), **R**outing (Forwarding), **E**ncapsulation/Translation.

# ⚙️ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference

## 2.1 Why Do We Need Internetworking? (The Core "Why")

The motivation for internetworking stems from **four fundamental limitations** of any single, isolated network:

1. **Scalability Limit** — A single LAN cannot host more than a few thousand devices due to broadcast storms, MAC table size, and address exhaustion.
2. **Geographic Limit** — Copper/fiber has a finite reach; LANs cover buildings, MANs cover cities, WANs cover countries.
3. **Technology Heterogeneity** — Industries and organizations have invested in Ethernet, Token Ring, ATM, Frame Relay, Wi-Fi, cellular, and satellite — all of which must interoperate.
4. **Administrative & Security Boundaries** — Organizations must control who can access their internal network, requiring logical separation combined with controlled interconnection.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Internetworking is **not** about replacing these technologies — it is about **bridging** them transparently so users experience one unified system.

## 2.2 The Layered Architectural Approach (How It Works)

Internetworking is achieved by designing a **common network-layer protocol** (IP) that sits *above* all the differing link-layer (Layer 2) technologies. The Open Systems Interconnection (OSI) and TCP/IP models make this possible through the **hourglass principle**.

### The Hourglass Model of Internetworking

```
        Application Layer (HTTP, FTP, SMTP, DNS)
                  ▲
                  │        ←  Many protocols above
                  │
            ┌─────┴─────┐
            │    IP      │   ←  ONE universal protocol
            └─────┬─────┘
                  │        ←  Many protocols below
                  ▼
    Link Layers (Ethernet, Wi-Fi, PPP, Frame Relay, ATM, SONET)
```

**Logical flow of an internetworked packet:**

1. Sender's application generates data → passed to **Transport layer (TCP/UDP)**.
2. Transport layer adds its header → **Network layer (IP)** adds the destination IP address.
3. IP hands the packet to the **Data Link layer** of the local network.
4. The local network frames and transmits the packet hop-by-hop.
5. At each **router**, the IP packet is extracted, the destination IP is examined, and a routing decision is made.
6. The IP packet is **re-encapsulated** into a new frame appropriate for the *next* link.
7. The process repeats until the packet reaches the destination network, where it is delivered to the destination host.

> [!NOTE]
> Notice that **only the IP header is preserved end-to-end**. The Layer-2 frame is **stripped and replaced at every router** — this is the essence of internetworking.

## 2.3 Devices Used in Internetworking

| Device | OSI Layer | Function in Internetwork | Typical Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Repeater** | Layer 1 (Physical) | Regenerates electrical signals; no protocol awareness | Extending cable length in a single LAN |
| **Bridge** | Layer 2 (Data Link) | Forwards frames based on MAC addresses; connects two LAN segments | Segmenting a LAN to reduce collision domain |
| **Switch** | Layer 2 (Data Link) | Multi-port bridge; creates separate collision domains per port | Modern LAN backbone |
| **Router** | Layer 3 (Network) | Forwards **packets** between different networks using IP addresses; maintains routing tables | **The core internetworking device** |
| **Gateway** | Layer 4-7 (Application) | Translates between **dissimilar protocols** (e.g., email gateways, VoIP gateways) | Bridging networks with completely different protocol stacks |
| **Brouter** | Layer 2/3 | Hybrid — bridges some traffic, routes other traffic | Legacy mixed environments |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Trap:** Students often confuse *Bridge* vs *Router*. Remember:
> - **Bridge** = same network, different segments, uses **MAC addresses** (Layer 2).
> - **Router** = **different networks**, uses **IP addresses** (Layer 3).
> - If a question asks "which device connects two *different* networks?" → answer is **ROUTER**.

## 2.4 Connectionless vs Connection-Oriented Internetworking

| Feature | Connectionless (Datagram) | Connection-Oriented (Virtual Circuit) |
|---|---|---|
| Path | Each packet routed independently | Fixed path established first |
| State in routers | None (stateless) | Virtual-circuit state per connection |
| Header overhead | Source + Destination address per packet | Small VC identifier per packet |
| Reliability | Provided by endpoints (TCP) | Often provided by network |
| Example protocols | **IP**, UDP | **ATM**, Frame Relay, X.25, MPLS |
| Failure recovery | Routers reroute around failure dynamically | All VCs on failed link must be re-established |
| Used in | **The Internet** | Legacy telecom, some modern MPLS backbones |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The modern Internet is fundamentally **connectionless at the network layer (IP)**, but **connection-oriented at the transport layer (TCP)**. This is a frequently tested KTU distinction.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula & Metric Reference Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Maximum packet size (MTU) | $D_{transit} = \min(MTU_{links})$ | Bytes |
| IP Header length (IPv4) | $20$ bytes minimum, up to $60$ bytes with options | Bytes |
| Effective payload per packet | $P_{eff} = MTU - H_{IP} - H_{L2}$ | Bytes |
| Fragment offset (IPv4) | $Offset_i = \dfrac{MF_i \cdot 8}{1}$ | Bytes; must be a multiple of $8$ |
| Total fragments from a packet of size $L$ | $n = \left\lceil \dfrac{L}{MTU - H_{IP}} \right\rceil$ | Integer count |
| Number of hops (path length) | $h = \vert P \vert - 1$ where $P$ is the path as a set of routers | Integer |
| End-to-end delay (approx.) | $D_{e2e} = \sum_{i=1}^{h} \left( d_{proc,i} + d_{queue,i} + d_{trans,i} + d_{prop,i} \right)$ | Seconds |

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU 14-mark questions on internetworking, the **MTU, fragmentation, and end-to-end delay formulas** are the most frequently tested. Master the table above.

# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Maximum Number of Fragments

**Problem Context:** A host needs to send a **single IP datagram of total size $L$** (including the IP header) across a network whose maximum transmission unit is $MTU$. The IP header consumes $H_{IP}$ bytes, leaving a **payload capacity per fragment** of $MTU - H_{IP}$ bytes — *except* for the last fragment, which can be smaller.

### Derivation — Step by Step

**Step 1 — Define the useful payload per fragment.**

The first fragment carries a full MTU minus the IP header. All intermediate fragments do the same. Only the last fragment may be smaller.

$$
P_{cap} = MTU - H_{IP}
$$

**Step 2 — Total payload bytes that must be transported.**

Since the original IP datagram has size $L$ including its header, the **total payload** (the data that must be fragmented) is:

$$
L_{payload} = L - H_{IP}
$$

**Step 3 — Number of full-capacity fragments required.**

The first $n-1$ fragments each carry exactly $P_{cap}$ bytes. The last fragment carries the remainder. So:

$$
L_{payload} = (n-1) \cdot P_{cap} + R, \quad \text{where} \quad 1 \le R \le P_{cap}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $n$.**

Since $R \le P_{cap}$, the maximum payload storable in $n-1$ full fragments is $(n-1) \cdot P_{cap}$. For $n-1$ fragments to be sufficient:

$$
(n-1) \cdot P_{cap} \ge L_{payload} - 1
$$

Rearranging:

$$
n - 1 \ge \frac{L_{payload} - 1}{P_{cap}}
$$

$$
n \ge 1 + \frac{L_{payload} - 1}{P_{cap}}
$$

Since $n$ must be an integer:

$$
\boxed{\,n = \left\lceil \frac{L_{payload}}{P_{cap}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{L - H_{IP}}{MTU - H_{IP}} \right\rceil\,}
$$

### Worked Numerical Example

**Given:** $L = 4000$ bytes, $H_{IP} = 20$ bytes, $MTU = 1500$ bytes.

**Step 1:** Compute payload capacity per fragment.
$$
P_{cap} = 1500 - 20 = 1480 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute total payload to fragment.
$$
L_{payload} = 4000 - 20 = 3980 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute number of fragments.
$$
n = \left\lceil \frac{3980}{1480} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 2.689 \right\rceil = 3 \text{ fragments}
$$

**Step 4 — Verify the fragment sizes.**

- Fragment 1: $20$ (header) $+ 1480$ (data) = $1500$ bytes. Fragment offset = $0$.
- Fragment 2: $20$ (header) $+ 1480$ (data) = $1500$ bytes. Fragment offset = $1480 / 8 = 185$.
- Fragment 3: $20$ (header) $+ (3980 - 2 \times 1480)$ (data) = $20 + 1020$ = $1040$ bytes. Fragment offset = $2960 / 8 = 370$.

**Step 5 — Verify reassembly.**

$$
1500 + 1500 + 1040 - 2 \times 20 = 3980 \text{ bytes} \quad \checkmark
$$

## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example: End-to-End Delay Across an Internetwork

**Problem:** A packet traverses **four links** between source and destination. The transmission rates, propagation delays, and per-hop processing/queueing delays are given below. Compute the **total end-to-end delay** assuming a single packet of size $L = 1500$ bytes and that each router introduces the stated $d_{proc} + d_{queue}$.

| Hop | Link rate $R_i$ | Propagation $d_{prop,i}$ | $d_{proc,i} + d_{queue,i}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 (Host → R1) | $1$ Mbps | $10$ ms | $2$ ms |
| 2 (R1 → R2) | $500$ Kbps | $20$ ms | $3$ ms |
| 3 (R2 → R3) | $2$ Mbps | $5$ ms | $1$ ms |
| 4 (R3 → Dest) | $1$ Mbps | $10$ ms | $0$ ms (destination) |

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute transmission delay for each hop.**

Transmission delay $d_{trans} = L / R_i$.

$$
d_{trans,1} = \frac{1500 \times 8 \text{ bits}}{1 \times 10^6 \text{ bps}} = 12 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
d_{trans,2} = \frac{12000 \text{ bits}}{500 \times 10^3 \text{ bps}} = 24 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
d_{trans,3} = \frac{12000 \text{ bits}}{2 \times 10^6 \text{ bps}} = 6 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
d_{trans,4} = \frac{12000 \text{ bits}}{1 \times 10^6 \text{ bps}} = 12 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 2 — Sum per-hop delay.**

For each hop $i$, the per-hop delay is:

$$
d_i = d_{trans,i} + d_{prop,i} + (d_{proc,i} + d_{queue,i})
$$

$$
d_1 = 12 + 10 + 2 = 24 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
d_2 = 24 + 20 + 3 = 47 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
d_3 = 6 + 5 + 1 = 12 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
d_4 = 12 + 10 + 0 = 22 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3 — Total end-to-end delay.**

$$
D_{e2e} = \sum_{i=1}^{4} d_i = 24 + 47 + 12 + 22 = 105 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,D_{e2e} = 105 \text{ ms}\,}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Bottleneck identification:** The slowest link (Hop 2 at 500 Kbps) contributes the **largest single delay (47 ms ≈ 45% of total)**. In real internetwork design, upgrading the bottleneck link has the highest ROI.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Interconnection Strategies

| Strategy | Underlying Mechanism | Packet Overhead | Network Awareness | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Bridge / Switch** | MAC address learning, frame forwarding | None (transparent) | Same Layer-2 network | LAN segmentation |
| **Router** | IP header inspection, routing table lookup | $20$ byte IP header per packet | Different Layer-3 networks | **Core internetworking** |
| **Tunneling (e.g., GRE, IPsec)** | Encapsulating one IP packet inside another | $20$–$58$ extra bytes | Hides inner packet's identity | VPNs, IPv4-over-IPv6 |
| **NAT (Network Address Translation)** | Rewriting IP/port headers at boundary | None added | Translates between private and public IP spaces | ISP edge, home routers |
| **Application Gateway** | Full protocol translation at Layer 7 | Variable | Highest level of awareness | Email gateways, HTTP proxies |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** The *transparency* of the interconnection increases as you move up the layers, but so does the **computational cost and latency**. Routers hit the sweet spot for general-purpose internetworking.

# 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Internetwork Topology (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    lanA["LAN A ETHERNET"]:::lan
    lanB["LAN B WIFI"]:::lan
    lanC["LAN C TOKEN RING"]:::lan
    wanA["WAN A FRAME RELAY"]:::wan
    wanB["WAN B ATM"]:::wan
    satLink["SATELLITE LINK"]:::wan
    router1["ROUTER R1"]:::router
    router2["ROUTER R2"]:::router
    router3["ROUTER R3"]:::router
    router4["ROUTER R4"]:::router
    router5["ROUTER R5"]:::router
    gw1["APPLICATION GATEWAY"]:::gateway
    hostA["HOST A"]:::host
    hostB["HOST B"]:::host
    hostC["HOST C"]:::host
    server1["WEB SERVER"]:::host

    hostA --- lanA
    hostB --- lanB
    hostC --- lanC
    server1 --- lanC

    lanA --- router1
    lanB --- router2
    lanC --- router3

    router1 --- wanA
    router2 --- wanB
    wanA --- router4
    wanB --- router4
    router4 --- satLink
    satLink --- router5
    router3 --- router5
    router5 --- gw1
    gw1 --- router2

    classDef lan fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,color:#0D47A1
    classDef wan fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,color:#BF360C
    classDef router fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#1B5E20
    classDef gateway fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#6A1B9A,color:#4A148C
    classDef host fill:#FAFAFA,stroke:#424242,color:#212121
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- **Three heterogeneous LANs** (Ethernet, Wi-Fi, Token Ring) attach to the internetwork via routers R1, R2, R3.
- **Routers R1, R2, R3** connect to **two distinct WANs** (Frame Relay and ATM) through **R4**.
- **R4 ↔ R5** link is a **satellite link** — high latency, low bit-error-rate resilience.
- **R5 ↔ R2** path goes through an **application gateway**, enabling protocol translation (e.g., email gateway between two incompatible mail systems).
- The single path from **Host A** (LAN A) to **Server** (LAN C) is: `Host A → R1 → R4 → R5 → R3 → LAN C → Server`. This path crosses **four different link-layer technologies**.

## 4.2 Functional Block Architecture of a Router (Internetwork Forwarding Engine)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    inPort["INPUT PORT 1"]:::port
    inPort2["INPUT PORT 2"]:::port
    inPort3["INPUT PORT 3"]:::port

    phys["PHYSICAL LAYER RECEIVER"]:::phys
    linkIn["DATA LINK LAYER DECAPSULATION"]:::link
    lookup["ROUTING TABLE LOOKUP ENGINE"]:::engine
    queue["INPUT QUEUE BUFFER"]:::buf
    switchFabric["HIGH SPEED SWITCH FABRIC"]:::switch
    queueOut["OUTPUT QUEUE BUFFER"]:::buf
    linkOut["DATA LINK LAYER ENCAPSULATION"]:::link
    physOut["PHYSICAL LAYER TRANSMITTER"]:::phys
    outPort["OUTPUT PORT 1"]:::port
    outPort2["OUTPUT PORT 2"]:::port
    outPort3["OUTPUT PORT 3"]:::port

    control["ROUTING PROCESSOR CPU"]:::cpu
    rtable["ROUTING TABLE IN RAM"]:::table
    rip["ROUTING PROTOCOL RIP OSPF BGP"]:::proto

    inPort --> phys --> linkIn --> lookup
    lookup --> queue
    queue --> switchFabric
    switchFabric --> queueOut
    queueOut --> linkOut --> physOut --> outPort
    switchFabric -.-> outPort2
    switchFabric -.-> outPort3

    control --- rtable
    control --- rip
    lookup -.-> rtable
    linkIn -.-> control

    classDef port fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#7F0000
    classDef phys fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#01579B,color:#01579B
    classDef link fill:#F1F8E9,stroke:#33691E,color:#33691E
    classDef engine fill:#FFF8E1,stroke:#F57F17,color:#E65100
    classDef buf fill:#ECEFF1,stroke:#37474F,color:#263238
    classDef switch fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#006064,color:#004D40
    classDef cpu fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#4A148C,color:#4A148C
    classDef table fill:#FFFDE7,stroke:#F9A825,color:#F57F17
    classDef proto fill:#E8EAF6,stroke:#1A237E,color:#1A237E
```

**Reading the block diagram:**

- The **input ports** terminate physical signals, decapsulate the link-layer frame, and extract the **IP packet**.
- The **lookup engine** consults the **routing table** (built and updated by the **routing processor** using protocols like OSPF, RIP, BGP) to decide the **output port**.
- The **switch fabric** (often a crossbar or shared-memory bus) transfers the packet from input to the correct output port at line speed.
- The **output port** re-encapsulates the IP packet in a **new link-layer frame** appropriate for the next hop, and transmits it physically.
- The **routing processor** (a CPU) runs the routing protocols to populate the routing table — this is the "intelligence" of the router.

## 4.3 Packet Encapsulation & Decapsulation Through an Internetwork

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    app["APPLICATION DATA"]:::app
    tcp["TCP HEADER PLUS APPLICATION DATA"]:::tcp
    ip["IP HEADER PLUS TCP SEGMENT PLUS DATA"]:::ip
    eth["ETHERNET HEADER PLUS IP PACKET PLUS ETH TRAILER"]:::eth
    ppp["PPP HEADER PLUS IP PACKET PLUS PPP FCS"]:::ppp
    wif["WIFI 802.11 HEADER PLUS IP PACKET PLUS WIFI FCS"]:::wif
    router["ROUTER STRIPS L2 ADDS NEW L2"]:::router
    ip2["IP PACKET UNCHANGED"]:::ip
    appH["APPLICATION DATA AT DESTINATION"]:::app

    app --> tcp --> ip --> eth
    eth --> router
    router --> ppp
    router -.-> wif
    ppp --> router
    router --> wif
    wif --> ip2
    ip2 --> appH

    classDef app fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#1B5E20
    classDef tcp fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#0D47A1
    classDef ip fill:#FFF8E1,stroke:#E65100
    classDef eth fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#4A148C
    classDef ppp fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#B71C1C
    classDef wif fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#006064
    classDef router fill:#FAFAFA,stroke:#212121,stroke-width:3px
```

**Key teaching point:** Notice how the **IP packet** (the purple/yellow box) travels *unchanged* across all three different link-layer technologies (Ethernet → PPP → Wi-Fi). The router only **strips the L2 header/trailer and adds a new one**. This is the practical embodiment of the **hourglass model**.

# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Internetwork. Differentiate between Internet, Intranet, and Extranet. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

An **Internetwork** is a collection of two or more distinct computer networks interconnected by routers and gateways such that they function as a single logical network for end-to-end data communication.

| Term | Definition | Accessibility |
|---|---|---|
| **Internet** | The global, public network of networks using the TCP/IP protocol suite | Worldwide, open to all |
| **Intranet** | A private internetwork confined to a single organization | Restricted to internal employees |
| **Extranet** | A controlled extension of an intranet, granting limited access to external partners | Restricted to authorized external users |

---

### Question 2: List any three functions of a Router in an internetwork. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The three primary functions of a router in an internetwork are:

1. **Path Determination** — Uses routing algorithms (e.g., OSPF, BGP) to build and maintain a routing table, computing the optimal path for each destination network.
2. **Packet Forwarding** — Receives an IP packet on one interface, consults the routing table, and forwards the packet out the appropriate outgoing interface after re-encapsulation at Layer 2.
3. **Network Isolation / Broadcast Domain Separation** — Unlike bridges/switches, routers do **not** forward link-layer broadcast frames, thereby isolating broadcast domains between networks.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU Format Reminder:** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks**, typically split as **7 + 7** in sub-parts. There is an **internal choice** — answer EITHER Choice A OR Choice B in full.

### ❓ Question Choice A (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Bridge vs Router, Connectionless vs Connection-Oriented Internetworking

**Q:** With neat diagrams, explain the difference between a **bridge** and a **router**. Also compare **connectionless** and **connection-oriented** internetworking with suitable examples. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Bridge vs Router (5 marks)**

| Parameter | Bridge | Router |
|---|---|---|
| OSI Layer | Layer 2 (Data Link) | Layer 3 (Network) |
| Addressing used | MAC address (48-bit) | IP address (32-bit / 128-bit) |
| Connects | Two segments of the **same** network | Two or more **different** networks |
| Forwards | Frames | Packets |
| Broadcast handling | Forwards broadcasts | Does not forward broadcasts (isolates broadcast domains) |
| Intelligence | Limited (MAC table learning) | High (routing algorithms, policies) |
| Speed | Faster (no IP processing) | Slightly slower (IP header inspection) |

**Diagram — Bridge connecting two LAN segments:**
```
[Hosts of LAN Segment 1]  ⇄  [BRIDGE]  ⇄  [Hosts of LAN Segment 2]
                              (Layer 2)
```

**Diagram — Router connecting two different networks:**
```
[Hosts of Network A]  ⇄  [ROUTER]  ⇄  [Hosts of Network B]
                       (Layer 3)
```

**(ii) Connectionless vs Connection-Oriented (2 marks)**

- **Connectionless (Datagram):** Each packet carries the full source and destination address and is routed independently. The Internet Protocol (IP) is the canonical example. No prior setup is required; routers are stateless about individual flows. Resilience to link failure is high because packets can be rerouted dynamically.

- **Connection-Oriented (Virtual Circuit):** A logical path (virtual circuit) is established before data transfer using a signaling phase (e.g., ATM, X.25, Frame Relay). Subsequent packets carry only a small VC identifier. Failure of any link along the VC disrupts all flows using it. Provides predictable QoS and in-order delivery at the network layer.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Tunneling in Internetworks

**Q:** What is **tunneling**? Explain with a diagram how it enables internetworking between two IPv6 networks across an IPv4 backbone. Mention any one tunneling protocol. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (2 marks):** Tunneling is an internetworking technique in which an entire packet of one protocol is **encapsulated as the payload** of another protocol, allowing it to traverse a network that would otherwise be unable to handle it. At the tunnel exit, the outer header is stripped and the original packet is forwarded normally.

**Working with IPv6-over-IPv4 Tunneling (4 marks):**

1. Host **H1** in **IPv6 Network A** generates an IPv6 packet destined for **H2** in **IPv6 Network B**.
2. The packet reaches the **ingress tunnel router (R1)** at the boundary of the IPv4 backbone.
3. R1 **encapsulates** the entire IPv6 packet inside a new **IPv4 packet** with:
   - Outer IPv4 header: source = R1's IPv4 address, destination = R2's IPv4 address, protocol = 41 (IPv6-in-IPv4).
   - Inner IPv6 packet: source = H1's IPv6 address, destination = H2's IPv6 address.
4. The IPv4 backbone routers forward the packet using the outer IPv4 header — they are **unaware** of the inner IPv6 packet.
5. At the **egress tunnel router (R2)**, the outer IPv4 header is removed and the original IPv6 packet is forwarded to **H2**.

**Diagram:**
```
[IPv6 Net A] H1 → R1 ─── IPv4 Backbone ─── R2 → H2 [IPv6 Net B]
              (Encapsulate)  (Pure IPv4 routing)   (Decapsulate)
   [IPv6 pkt] → [IPv4 hdr | IPv6 pkt] → ... → [IPv4 hdr | IPv6 pkt] → [IPv6 pkt]
```

**Tunneling Protocol Example (1 mark):** **6in4** (protocol number 41), **GRE** (Generic Routing Encapsulation, IP protocol 47), or **IPsec Tunnel Mode**.

---

### ❓ Question Choice B (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Need for Internetworking & Devices

**Q:** Explain the **need for internetworking** with reference to scalability, geographic reach, and technology heterogeneity. Describe the role of **repeaters, bridges, routers, and gateways** with a comparative table. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**Need for Internetworking (3 marks):**

1. **Scalability:** A single LAN can support only a few thousand devices before broadcast storms and MAC address table limitations degrade performance. Internetworking allows growth by adding more networks, with routers keeping broadcast domains separate.

2. **Geographic Reach:** Physical media have finite range (e.g., Ethernet UTP ≈ 100 m). Internetworking via WAN links (optical fiber, satellite, cellular) allows devices thousands of kilometers apart to communicate seamlessly.

3. **Technology Heterogeneity:** Organizations have invested in diverse technologies — Ethernet, Wi-Fi, Token Ring, ATM, Frame Relay, ISDN. Internetworking via a common network-layer protocol (IP) enables these heterogeneous systems to interoperate without requiring global technology migration.

**Comparative Table of Devices (4 marks):**

| Device | Layer | Connects | Addressing | Typical Function |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Repeater | 1 | Same network | None | Signal regeneration |
| Bridge | 2 | Two LAN segments | MAC | Frame forwarding, collision domain separation |
| Router | 3 | Different networks | IP | Packet routing, broadcast isolation |
| Gateway | 4–7 | Dissimilar protocol stacks | Multiple | Full protocol translation |

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Internetwork with Diagram and IP Packet Format

**Q:** Draw a neat diagram of an internetwork showing **three LANs and two WANs interconnected by routers**. Identify the **path** from a source host in LAN 1 to a destination server in LAN 3 and list the **devices and links** traversed. Briefly explain the **IPv4 header fields** that change vs remain unchanged along the path. `[KTU University Exam - May 2022]`

**Model Solution:**

**Internetwork Diagram (4 marks):**

```
         ┌──────────┐       ┌──────────┐       ┌──────────┐
         │   LAN 1  │       │   LAN 2  │       │   LAN 3  │
         │ Ethernet │       │   Wi-Fi  │       │ Token R. │
         └────┬─────┘       └────┬─────┘       └────┬─────┘
              │                  │                  │
           [H1]              [H3]               [Server]
              │                  │                  │
              │                  │                  │
         ┌────┴────┐        ┌────┴────┐        ┌────┴────┐
         │  R1     │        │  R2     │        │  R3     │
         └────┬────┘        └────┬────┘        └────┬────┘
              │                  │                  │
         ╔════╧══════════════════╧══════════════════╧════╗
         ║              WAN 1 (Frame Relay)              ║
         ╚════════════════════╤══════════════════════════╝
                              │
                         ┌────┴────┐
                         │   R4    │
                         └────┬────┘
                              │
         ╔════════════════════╧══════════════════════════╗
         ║              WAN 2 (SONET/Optical)            ║
         ╚════════════════════╤══════════════════════════╝
                              │
                         ┌────┴────┐
                         │   R5    │
                         └────┬────┘
                              │
                            [ISP Cloud]
```

**Identified Path (2 marks):**
**H1 → LAN 1 (Ethernet) → R1 → WAN 1 (Frame Relay) → R4 → WAN 2 (SONET) → R5 → ISP Cloud → R3 → LAN 3 (Token Ring) → Server.**

The packet traverses **3 LAN links + 4 router-to-router WAN links + 1 LAN link** = **8 links** in total, crossing **4 different link-layer technologies**.

**IPv4 Header Fields — Change vs Remain Unchanged (1 mark):**

- **Unchanged end-to-end:** Source IP Address, Destination IP Address, Identification, Protocol, Options.
- **Changed at every hop:** Source MAC, Destination MAC (in the inner Ethernet/Wi-Fi/Token Ring header), TTL (decremented by 1 at each router), Header Checksum (recomputed).
- **Changed at NAT boundaries:** Source/Destination IP and Port (if NAT is applied).

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes KTU examiners penalize heavily:**
> 1. **Confusing Bridge with Router** (loss of 2 marks per occurrence). A bridge is **Layer 2**, operates on **MAC addresses**, and connects segments of the **same** network. A router is **Layer 3**, operates on **IP addresses**, and connects **different** networks.
> 2. **Writing "Internet" when the question asks for "Internetwork"** (loss of 1–2 marks). *The Internet is a specific instance; Internetwork is the generic concept.*
> 3. **Skipping the diagram** in "Explain with a neat diagram" questions. A clear labeled diagram is worth **2–3 marks** by itself.
> 4. **Forgetting to label the path** in topology questions. Examiners want explicit identification of every router and link traversed.
> 5. **MTU/Fragmentation errors** — students often forget to subtract the IP header (20 bytes) before dividing by MTU. Always state $P_{cap} = MTU - H_{IP}$.
> 6. **Connection-oriented vs Connection-less confusion** — students often claim "the Internet is connection-oriented" because they use TCP. The correct statement is: **The Internet is connection-less at the network layer (IP) and connection-oriented at the transport layer (TCP).**

---

## 5.4 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Internetwork = network of networks**; **The Internet** = the global instance using **TCP/IP**.
- ✅ The three core problems solved by internetworking: **Addressing, Routing, Encapsulation/Translation** (mnemonic: **A.R.E.**).
- ✅ The **Hourglass Model** centers on a single universal protocol (**IP**) supported by many link layers and serving many applications.
- ✅ **Bridge = Layer 2, MAC, same network**; **Router = Layer 3, IP, different networks**; **Gateway = Layer 4–7, full protocol translation**.
- ✅ Routers **strip and re-encapsulate** the Layer-2 frame at every hop, but the **IP packet header (with source/destination IP) remains unchanged** end-to-end (except for **TTL decrement** and **header checksum recomputation**).
- ✅ Routers **isolate broadcast domains**; switches/bridges isolate **collision domains** but not broadcast domains.
- ✅ **MTU-based fragmentation formula:** $n = \lceil (L - H_{IP}) / (MTU - H_{IP}) \rceil$.
- ✅ **Fragment offset** in IPv4 **must be a multiple of 8** (since the field is 13 bits and represents bytes divided by 8).
- ✅ The Internet is **connectionless at Layer 3 (IP)** but **connection-oriented at Layer 4 (TCP)** — a high-yield KTU distinction.
- ✅ **Tunneling** = encapsulating one protocol's packet inside another to traverse an incompatible network; examples: **6in4, GRE, IPsec Tunnel Mode**.
- ✅ **End-to-end delay** $D_{e2e} = \sum (d_{trans,i} + d_{prop,i} + d_{proc,i} + d_{queue,i})$ across all $i = 1$ to $h$ hops.
- ✅ **NAT** (Network Address Translation) rewrites IP/port headers at the boundary between private and public address spaces — common in home/enterprise routers.
- ✅ **Repeater (L1) < Bridge/Switch (L2) < Router (L3) < Gateway (L4–7)** — increasing capability and processing cost.

---

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Topic: Interconnection of Networks: Internetwork**
> *Aligned to KTU 2024 Scheme • PECPT522 • Computer Communication*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Network Models: Layered Tasks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT522 - COMPUTER COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Network Models: Layered Tasks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Network Model** is a structured, logical architecture that decomposes the complex process of data communication into a hierarchy of discrete, manageable **layers**. Each layer performs a specific set of **layered tasks**, providing well-defined services to the layer directly above it while consuming services from the layer directly below it. This modular decomposition is formalized in reference models such as the **OSI (Open Systems Interconnection) Model** and the **TCP/IP Protocol Suite**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core KTU Definition:** A layered task refers to the breakdown of the communication process into smaller, self-contained sub-problems. Each layer handles ONE responsibility (e.g., routing, error detection, addressing) and communicates with its peer layer on the remote machine through a defined **Protocol**.

### 1.2 Key Terminology (KTU Syllabus Glossary)

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Service** | A set of capabilities a lower layer offers to the upper layer (the *what*). |
| **Protocol** | A set of rules governing peer-to-peer communication between the same layers on different machines (the *how*). |
| **Interface** | The boundary defining HOW the upper layer accesses the services of the lower layer. |
| **Service Access Point (SAP)** | The logical channel/port through which a layer requests services from the adjacent layer. |
| **PDU (Protocol Data Unit)** | The formatted block of data exchanged between peer layers. |
| **Encapsulation** | The process of a layer appending its own header (and sometimes trailer) to the SDU received from the upper layer. |
| **De-encapsulation** | The reverse process on the receiving side where each layer strips its corresponding header. |

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The International Postal System Analogy**
> Imagine two friends, Arjun in India and Kevin in Germany, exchanging a letter:
> 1. **Application Layer (Writing the letter):** Arjun writes the actual message content (the "data"). This is the user's intent.
> 2. **Transport Layer (Putting it in an envelope):** He puts the letter in an envelope and writes Kevin's name (logical addressing / port number).
> 3. **Network Layer (Global addressing):** The postal service stamps the *destination country and city* (IP address) on the envelope.
> 4. **Data Link Layer (Local delivery routing):** The local post office decides which truck, lane, and route the envelope takes within the city (MAC address / hop).
> 5. **Physical Layer (The truck itself):** The physical medium (roads, trucks, wheels) actually carries the envelope.
>
> **The beauty of layering:** The truck driver (Physical) does NOT need to know English. The local postman (Data Link) does NOT need to know German. Each layer is *independent* and only worries about its own job.

> [!TIP]
> **Why do we need Layered Tasks?**
> - **Modularity:** Each layer can be designed/replaced independently (e.g., swapping Wi-Fi for Ethernet without changing the application).
> - **Abstraction:** Complex internal mechanics are hidden from the upper layer.
> - **Reusability:** A single layer design supports multiple applications.
> - **Standardization:** Vendors worldwide can interoperate (a Windows PC talks to a Linux server).
> - **Easier Troubleshooting:** A network engineer can isolate faults to a specific layer.
> - **Parallel Development:** Different teams can work on different layers simultaneously.
> - **Technology Independence:** Layer n+1 is unaffected by changes in layer n.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

### 2.1 The OSI Reference Model (7 Layers)

The **OSI Model** is a theoretical, vendor-neutral conceptual framework defined by the **ISO (International Organization for Standardization)** in **ISO/IEC 7498-1**. It divides communication into **7 hierarchical layers**.

#### 2.1.1 Layer Breakdown (Top-Down: Application to Physical)

**Layer 7 — Application Layer**
- Closest to the end user.
- Provides network services to user applications (HTTP, FTP, SMTP, DNS, SSH, Telnet).
- Data unit: **Message / Data**.

**Layer 6 — Presentation Layer**
- Translates data between application and network formats.
- Handles **encryption/decryption** (SSL/TLS), **compression**, and **data formatting** (ASCII, EBCDIC, JPEG, MPEG).
- Data unit: **Message / Data**.

**Layer 5 — Session Layer**
- Manages and controls connections (sessions) between two communicating hosts.
- Handles **dialog control** (half-duplex/full-duplex), **synchronization** (checkpoints), and **session recovery**.
- Protocols: NetBIOS, RPC, PPTP.

**Layer 4 — Transport Layer**
- Ensures end-to-end communication, reliability, and segmentation.
- Two main protocols: **TCP (Transmission Control Protocol)** — connection-oriented, reliable, and **UDP (User Datagram Protocol)** — connectionless, best-effort.
- Performs **port addressing** (16-bit port numbers, range **0 to 65535**), **flow control**, **error control**, **sequencing**.
- Data unit: **Segment** (TCP) or **Datagram** (UDP).

**Layer 3 — Network Layer**
- Provides **logical addressing** (IP addresses — **32 bits in IPv4, 128 bits in IPv6**) and **routing** of packets across multiple networks.
- Routers operate at this layer.
- Data unit: **Packet / Datagram**.

**Layer 2 — Data Link Layer**
- Provides **node-to-node** data transfer on the same network segment.
- Uses **MAC (Media Access Control) addresses** — 48 bits hardcoded in NICs.
- Sub-divided into:
  - **LLC (Logical Link Control)** sublayer — upper half, interfaces with Network Layer.
  - **MAC sublayer** — lower half, controls access to the shared medium.
- Performs **framing**, **error detection** (CRC), and **collision handling** (CSMA/CD).
- Data unit: **Frame**.
- Devices: **Switches, Bridges, NICs**.

**Layer 1 — Physical Layer**
- Transmits raw **bits** as electrical signals, light pulses, or radio waves over a physical medium.
- Defines **voltage levels**, **connector types** (RJ45, LC), **topology** (star, bus, mesh), and **bit rate (bps)**.
- Data unit: **Bits**.
- Devices: **Hubs, Repeaters, Cables, Fiber**.

#### 2.1.2 Mnemonic to Remember OSI Layers (Top-Down)
> **A**ll **P**eople **S**eem **T**o **N**eed **D**ata **P**rocessing
> *(Application, Presentation, Session, Transport, Network, Data Link, Physical)*

### 2.2 The TCP/IP Reference Model (4 Layers)

Developed by the **DARPA (Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency)** in the 1970s, also known as the **Internet Protocol Suite**. It is the practical, real-world model that powers the internet.

**Layer 4 — Application Layer**
- Combines OSI's Application + Presentation + Session layers.
- Protocols: HTTP, HTTPS, FTP, SMTP, DNS, Telnet, SNMP.

**Layer 3 — Transport Layer**
- Equivalent to OSI Transport Layer.
- Protocols: **TCP, UDP**.

**Layer 2 — Internet Layer**
- Equivalent to OSI Network Layer.
- Protocols: **IP, ICMP, ARP, RARP, IGMP**.

**Layer 1 — Network Access / Link Layer**
- Combines OSI's Data Link + Physical layers.
- Protocols: Ethernet, Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11), PPP, Frame Relay.

### 2.3 KTU Comparison Cheat Sheet: OSI vs TCP/IP

| Feature | OSI Model | TCP/IP Model |
|---|---|---|
| Layers | **7** | **4** |
| Origin | ISO (Theoretical) | DARPA (Practical) |
| Development | ISO Standardized | Internet Community |
| Usage | Reference teaching model | Practical internet implementation |
| Strict Layer Boundaries | Yes, very strict | Loose boundaries |
| Session & Presentation | Separate layers | Merged into Application |
| Physical + Data Link | Separate | Combined as Network Access |
| Protocol Dependency | Protocol-independent | Built around TCP, IP, UDP |
| Adopted By | Academia, Europe | Global Internet |

### 2.4 Protocol Data Units (PDU) — KTU High-Yield Table

| Layer | OSI PDU Name | TCP/IP PDU Name |
|---|---|---|
| Application / Transport | Data / Message | Data / Stream |
| Transport (TCP/UDP) | Segment / Datagram | Segment / Datagram |
| Network | Packet | Datagram |
| Data Link | Frame | Frame |
| Physical | Bits | Bits |

### 2.5 The Encapsulation Process (Most Repeated KTU Question)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Encapsulation** is the process where each layer adds its own control information (header, and sometimes trailer) to the data received from the layer above. This is analogous to **packing a box within a box within a box**.

**Flow (Sender Side - Top Down):**
1. **Application Layer** generates the user **DATA** (e.g., an email body).
2. **Transport Layer** adds a **TCP/UDP Header** (source port, dest port, seq no.) → becomes a **SEGMENT**.
3. **Network Layer** adds an **IP Header** (source IP, dest IP, TTL) → becomes a **PACKET**.
4. **Data Link Layer** adds a **MAC Header AND Trailer** (CRC for error detection) → becomes a **FRAME**.
5. **Physical Layer** converts the frame into **BITS** and transmits as electrical/optical signals.

On the receiver side, the reverse process is called **De-encapsulation** (Bottom-Up), where each layer strips its corresponding header and passes the payload up.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthroughs & Process Derivations

### 3.1 Analytical Process: End-to-End Data Transmission Walkthrough

Let us trace a single **HTTP GET request** from Host A (192.168.1.10) to Host B (93.184.216.34, example.com) through every layer.

**Given:**
- Source IP: $192.168.1.10$
- Destination IP: $93.184.216.34$
- Source MAC: $AA:AA:AA:AA:AA:AA$
- Destination MAC (next hop router): $BB:BB:BB:BB:BB:BB$
- Source Port: $50000$ (ephemeral)
- Destination Port: $80$ (HTTP)
- Data payload: $D$ bytes of HTTP request

**Step 1 — Application Layer (No header added)**
The browser generates HTTP request bytes, denoted simply as $D$.

$$ PDU_{App} = D $$

**Step 2 — Transport Layer (TCP Header added)**
TCP adds a 20-byte minimum header containing Source Port, Destination Port, Sequence Number, Acknowledgment Number, Flags, Window Size, and Checksum.

$$ PDU_{Transport} = H_{TCP} + D $$

**Step 3 — Network Layer (IP Header added)**
IP adds a 20-byte (IPv4) minimum header containing Source IP, Destination IP, TTL, Protocol, and Header Checksum.

$$ PDU_{Network} = H_{IP} + H_{TCP} + D $$

This entire entity is now called a **Packet** or **IP Datagram**.

**Step 4 — Data Link Layer (Ethernet Frame constructed)**
The Data Link layer adds a 14-byte Ethernet Header (Source MAC, Destination MAC, EtherType) AND a 4-byte Ethernet Trailer (FCS / CRC-32).

$$ PDU_{DataLink} = H_{ETH} + H_{IP} + H_{TCP} + D + T_{ETH} $$

**Step 5 — Physical Layer (Bit Stream Transmission)**
The frame is encoded onto the physical medium. If the frame is $N$ bytes, the number of bits transmitted is:

$$ N_{bits} = N_{bytes} \times 8 $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Observation for KTU Exams:** Notice that the MAC addresses change at every hop (router), but the IP addresses (Source and Destination) remain **unchanged** end-to-end. This is a classic 14-mark question.

### 3.2 The Layer Service Provision Formula

The OSI architecture strictly follows the **n-layer service model**. If layer $n$ provides service to layer $n+1$, the relationship can be expressed as:

$$ S_{n+1} = f(S_n, P_n) $$

Where:
- $S_{n+1}$ = Service provided to upper layer
- $S_n$ = Service inherited from lower layer
- $P_n$ = Protocol operations of layer $n$
- $f$ = Layer $n$'s internal logic

This means: *The service a layer offers is a function of the service it received from below, enhanced by its own protocol operations.*

### 3.3 Connection-Oriented vs Connectionless Services (KTU Favourite 14-Mark Topic)

#### 3.3.1 Connection-Oriented Service (e.g., TCP)

Operates in **three phases**:

1. **Connection Establishment (Handshake)**
   Uses the famous **TCP 3-Way Handshake**:
   - Client → Server: $\text{SYN} = 1, \text{seq} = x$
   - Server → Client: $\text{SYN} = 1, \text{ACK} = 1, \text{seq} = y, \text{ack} = x+1$
   - Client → Server: $\text{ACK} = 1, \text{seq} = x+1, \text{ack} = y+1$

2. **Data Transfer**
   Packets are sequenced, acknowledged, and retransmitted if lost.

3. **Connection Termination (4-Way Teardown)**
   Uses FIN/ACK flags.

**Properties:** Reliable, ordered, flow-controlled, congestion-controlled. Slower due to overhead.

#### 3.3.2 Connectionless Service (e.g., UDP)

- **No handshake**, no connection state.
- Sender simply transmits each datagram independently.
- Receiver does not acknowledge.
- Packets may arrive out-of-order, duplicated, or lost.

**Properties:** Fast, low overhead, unreliable, no ordering. Used for DNS, video streaming, VoIP, online gaming.

#### 3.3.3 Comparison Table

| Feature | Connection-Oriented | Connectionless |
|---|---|---|
| Phases | 3 (Setup, Data, Teardown) | 1 (Send immediately) |
| Reliability | High | Best-effort |
| Ordering | Maintained | Not guaranteed |
| Overhead | High | Low |
| Example | TCP, ATM | UDP, IP, SMTP |
| Speed | Slower | Faster |

### 3.4 SAP and PDU Logical Mapping

At every layer, the data flow follows a strict structure:

$$ \text{PDU}_n = \text{Header}_n + \text{SDU}_{n+1} $$

Where $SDU_{n+1}$ is the **Service Data Unit** received from the upper layer $n+1$, and $PDU_n$ is the **Protocol Data Unit** passed to the lower layer $n$.

The **SAP (Service Access Point)** is the logical interface between layer $n$ and layer $n+1$. For example:
- **Transport SAP** is identified by the **Port Number**.
- **Network SAP** is identified by the **IP Address**.
- **Data Link SAP** is identified by the **MAC Address**.

This three-address tuple (MAC, IP, Port) uniquely identifies a process running on a specific host across the network.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Schematic: The 7-Layer OSI Model (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TD
    L7["LAYER 7 APPLICATION - HTTP FTP SMTP DNS"]
    L6["LAYER 6 PRESENTATION - SSL TLS JPEG MPEG ENCRYPTION"]
    L5["LAYER 5 SESSION - NETBIOS RPC DIALOG CONTROL"]
    L4["LAYER 4 TRANSPORT - TCP UDP PORT NUMBERING SEGMENTATION"]
    L3["LAYER 3 NETWORK - IP ICMP ROUTING LOGICAL ADDRESS"]
    L2["LAYER 2 DATA LINK - MAC ETHERNET PPP FRAMING CRC"]
    L1["LAYER 1 PHYSICAL - BITS VOLTAGE CABLE HUB REPEATER"]
    L7 --> L6
    L6 --> L5
    L5 --> L4
    L4 --> L3
    L3 --> L2
    L2 --> L1
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Schematic: TCP/IP 4-Layer Model Mapping to OSI

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["TCP/IP APPLICATION LAYER"]
    B["TCP/IP TRANSPORT LAYER - TCP UDP"]
    C["TCP/IP INTERNET LAYER - IP ICMP ARP"]
    D["TCP/IP NETWORK ACCESS LAYER - ETHERNET WIFI"]
    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    A -.maps to.-> M1["OSI 7 APPLICATION"]
    A -.maps to.-> M2["OSI 6 PRESENTATION"]
    A -.maps to.-> M3["OSI 5 SESSION"]
    B -.maps to.-> M4["OSI 4 TRANSPORT"]
    C -.maps to.-> M5["OSI 3 NETWORK"]
    D -.maps to.-> M6["OSI 2 DATA LINK"]
    D -.maps to.-> M7["OSI 1 PHYSICAL"]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Schematic: Encapsulation Process (Sender Side)

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1["USER DATA - HTTP REQUEST"]
    S2["SEGMENT - TCP HEADER PLUS DATA"]
    S3["PACKET - IP HEADER PLUS SEGMENT"]
    S4["FRAME - MAC HEADER PLUS PACKET PLUS CRC TRAILER"]
    S5["BITS - ELECTRICAL OR OPTICAL SIGNALS ON WIRE"]
    S1 --> S2
    S2 --> S3
    S3 --> S4
    S4 --> S5
    S5 --> S6["PHYSICAL MEDIUM - COPPER FIBER WIRELESS"]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Schematic: De-encapsulation Process (Receiver Side)

```mermaid
graph TD
    R1["BITS RECEIVED FROM MEDIUM"]
    R2["FRAME - CRC CHECK MAC STRIP"]
    R3["PACKET - IP HEADER STRIP ROUTING CHECK"]
    R4["SEGMENT - TCP HEADER STRIP REASSEMBLY ACK"]
    R5["DATA - PRESENTED TO APPLICATION LAYER"]
    R1 --> R2
    R2 --> R3
    R3 --> R4
    R4 --> R5
```

### 4.5 Mermaid Schematic: Service vs Protocol Distinction

```mermaid
graph LR
    HostA["HOST A LAYER N"]
    HostB["HOST B LAYER N"]
    HostA -- "PROTOCOL COMMUNICATION PEER TO PEER" --> HostB
    LA["LAYER N PLUS 1 ON HOST A"]
    HostA -- "SERVICE ACCESS POINT SAP INTERFACE" --> LA
```

> [!TIP]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** Horizontal arrows between the same layer on different machines = **Protocol**. Vertical arrows between adjacent layers on the same machine = **Service Interface (SAP)**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define the term "Network Model." List any two advantages of using a layered network architecture.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A Network Model is a logical framework that divides the complex data communication process into multiple hierarchical layers, each performing specific tasks and communicating with peer layers through well-defined protocols. (2 marks)

*Advantages:* (1 mark for any two, 0.5 each)
1. Modularity and ease of design.
2. Vendor interoperability.
3. Simplified troubleshooting.
4. Technology independence — a layer can be upgraded without affecting others.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Differentiate between Connection-Oriented and Connectionless services with one example each.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Connection-Oriented | Connectionless |
|---|---|---|
| Setup | Requires handshake (e.g., TCP 3-way) | No setup, immediate send |
| Reliability | Reliable, ACK-based | Unreliable, no ACK |
| Example | TCP | UDP / IP |

(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for difference, 1 mark for examples)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the OSI Reference Model in detail. List the functions of each of the 7 layers. (7 Marks)

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the process of data encapsulation and de-encapsulation as data travels from the Application layer of the sender to the Application layer of the receiver. (7 Marks)

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) — 7 Marks**

The OSI (Open Systems Interconnection) model was developed by **ISO** to provide a universal, vendor-neutral framework for network communication. It has 7 layers:

1. **Application Layer (L7):** Provides network services to end-user applications like browsers and email clients. Protocols: HTTP, FTP, SMTP. PDU: Message.

2. **Presentation Layer (L6):** Handles data translation, encryption/decryption, and compression. Ensures data is in a usable format. Example: SSL/TLS for HTTPS.

3. **Session Layer (L5):** Establishes, manages, and terminates sessions between two hosts. Handles authentication and reconnection after interruption. Example: NetBIOS.

4. **Transport Layer (L4):** Provides end-to-end communication, segmentation, flow control, and error recovery. Uses port numbers (0–65535). Protocols: TCP (reliable) and UDP (unreliable). PDU: Segment/Datagram.

5. **Network Layer (L3):** Handles logical addressing (IP) and routing of packets across multiple networks. Routers operate here. PDU: Packet.

6. **Data Link Layer (L2):** Handles node-to-node data transfer, MAC addressing, framing, and error detection using CRC. Switches and bridges work here. PDU: Frame.

7. **Physical Layer (L1):** Transmits raw bits as electrical signals over cables/fiber/wireless. Defines voltage, connector type, and topology. Devices: Hubs, repeaters. PDU: Bits.

*(7 layers × 1 mark each = 7 marks)*

**(b) — 7 Marks**

**Encapsulation (Sender Side — Top Down):**

The process of adding headers (and trailers) at each layer:

```
+--------------------------------+
| Application: DATA (Message)    |
+--------------------------------+
         |
         v  (Transport adds TCP header)
+--------------------------------+
| TCP Header | DATA              |  -> SEGMENT
+--------------------------------+
         |
         v  (Network adds IP header)
+--------------------------------+
| IP Header | TCP Header | DATA  |  -> PACKET
+--------------------------------+
         |
```
