**Total Internal Reflection (TIR)** is the optical phenomenon in which a light ray striking the boundary between two media at an angle greater than the critical angle is completely reflected back into the denser medium, with no part of the ray being refracted into the rarer medium.

**The two necessary conditions for TIR are:**
1. Light must travel from a **medium of higher refractive index** to a medium of lower refractive index (i.e., from core $n_1$ to cladding $n_2$ with $n_1 > n_2$).
2. The **angle of incidence** at the core-cladding interface must be **greater than the critical angle** $\theta_c$ (i.e., $\theta_1 > \theta_c$, where $\theta_c = \sin^{-1}(n_2/n_1)$).

In an optical fiber, these conditions are satisfied by design, allowing light to propagate over long distances with negligible leakage.

---

### 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Module 2 Internal Choice:** Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Model]**

**(a) Explain the construction and working of a coaxial cable. Discuss the different types of coaxial cables with their characteristic impedances and typical applications.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**1. Construction (3 marks):**
A coaxial cable consists of four concentric layers, all sharing a common geometric axis (hence "co-axial"):
- **Inner Conductor:** A solid or stranded copper wire (sometimes silver-plated) that carries the high-frequency signal.
- **Dielectric Insulator:** A polyethylene, PVC, or foam polymer layer that physically separates and electrically insulates the inner conductor from the outer shield, while maintaining a uniform geometry to preserve a constant characteristic impedance.
- **Outer Conductor (Shield):** A braided copper mesh, an aluminum foil wrap, or a combination of both. It serves two purposes: (i) it provides the return path for the signal, completing the circuit, and (ii) it acts as a Faraday shield, containing the electromagnetic field inside the cable and blocking external EMI.
- **Outer Jacket:** A PVC, polyethylene, or fire-retardant polymer sheath that protects the cable against mechanical damage, moisture, chemicals, and UV radiation.

**2. Working Principle (2 marks):**
The signal is applied between the inner and outer conductors. The electromagnetic field exists entirely in the dielectric region between them; the outer shield both confines this field (preventing radiation losses) and rejects external interference. Because the field is bounded, coaxial cable supports much higher frequencies and longer distances than unshielded twisted pair.

**Characteristic impedance** of a coax cable is given by:

$$Z_0 = \frac{60}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}} \ln\!\left(\frac{D}{d}\right)$$

**3. Types, Impedances, and Applications (2 marks):**

| Type | Impedance | Typical Application |
|------|-----------|---------------------|
| RG-58 / 10Base-2 (Thinnet) | 50 Ω | Legacy Ethernet, RF signal distribution |
| RG-8 / 10Base-5 (Thicknet) | 50 Ω | Early Ethernet backbone |
| RG-59 | 75 Ω | CCTV, older cable TV, short video runs |
| RG-6 | 75 Ω | Modern CATV, satellite TV, broadband internet |
| RG-11 | 75 Ω | Long-distance CATV backbone |

The 50 Ω standard is a compromise between minimum attenuation (~75 Ω) and maximum power-handling (~30 Ω); 75 Ω is optimized for minimum attenuation, ideal for video.

---

**(b) With a neat block diagram, explain the structure of an optical fiber. Derive the expression for Numerical Aperture (NA) in terms of the core and cladding refractive indices.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**1. Structure (2 marks):**

An optical fiber consists of:
- **Core** (refractive index $n_1$, diameter 8–100 µm): The central glass region that carries the light.
- **Cladding** (refractive index $n_2 < n_1$, diameter ~125 µm): Surrounds the core, enables TIR.
- **Buffer/Primary Coating:** Acrylate polymer to protect against microbends and moisture.
- **Strength Members:** Kevlar aramid yarn to provide tensile strength.
- **Outer Jacket:** Final protective polymer sheath.

(Refer to Section 4.3 diagram for the layered cross-section.)

**2. Derivation of Numerical Aperture (5 marks):**

Consider a light ray entering the fiber end-face from air ($n_0 \approx 1$) at the maximum acceptance angle $\theta_a$. At the air-core interface, by Snell's law:

$$n_0 \sin\theta_a = n_1 \sin\theta_1$$

At the *limit* of acceptance, this ray strikes the core-cladding boundary at exactly the critical angle $\theta_c$, so the internal angle of incidence $\theta_1$ and $\theta_c$ are complementary:

$$\theta_1 = 90° - \theta_c \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sin\theta_1 = \cos\theta_c$$

Substituting:

$$n_0 \sin\theta_a = n_1 \cos\theta_c$$

By definition of the critical angle: $\sin\theta_c = \dfrac{n_2}{n_1}$

Using the identity $\cos\theta_c = \sqrt{1 - \sin^2\theta_c} = \dfrac{\sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}}{n_1}$:

$$n_0 \sin\theta_a = n_1 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}}{n_1} = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$$

With $n_0 = 1$:

$$\boxed{\;NA = \sin\theta_a = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}\;}$$

A high NA implies a large acceptance cone, easier source-to-fiber coupling, but more intermodal dispersion in multimode fibers.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024 / Model]**

**(a) Compare the various types of optical fibers based on their construction, mode of propagation, and bandwidth-distance characteristics. State one application for each type.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | Step-Index Multimode (SI-MM) | Graded-Index Multimode (GI-MM) | Single-Mode Step-Index (SMF) |
|-----------|------------------------------|--------------------------------|------------------------------|
| **Core diameter** | 50–100 µm | 50 / 62.5 µm | 8–10 µm |
| **Refractive index** | Uniform in core (abrupt step at cladding) | Parabolically decreasing in core | Uniform, very small core |
| **Modes of propagation** | Many, distinct paths | Many, helical paths with self-focusing | Only the fundamental (axial) mode |
| **Intermodal dispersion** | Very high | Reduced (rays arrive together) | Negligible |
| **Bandwidth-distance product** | ~10–100 MHz·km | ~200–2000 MHz·km | > 10,000 MHz·km (effectively THz) |
| **Typical light source** | LED | LED or laser | Laser only |
| **Typical max distance** | < 1 km | < 2 km | > 100 km (with amplifiers: thousands) |
| **Cost** | Lowest | Moderate | Highest |
| **Application** | LAN backbones, short data links, industrial networks | LANs, medium-haul telecom, CCTV backbone | Long-haul telecom, undersea cables, FTTH, CATV distribution |

**Numerical sketch of dispersion (1 mark for diagram credit):**
In SI-MM, different modes take different path lengths and arrive at the receiver at different times, causing the output pulse to spread. In GI-MM, the index profile continuously refracts the off-axis rays back toward the axis, equalizing their travel times. In SMF, only one mode exists, so no intermodal dispersion occurs.

---

**(b) A step-index optical fiber has a core refractive index of 1.50 and a cladding refractive index of 1.45. Calculate the numerical aperture, the acceptance angle, and the critical angle. If the fiber length is 5 km and the input power is 0.5 mW at 1310 nm, find the output power assuming an attenuation of 0.4 dB/km.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $n_1 = 1.50$, $n_2 = 1.45$
- $L = 5$ km, $P_{in} = 0.5$ mW, $\alpha = 0.4$ dB/km

**Step 1 — Numerical Aperture (2 marks):**

$$NA = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2} = \sqrt{(1.50)^2 - (1.45)^2} = \sqrt{2.2500 - 2.1025} = \sqrt{0.1475} \approx 0.3841$$

**Step 2 — Acceptance Angle (1 mark):**

$$\theta_a = \sin^{-1}(NA) = \sin^{-1}(0.3841) \approx 22.59°$$

**Step 3 — Critical Angle (1 mark):**

$$\theta_c = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{n_2}{n_1}\right) = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{1.45}{1.50}\right) = \sin^{-1}(0.9667) \approx 75.16°$$

*(Note: $\theta_c$ is large because the index difference is small — characteristic of weakly guiding optical fibers.)*

**Step 4 — Total Attenuation in dB (1 mark):**

$$A_{total} = \alpha \cdot L = 0.4 \text{ dB/km} \times 5 \text{ km} = 2.0 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 5 — Output Power (2 marks):**

Using $P_{out} = P_{in} \cdot 10^{-A_{total}/10}$:

$$P_{out} = 0.5 \text{ mW} \times 10^{-2.0/10} = 0.5 \text{ mW} \times 10^{-0.2}$$

$$10^{-0.2} \approx 0.6310$$

$$P_{out} = 0.5 \times 0.6310 = 0.3155 \text{ mW} \approx 315.5\;\mu\text{W}$$

**Final Answer:** $NA \approx 0.3841$, $\theta_a \approx 22.59°$, $\theta_c \approx 75.16°$, $P_{out} \approx 0.316$ mW.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in PECPT522 Module 2 Questions:**
> 1. **Mixing up angles:** Students frequently confuse the *angle of acceptance* $\theta_a$ (measured in air, from the fiber axis) with the *angle of incidence* $\theta_1$ (measured inside the core, from the normal at the core-cladding boundary). Remember the **complementary relationship**: $\theta_1 = 90° - \theta_c$ at the acceptance limit.
> 2. **Forgetting units in attenuation:** Always state the answer in **dB/km**, not dB. Two marks are routinely lost in Part B numericals.
> 3. **Wrong impedance for the application:** A 50 Ω cable used for a 75 Ω application causes severe signal reflections and standing waves. The examiner will test whether you know *why* 50 Ω and 75 Ω exist.
> 4. **Confusing bandwidth with bandwidth-distance product:** The bandwidth $B$ alone is for a specific length; the figure of merit is the *product* $B \cdot L$ (e.g., 400 MHz·km).
> 5. **Skipping the "n₀ = 1 (air)" step:** When deriving NA, students often forget to substitute $n_0 = 1$ and lose the simplification step marks.
> 6. **Writing "TIR happens because of total reflection"** — this is a circular definition. You must state the **two conditions** explicitly (denser-to-rarer medium AND incidence > critical angle).

---

### 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — PECPT522 Module 2**

- ☐ Transmission media are classified as **Guided (bounded)** and **Unguided (unbounded)**; the topic focuses on **Coaxial Cable** and **Fiber Optic Cable** under guided media.
- ☐ A **coaxial cable** has 4 layers: inner conductor → dielectric → outer shield (braid/foil) → outer jacket. The two conductors share a common axis.
- ☐ **Characteristic impedance** of a coax cable: $Z_0 = \dfrac{60}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}} \ln(D/d)$ Ω. Common standards are **50 Ω (data/RF)** and **75 Ω (video/CATV)**.
- ☐ **Attenuation in dB/km** formula: $\alpha = \dfrac{10}{L} \log_{10}(P_{in}/P_{out})$.
- ☐ An **optical fiber** carries light pulses through a glass core surrounded by cladding with $n_2 < n_1$.
- ☐ **Total Internal Reflection (TIR)** is the operating principle of optical fiber; requires (a) $n_1 > n_2$ and (b) angle of incidence > critical angle.
- ☐ **Critical angle:** $\theta_c = \sin^{-1}(n_2/n_1)$.
- ☐ **Numerical Aperture (NA):** $NA = \sin\theta_a = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2} = n_1\sqrt{2\Delta}$.
- ☐ Three types of optical fiber:
  - **Step-Index Multimode (SI-MM):** Large core, many modes, high dispersion, short reach.
  - **Graded-Index Multimode (GI-MM):** Parabolic index profile, reduced dispersion, medium reach.
  - **Single-Mode Fiber (SMF):** Tiny core (~9 µm), one mode, no intermodal dispersion, long-haul.
- ☐ Three **transmission windows** in optical fiber: **850 nm**, **1310 nm**, **1550 nm** (1550 nm has the lowest attenuation ~0.2 dB/km).
- ☐ Coaxial cable is **susceptible to EMI** (despite shielding) and limited to ~1 GHz practically; **optical fiber is immune to EMI** and supports bandwidths in the THz range.
- ☐ Output power for a given attenuation: $P_{out} = P_{in} \cdot 10^{-\alpha L / 10}$.
- ☐ Coaxial cable is **cheaper and easier to install**; fiber is **lighter, more secure, and supports longer distances without repeaters** (50–100 km for SMF vs. 1–10 km for coax).
- ☐ In KTU numericals, **always** carry units in every line of the solution; **always** show the substitution step; **always** quote the final answer with units.
- ☐ Remember the **physical meaning** behind every equation — the examiner often awards 1–2 marks for "explanation" sub-parts even in numerical questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Link Layer: Framing
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT522 - COMPUTER COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Data Link Layer: Framing

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Framing** is the **Data Link Layer (DLL)** process of dividing a continuous bit stream received from the **Network Layer** into manageable, discrete units called **frames** before transmitting them over a shared physical medium. Each frame acts as a self-contained packet of information that can be independently addressed, synchronized, error-checked, and routed at Layer 2 of the **OSI (Open Systems Interconnection)** model.

The **Data Link Layer** is the second layer in both the **OSI 7-layer model** and the **TCP/IP model**, sitting directly above the **Physical Layer**. Its primary responsibilities are organized into two sub-layers:
- **LLC (Logical Link Control)** – Upper sub-layer: handles multiplexing, flow control, and error recovery.
- **MAC (Media Access Control)** – Lower sub-layer: handles addressing, channel access, and **framing**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Framing is Mandatory:**
> The Physical Layer delivers only a raw stream of bits (`0`s and `1`s) with **no inherent boundaries**. Without framing, the receiver cannot determine:
> - Where one frame ends and the next begins.
> - Which bits belong to the **payload (data)** vs. **control information** (header/trailer).
> - Whether the received frame is corrupted.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **postal system** without envelopes:
- A sender writes a **100-page letter** continuously without separation.
- The postal worker (physical transport) hands it over to a receiver.
- The receiver receives **one continuous scroll** and has no idea: *Where does Page 1 end? Where does Page 50 begin? Is any page missing?*

**Framing is the "envelope" of the networking world.** Just as an envelope:
- Has a **start marker** (postmark seal),
- Has an **end marker** (closing seal),
- Contains the **payload** (the actual letter),
- Carries **metadata** (sender/receiver address, tracking ID, stamps, weight).

Similarly, a **frame** in the Data Link Layer:
- Has a **start flag** (begin marker),
- Has an **end flag** (closing marker),
- Contains the **payload** (the actual data from the Network Layer),
- Carries **metadata** like MAC addresses, length/type fields, and an **error-detecting code** (FCS/CRC).

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> Key standardized values used in framing protocols:
> - **Flag Byte (HDLC/PPP):** `01111110` (binary) = `0x7E` (hex) = `~` (ASCII)
> - **Escape Byte (PPP):** `01111101` (binary) = `0x7D` (hex) = `}`
> - **Minimum Frame Size (Ethernet):** **64 bytes** (to ensure collision detection on CSMA/CD networks)
> - **Maximum Frame Size (Ethernet):** **1518 bytes** (including header/trailer; payload = **1500 bytes**)
> - **Maximum Frame Size (802.11 Wi-Fi):** **2346 bytes**

## 1.4 Layered Position of Framing

$$\text{Application Layer} \rightarrow \text{Transport Layer} \rightarrow \text{Network Layer} \rightarrow \boxed{\text{Data Link Layer (Framing)}} \rightarrow \text{Physical Layer}$$

The Data Link Layer takes a **network-layer packet (datagram)** and encapsulates it into a **frame** before handing it to the Physical Layer for bit-level transmission.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Framing Strategies

The Data Link Layer employs **two principal strategies** to identify frame boundaries:

### 2.1.1 Fixed-Size Framing

- Frames have a **predetermined, constant length** (e.g., **ATM cells = 53 bytes**).
- **No start/end delimiters needed** — the receiver simply counts incoming bits to determine frame boundaries.
- **Advantage:** Simple parsing, predictable latency, ideal for real-time/voice traffic.
- **Disadvantage:** Wastes bandwidth (padding required for small payloads); inflexible for variable-length data.

### 2.1.2 Variable-Size Framing (Most Common)

Frames have **variable length**, and explicit **delimiters** mark boundaries. This is further divided into two sub-categories:

#### (A) Character-Oriented Framing (Byte/ASCII Framing)

- The smallest unit of framing is a **byte (8 bits)**.
- Special **reserved ASCII characters** serve as delimiters:
  - **SYN (Synchronous Idle):** `0x16` — used to establish synchronization at session start.
  - **STX (Start of Text):** `0x02` — marks frame beginning.
  - **ETX (End of Text):** `0x03` — marks frame end.
  - **DLE (Data Link Escape):** `0x10` — used for transparency.
- **Problem:** If the data payload accidentally contains `STX` or `ETX`, the receiver misinterprets it as a delimiter.
- **Solution: Byte/Character Stuffing**

**Byte Stuffing Rules (Character-Oriented):**
1. Sender scans payload for any reserved control byte (`STX`, `ETX`, `DLE`).
2. If found, sender **inserts a `DLE` byte** before it (i.e., "stuffs" an extra DLE).
3. The receiver, upon seeing `DLE` followed by a reserved byte, **removes the stuffed DLE** and treats the second byte as data.
4. A `DLE` appearing in actual data is sent as `DLE DLE` (doubled).

#### (B) Bit-Oriented Framing (Bit Stuffing)

- The smallest unit is a **single bit**, not a byte.
- Uses an **8-bit flag pattern: `01111110`** to mark both start and end of a frame.
- **Problem:** If the payload contains the pattern `01111110` in its data, it will be misinterpreted as a flag.
- **Solution: Bit Stuffing**

**Bit Stuffing Rules (HDLC/PPP Style):**
1. Sender scans the payload bit-by-bit.
2. Whenever **five consecutive `1` bits** are detected, the sender **automatically inserts a `0` bit** after them.
3. The receiver, upon detecting **five `1` bits followed by a `0`**, **removes the stuffed `0`** to recover the original data.
4. If the receiver detects **six or more `1` bits**, it signals an error (abort).

## 2.2 Frame Structure (Generic & Protocol-Specific)

### Generic Frame Format
$$\underbrace{\text{Flag}}_{\text{8 bits}} \mid \underbrace{\text{Header}}_{\text{Source/Dest/Type}} \mid \underbrace{\text{Payload (Data)}}_{\text{Variable}} \mid \underbrace{\text{Trailer (FCS/CRC)}}_{\text{16 or 32 bits}} \mid \underbrace{\text{Flag}}_{\text{8 bits}}$$

### HDLC (High-Level Data Link Control) Frame
$$\text{Flag (01111110)} \mid \text{Address (8/16 bits)} \mid \text{Control (8/16 bits)} \mid \text{Information} \mid \text{FCS (16/32 bits)} \mid \text{Flag (01111110)}$$

### PPP (Point-to-Point Protocol) Frame
$$\text{Flag (7E)} \mid \text{Address (FF)} \mid \text{Control (03)} \mid \text{Protocol (8/16 bits)} \mid \text{Information} \mid \text{FCS (16/32 bits)} \mid \text{Flag (7E)}$$

### Ethernet (IEEE 802.3) Frame
$$\text{Preamble} \mid \text{SFD} \mid \text{Dest MAC} \mid \text{Src MAC} \mid \text{Length/Type} \mid \text{Data (46-1500)} \mid \text{FCS (32 bits)}$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is a **high-yield cheat sheet** for KTU examinations on Framing & Data Link Layer fundamentals.

| **Parameter** | **Symbol/Formula** | **Value/Description** | **Unit** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Frame Transmission Time | $T_f = \dfrac{L}{R}$ | $L$ = frame length, $R$ = link rate | seconds |
| Propagation Delay | $T_p = \dfrac{d}{v}$ | $d$ = distance, $v \approx 2 \times 10^8$ m/s in copper/fiber | seconds |
| End-to-End Delay | $T_{total} = T_f + T_p$ | Sum of transmission + propagation delay | seconds |
| Efficiency (Stop-and-Wait) | $\eta = \dfrac{T_f}{T_f + 2T_p}$ | Used in flow control analysis | dimensionless (0-1) |
| Effective Throughput | $S = R \times \eta$ | Maximum data rate accounting for overhead | bps |
| Minimum Frame Size (Ethernet) | $L_{min} = R \times \dfrac{2T_p}{bandwidth\_factor}$ | **64 bytes** for 10/100 Mbps Ethernet | bytes |
| Bit Stuffing Overhead | $\text{Overhead} \approx \dfrac{\text{stuff bits}}{\text{original bits}}$ | Roughly 1 stuffed bit per 5 consecutive `1`s | ratio |
| CRC Generator Polynomial | $G(x)$ of degree $n$ | Used to compute FCS for error detection | polynomial |
| Hamming Distance | $d(x,y) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i \oplus y_i$ | Number of differing bits between codewords | integer |
| Checksum (1's complement sum) | $C = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \pmod{2^{16}-1}$ | 16-bit Internet checksum | 16-bit value |
| Parity Bit (Even) | $P = x_1 \oplus x_2 \oplus \ldots \oplus x_n$ | XOR of all data bits | 1 bit |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial & Research Applications of Framing:**
> - **Ethernet (802.3):** Backbone of all LANs, data centers, and enterprise networks — relies on variable-size framing with MAC addressing.
> - **Wi-Fi (802.11):** Wireless LANs use a modified version of Ethernet framing with additional fields for radio management.
> - **PPP/PPPoE:** Used in DSL broadband, dial-up, and VPN tunnels.
> - **HDLC/SDLC:** Foundation of legacy telecom (X.25, ISDN) and modern cellular signaling (LTE S1-AP uses SCTP-over-IP, but HDLC principles persist in lower layers).
> - **5G NR (New Radio):** Uses flexible **subframe numerology** with variable-size slot framing for ultra-low latency (URLLC) and eMBB.
> - **Optical Networks (OTN):** Use fixed-size framing via **OPU/ODU/OTU containers** for predictable optical transport.
> - **IoT Protocols:** LoRaWAN, Zigbee, and BLE use custom bit-oriented framing optimized for low-power, low-bandwidth channels.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Bit Stuffing Derivation (Worked Example)

**Given:** A raw data bit-stream from the Network Layer:  
$$\text{Data} = 011011111110011111101111110$$

We need to apply **HDLC bit stuffing** before transmission.

### Step 1: Identify the Working Payload
We extract the network-layer data **excluding** the start and end flags. The flags themselves (`01111110`) are *never* stuffed — they are the delimiters.

$$\text{Payload} = 011011111110011111101111110$$

### Step 2: Apply the Bit Stuffing Rule
**Rule:** After every occurrence of **five consecutive `1` bits**, insert a single `0` bit.

Scanning left to right:

| **Position in Stream** | **Detected Pattern (Last 5 bits)** | **Action** | **Output Stream So Far** |
|---|---|---|---|
| `0` `1` `1` `0` `1` | `01101` (only 2 `1`s) | Pass through | `01101` |
| `1` `1` `1` `1` `1` | **`11111`** ← Trigger! | Insert `0` after 5th `1` | `011011111` `0` |
| `1` `1` `1` `0` `0` | `11110` | Pass through | `0110111110` `0` `1100` |
| `1` `1` `1` `1` `1` | **`11111`** ← Trigger! | Insert `0` | `...1100` `11111` `0` |
| `0` `1` `1` `1` `1` | `11110` | Pass through | `...111110` `1111` `0` |
| `1` `1` `1` `0` | `11110` | Pass through | `...1111110111` `0` |
| Final bit | — | Pass through | `...011110` |

### Step 3: Final Stuffed Bit Stream (Between Flags)
$$\boxed{\text{Stuffed Payload} = 0110111110\mathbf{0}1100111110\mathbf{0}111110\mathbf{0}11110}$$

Notice the **three inserted `0` bits** (highlighted in bold) — one for each occurrence of five consecutive `1`s in the original data.

### Step 4: Complete HDLC Frame Construction
$$\text{Transmitted Frame} = \underbrace{01111110}_{\text{Start Flag}} \mid 011011111\mathbf{0}0110011111\mathbf{0}011111\mathbf{0}011110 \mid \underbrace{01111110}_{\text{End Flag}}$$

### Step 5: Receiver-Side De-Stuffing (Verification)
The receiver scans the incoming bit stream. Upon detecting **five `1` bits followed by a `0`**, it **deletes that `0`** and outputs the original bit stream.

$$\text{Received: } 011011111\mathbf{0}0110011111\mathbf{0}011111\mathbf{0}011110$$
$$\text{After removing stuffed 0s: } 011011111110011111101111110 \;\checkmark$$

## 3.2 Byte Stuffing Derivation (Worked Example)

**Given:** A Network Layer message containing binary data mixed with reserved control bytes. We'll represent the message in **hexadecimal** form:

$$\text{Data (Hex)} = 41\;02\;45\;4D\;03\;FF\;10\;7E\;C3$$

Where the reserved characters are:
- `02` = **STX** (Start of Text)
- `03` = **ETX** (End of Text)
- `10` = **DLE** (Data Link Escape)

### Step 1: Identify Reserved Bytes in Data
Scanning the data, the reserved bytes found are: `02`, `03`, and `10`.

### Step 2: Apply Byte Stuffing Rule
**Rule:** Insert a `DLE` (`0x10`) byte **before** every reserved byte. If a real `DLE` appears in data, transmit it as `DLE DLE` (double it).

| **Original Byte** | **Classification** | **Stuffed Output** |
|---|---|---|
| `41` (A) | Data | `41` |
| `02` (STX) | **Reserved** | `10 02` |
| `45` (E) | Data | `45` |
| `4D` (M) | Data | `4D` |
| `03` (ETX) | **Reserved** | `10 03` |
| `FF` | Data | `FF` |
| `10` (DLE) | **Real DLE in data** | `10 10` |
| `7E` (~) | Data | `7E` |
| `C3` | Data | `C3` |

### Step 3: Construct the Final Stuffed Frame
$$\text{Frame Body (Hex)} = 41\; \mathbf{10}\;02\;45\;4D\; \mathbf{10}\;03\;FF\; \mathbf{10}\;10\;7E\;C3$$

The stuffed data is then sandwiched between STX/ETX delimiters (or flags in more modern protocols):

$$\underbrace{02}_{\text{STX}} \mid 41\;\mathbf{10}\;02\;45\;4D\;\mathbf{10}\;03\;FF\;\mathbf{10}\;10\;7E\;C3 \mid \underbrace{03}_{\text{ETX}}$$

### Step 4: Receiver De-Stuffing
- See `10 02` → output `02` (data, not delimiter).
- See `10 03` → output `03` (data, not delimiter).
- See `10 10` → output single `10` (data, not escape).
- The receiver thus reconstructs: `41 02 45 4D 03 FF 10 7E C3` $\checkmark$

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This **comparison matrix** is a high-frequency question type in KTU exams. Master this for full marks.

| **Feature** | **Character-Oriented Framing** | **Bit-Oriented Framing** |
|---|---|---|
| **Smallest Unit** | Byte (8 bits) | Bit (1 bit) |
| **Delimiter** | Reserved ASCII bytes (STX, ETX) | Flag pattern `01111110` |
| **Transparency Method** | Byte stuffing (insert DLE) | Bit stuffing (insert 0 after 5 ones) |
| **Overhead** | Higher (1 byte per occurrence) | Lower (1 bit per 5 `1`s) |
| **Code Dependency** | Tied to specific character codes (e.g., ASCII) | Code-independent (works on any binary data) |
| **Typical Protocols** | **BSC (Binary Synchronous Comm.)**, **DDCMP** | **HDLC**, **PPP**, **SDLC** |
| **Reliability** | Prone to byte-sequence errors | Robust against bit-pattern corruption |
| **Modern Usage** | Mostly legacy | Industry standard (WAN, broadband) |
| **Error Detection Trigger** | Reserved byte mismatch | **6 or more** consecutive `1`s = abort |

## 3.4 Derivation: Frame Transmission Time & Efficiency

For a link of rate $R$ bps, frame length $L$ bits, and one-way propagation delay $T_p$:

### Step 1: Define Frame Transmission Time
The time to push all $L$ bits of a frame onto the wire:
$$T_f = \frac{L}{R} \quad \text{(seconds)}$$

### Step 2: Define Propagation Delay
The time for a single bit to travel from sender to receiver across distance $d$ at speed $v$:
$$T_p = \frac{d}{v} \quad \text{(seconds)}$$

For copper/fiber, $v \approx 2 \times 10^8$ m/s.

### Step 3: Total Time for One-Way Delivery
$$T_{delivery} = T_f + T_p = \frac{L}{R} + \frac{d}{v}$$

### Step 4: End-to-End Round-Trip (for Stop-and-Wait ARQ)
$$T_{round} = T_f + 2T_p = \frac{L}{R} + \frac{2d}{v}$$

### Step 5: Link Utilization (Efficiency)
The fraction of time the link is actually used for transmitting useful data:
$$\eta = \frac{T_f}{T_f + 2T_p} = \frac{1}{1 + 2a}$$
where $a = \dfrac{T_p}{T_f}$ is the **bandwidth-delay product ratio**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** When $a \ll 1$ (i.e., $T_f \gg T_p$, fast links or short distances), $\eta \to 1$ (excellent utilization). When $a \gg 1$ (long fat pipes, e.g., satellite links), $\eta \to 0$ — framing must be paired with **sliding window protocols** for acceptable throughput.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Framing Process in the Data Link Layer

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Network Layer Packet Arrives] --> B[Data Link Layer Receives Datagram]
    B --> C{Choose Framing Strategy}
    C -->|Fixed-Size| D[Pad to Fixed Length e.g. ATM 53 bytes]
    C -->|Variable-Size| E{Character or Bit Oriented}
    E -->|Character Oriented| F[Insert STX and ETX Delimiters]
    E -->|Bit Oriented| G[Insert 01111110 Flags]
    F --> H[Apply Byte Stuffing for Transparency]
    G --> I[Apply Bit Stuffing for Transparency]
    H --> J[Add Header Source Dest MAC]
    I --> J[Add Header Source Dest MAC]
    D --> K[Add Header Address Control]
    J --> L[Compute FCS or CRC]
    K --> L[Compute FCS or CRC]
    L --> M[Assemble Complete Frame]
    M --> N[Hand Over to Physical Layer]
    N --> O[Transmit Bits on Medium]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Bit Stuffing Algorithm Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: Begin Bit Stream] --> A[Read Next Bit from Payload]
    A --> B{Is it a 1 bit}
    B -->|No| C[Reset 1s Counter to 0]
    B -->|Yes| D[Increment 1s Counter]
    D --> E{Counter equals 5}
    E -->|No| F[Append Bit to Output]
    E -->|Yes| G[Insert Stuffed 0 to Output]
    G --> H[Reset Counter to 0]
    C --> I{End of Payload}
    F --> I{End of Payload}
    H --> I{End of Payload}
    I -->|No| A
    I -->|Yes| J[Append Closing Flag 01111110]
    J --> K[Transmit Frame]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: HDLC Frame Architecture Block Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Flag 01111110] --> P2[Address Field 8 or 16 bits]
    P2 --> P3[Control Field 8 or 16 bits]
    P3 --> P4[Information Field Variable Length]
    P4 --> P5[FCS Frame Check Sequence 16 or 32 bits]
    P5 --> P6[Flag 01111110]
    
    subgraph transparency
        T1[Bit Stuffing: Insert 0 after 5 ones] -.-> P4
    end
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Data Link Layer Sub-Layer Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    NW[Network Layer] --> DLL[Data Link Layer]
    DLL --> LLC[LLC Sub-Layer: Framing, Error Control, Flow Control]
    DLL --> MAC[MAC Sub-Layer: Addressing, Channel Access]
    MAC --> PHY[Physical Layer: Bit Transmission]
    
    subgraph Services of DLL
        S1[Framing]
        S2[Physical Addressing]
        S3[Error Detection and Correction]
        S4[Flow Control]
        S5[Access Control]
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define framing in the Data Link Layer. List and briefly explain the two main types of variable-size framing.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Framing** is the process of dividing a continuous bit stream from the Network Layer into discrete units called **frames** at the Data Link Layer, so that the receiver can synchronize and extract meaningful data.

The two main types of **variable-size framing** are:

1. **Character-Oriented Framing (Byte-Oriented):**
   - Uses **reserved ASCII control characters** (e.g., `STX`, `ETX`) to mark frame start and end.
   - Ensures transparency via **byte stuffing** (inserting a `DLE` byte before any reserved character in the payload).
   - **Example Protocol:** **BSC (Binary Synchronous Communication)**.

2. **Bit-Oriented Framing:**
   - Uses a special **8-bit flag pattern `01111110`** to mark both the beginning and end of every frame.
   - Ensures transparency via **bit stuffing** (inserting a `0` after every sequence of five consecutive `1`s in the payload).
   - **Example Protocols:** **HDLC**, **PPP**, **SDLC**.

| Aspect | Character-Oriented | Bit-Oriented |
|---|---|---|
| Unit | Byte | Bit |
| Delimiter | STX, ETX | `01111110` |
| Transparency | Byte stuffing | Bit stuffing |
| Example | BSC | HDLC |

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**With a neat diagram, explain the bit stuffing technique used in HDLC protocol.**

**Model Answer:**

**HDLC Bit Stuffing** ensures that the flag pattern `01111110` never appears inside the data payload. The procedure is:

**At the Sender:**
1. A flag byte `01111110` is transmitted to mark frame start.
2. The sender transmits the **header** (address + control fields).
3. While transmitting the **data/payload**, the sender counts consecutive `1` bits.
4. After transmitting **five consecutive `1` bits**, the sender **automatically inserts (stuffs) a single `0` bit**.
5. The **FCS (Frame Check Sequence)** is appended.
6. A closing flag `01111110` is transmitted.

**At the Receiver:**
1. The receiver scans incoming bits.
2. Upon detecting **five `1` bits followed by a `0`**, it **removes the stuffed `0`** to recover the original data.
3. If it sees **six or more consecutive `1` bits**, it interprets this as the closing flag or an error/abort.

**Example:**  
Data to send: `011011111110011111101111110`  
Stuffed data: `0110111110` `0` `1100111110` `0` `111110` `0` `11110` (three `0`s inserted)

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the bit stuffing and byte stuffing techniques with suitable examples. Compare their efficiency and overhead.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 1 Mbps satellite link connects two ground stations separated by a distance of 36,000 km. The data link layer uses HDLC framing with frames of size 1000 bits. Calculate:
1. The propagation delay $T_p$.
2. The frame transmission time $T_f$.
3. The total round-trip time for Stop-and-Wait ARQ.
4. The link utilization $\eta$.
5. Suggest one method to improve utilization.

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Byte Stuffing (Character-Oriented):**

In **character-oriented protocols** (e.g., **BSC**), special ASCII characters like `STX` (Start of Text, `0x02`) and `ETX` (End of Text, `0x03`) are used as frame delimiters. To prevent the data payload from being mistakenly interpreted as a delimiter, **byte stuffing** is used.

**Rules:**
- Before sending, the sender scans the data.
- For every reserved character (`STX`, `ETX`, `DLE`) appearing in the data, the sender **inserts a `DLE` byte** in front of it.
- A `DLE` byte present in actual data is sent as **two consecutive `DLE` bytes** (DLE stuffing).

**Example:**  
Original data (hex): `41 02 45 4D 03 FF 10 7E C3`  
Stuffed data: `41 10 02 45 4D 10 03 FF 10 10 7E C3`

The receiver reverses this process: seeing `DLE` followed by a reserved character, it discards the `DLE` and outputs the original character.

**Bit Stuffing (Bit-Oriented):**

In **bit-oriented protocols** (e.g., **HDLC**, **PPP**), the frame is delimited by the flag `01111110`. To prevent this pattern from appearing inside the data, **bit stuffing** is used.

**Rules:**
- The sender inserts a `0` bit after every sequence of **five consecutive `1` bits** in the payload.
- The receiver removes any `0` bit that follows five consecutive `1` bits.

**Example:**  
Original data: `011011111110011111101111110`  
Stuffed data: `0110111110` `0` `1100111110` `0` `111110` `0` `11110`

**Comparison Table:**

| **Aspect** | **Byte Stuffing** | **Bit Stuffing** |
|---|---|---|
| **Overhead per occurrence** | 1 byte (8 bits) | 1 bit |
| **Code dependency** | Requires specific character set | Independent of character encoding |
| **Suitability** | Text/ASCII data | Any binary data (images, video, encrypted) |
| **Modern relevance** | Largely obsolete | Industry standard |
| **Efficiency for random data** | Lower | Higher |

**Efficiency & Overhead Analysis:**

For random binary data, the probability of encountering five consecutive `1`s in any block of 5 bits is $1/32$. So, on average, **1 stuffed bit is inserted every 32 bits**, giving an overhead of $\approx 3.125\%$.

For byte stuffing in ASCII text, every reserved control byte triggers a full 8-bit overhead, which is more expensive per occurrence.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:**
- Link rate: $R = 1 \text{ Mbps} = 10^6$ bps
- Distance: $d = 36{,}000$ km $= 3.6 \times 10^7$ m
- Frame size: $L = 1000$ bits
- Propagation speed in vacuum/satellite: $v \approx 3 \times 10^8$ m/s

### Step 1: Propagation Delay $T_p$
$$T_p = \frac{d}{v} = \frac{3.6 \times 10^7}{3 \times 10^8} = 0.12 \text{ seconds} = 120 \text{ ms}$$

### Step 2: Frame Transmission Time $T_f$
$$T_f = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{1000}{10^6} = 10^{-3} \text{ seconds} = 1 \text{ ms}$$

### Step 3: Total Round-Trip Time (Stop-and-Wait ARQ)
$$T_{total} = T_f + 2T_p = 1 \text{ ms} + 2(120 \text{ ms}) = 1 + 240 = 241 \text{ ms}$$

### Step 4: Link Utilization $\eta$
$$\eta = \frac{T_f}{T_f + 2T_p} = \frac{1}{241} \approx 0.00415 = 0.415\%$$

### Step 5: Method to Improve Utilization
The utilization is abysmally low because the **bandwidth-delay product is very large** compared to the frame size:
$$a = \frac{T_p}{T_f} = \frac{120}{1} = 120$$

**Solution:** Use a **Sliding Window Protocol** (e.g., Go-Back-N or Selective Repeat) with a window size $W \geq 2a + 1 = 241$ to keep the pipeline full and achieve near-100% utilization.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, describe the HDLC frame format. Explain the role of each field. What is the significance of the FCS field?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A data link layer protocol uses byte stuffing with the following reserved characters:
- `STX = 02`, `ETX = 03`, `DLE = 10` (all in hex)

Given the following data to be transmitted (in hex):  
`48 45 4C 4C 4F 02 10 03 57 4F 52 4C 44`

Apply byte stuffing and show the resulting frame. Also show the de-stuffing process at the receiver.

**Model Solution for (a):**

**HDLC Frame Format Diagram:**

```
+--------+--------+--------+--------+--------+--------+--------+
| Flag   | Address| Control| Information     | FCS    | Flag   |
|01111110| 8/16 b | 8/16 b | Variable        |16/32 b |01111110|
+--------+--------+--------+-----------------+--------+--------+
```

**Role of Each Field:**

1. **Flag Field (8 bits):**  
   The bit pattern `01111110` marks both the **beginning and end** of the frame. The same flag is used as both opener and closer. Two consecutive frames can share a single flag.

2. **Address Field (8 or 16 bits):**  
   Identifies the **secondary station** (sender or receiver) in point-to-multipoint configurations. In point-to-point links, it may be fixed.

3. **Control Field (8 or 16 bits):**  
   Defines the **frame type** and its function:
   - **I-frames (Information):** Carry user data; contain sequence numbers for flow/error control.
   - **S-frames (Supervisory):** Used for flow control and error recovery (e.g., ACK, NAK, RNR).
   - **U-frames (Unnumbered):** Used for link management (e.g., connection setup, disconnect).

4. **Information Field (Variable Length):**  
   Contains the **actual user payload** (data from the Network Layer). May be empty for supervisory frames.

5. **FCS (Frame Check Sequence) Field (16 or 32 bits):**  
   Contains a **CRC (Cyclic Redundancy Check)** code computed over the Address, Control, and Information fields.  
   **Significance of FCS:**
   - Enables **error detection** at the receiver.
   - The receiver recomputes the CRC over the received data and compares it with the FCS field.
   - Mismatch → frame is **discarded** (or retransmitted in ARQ protocols).
   - **16-bit CRC** detects all single-bit errors, all double-bit errors, and all burst errors up to 16 bits.
   - **32-bit CRC** detects all burst errors up to 32 bits with high probability.

6. **Bit Stuffing:**  
   The Information field undergoes bit stuffing (insert 0 after five 1s) to ensure the flag pattern does not appear inside the data.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given Data (Hex):** `48 45 4C 4C 4F 02 10 03 57 4F 52 4C 44`

### Step 1: Identify Reserved Bytes in the Data
Scanning the data:
- `48` (H) – data
- `45` (E) – data
- `4C` (L) – data
- `4C` (L) – data
- `4F` (O) – data
- `02` – **STX (reserved)** → needs stuffing
- `10` – **DLE (reserved)** → real DLE in data → needs doubling
- `03` – **ETX (reserved)** → needs stuffing
- `57 4F 52 4C 44` (WORLD) – data

### Step 2: Apply Byte Stuffing Rule
For each reserved byte, insert a `DLE` (10) before it. For a real DLE, transmit two consecutive DLEs.

| Original | Stuffed |
|---|---|
| `48` | `48` |
| `45` | `45` |
| `4C` | `4C` |
| `4C` | `4C` |
| `4F` | `4F` |
| `02` | `10 02` |
| `10` | `10 10` |
| `03` | `10 03` |
| `57` | `57` |
| `4F` | `4F` |
| `52` | `52` |
| `4C` | `4C` |
| `44` | `44` |

### Step 3: Final Stuffed Frame

$$\underbrace{02}_{\text{STX}} \mid \underbrace{48\ 45\ 4C\ 4C\ 4F\ \mathbf{10\ 02}\ \mathbf{10\ 10}\ \mathbf{10\ 03}\ 57\ 4F\ 52\ 4C\ 44}_{\text{Stuffed Data}} \mid \underbrace{03}_{\text{ETX}}$$

In hex:
```
02 48 45 4C 4C 4F 10 02 10 10 10 03 57 4F 52 4C 44 03
```

### Step 4: Receiver De-Stuffing Process
1. Receiver sees `STX` (`02`) → frame start.
2. Reads `48 45 4C 4C 4F` → output as data.
3. Sees `10 02` → **DLE followed by STX** → output `02` as data.
4. Sees `10 10` → **doubled DLE** → output single `10` as data.
5. Sees `10 03` → **DLE followed by ETX** → output `03` as data.
6. Reads `57 4F 52 4C 44` → output as data.
7. Sees standalone `03` → **frame end** (not stuffed).

**Recovered data:** `48 45 4C 4C 4F 02 10 03 57 4F 52 4C 44` $\checkmark$  
(Decodes to ASCII: "HELLO" + control bytes + "WORLD")

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to double a real DLE byte:** Students often stuff reserved characters but forget that a real `DLE` in data must also be transmitted as `DLE DLE`. Failure to do this causes the receiver to mis-interpret the next byte.
> 2. **Confusing bit stuffing rule:** Some students stuff a `1` instead of a `0` after five `1`s. The stuffed bit is **always a `0`**.
> 3. **Ignoring the flag pattern's role:** The flag `01111110` is not part of the payload — it is the delimiter. Make sure the stuffed stream does not have the flag pattern at the end.
> 4. **Unit errors in numerical problems:** Propagation delay requires distance in **meters** and speed in **m/s**. Frame transmission time requires frame length in **bits** and rate in **bps**.
> 5. **Mixing character-oriented and bit-oriented examples:** Always state the protocol you're using. PPP and HDLC use **bit stuffing**, BSC and DDCMP use **byte stuffing**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

- **Framing** is the DLL's process of converting a Network Layer datagram into a discrete unit (**frame**) for transmission.
- **Two strategies:** Fixed-size (e.g., ATM, OTN) and Variable-size (e.g., Ethernet, HDLC, PPP).
- **Character-Oriented Framing:** Uses reserved ASCII bytes (`STX`, `ETX`, `DLE`); transparency via **byte stuffing**.
- **Bit-Oriented Framing:** Uses 8-bit flag `01111110`; transparency via **bit stuffing** (insert `0` after five `1`s).
- **HDLC Flag:** `01111110` (binary) = `0x7E` (hex).
- **Receiver Rule for Bit Stuffing:** Detect 5 `1`s + 1 `0` → remove the `0`; detect 6+ `1`s → flag or error.
- **Byte Stuffing Rule:** Insert `DLE` before reserved bytes; double any real `DLE` in data.
- **PPP Frame:** `Flag | FF | 03 | Protocol | Info | FCS | Flag` (FF=address, 03=control).
- **HDLC Frame:** `Flag | Address | Control | Info | FCS | Flag`.
- **Ethernet (802.3) Limits:** Min 64 bytes, Max 1518 bytes, payload 46–1500 bytes.
- **FCS Field:** Contains CRC (16-bit or 32-bit) for error detection over header + payload.
- **Frame Transmission Time:** $T_f = L/R$; **Propagation Delay:** $T_p = d/v$ (use $v = 2 \times 10^8$ for fiber/copper, $3 \times 10^8$ for wireless/vacuum).
- **Link Utilization (Stop-and-Wait):** $\eta = T_f / (T_f + 2T_p)$.
- **Bandwidth-Delay Product:** $a = T_p / T_f$. If $a \gg 1$, use **sliding window** protocols to improve throughput.
- **Modern Protocols:** All contemporary framing uses **bit-oriented** techniques (HDLC, PPP, Ethernet, Wi-Fi).
- **Transparency:** Both byte stuffing and bit stuffing exist purely to preserve **data integrity** in the presence of delimiter-like patterns in the payload.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flow Control: Stop and Wait, Sliding Window Flow Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT522 - COMPUTER COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Flow Control: Stop and Wait, Sliding Window Flow Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 What is Data Link Layer Flow Control?

**Flow Control** is a fundamental Data Link Layer function that coordinates the rate of data transmission between a **Sender** and a **Receiver** so that the sender does not transmit data faster than the receiver can process it. It is essentially a speed-matching mechanism that prevents the receiver's buffer from overflowing when the sender is faster than the receiver.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):** Flow control is the set of procedures used to restrict the amount of data that the sender can send before waiting for an acknowledgement from the receiver. It ensures that the receiver's buffer does not become overwhelmed and that no data frames are lost due to buffer overflow.

## 1.2 Why is Flow Control Needed?

In any communication system, the sender and receiver typically operate at different speeds. The receiver needs time to:
- Process each incoming frame
- Copy the data from the hardware buffer to memory
- Execute the upper-layer protocol tasks

Without flow control, the sender will **flood** the receiver, leading to **buffer overflow** and **data loss**.

## 1.3 Two Categories of Flow Control

| Category | Description | Example Protocols |
|----------|-------------|-------------------|
| **Stop-and-Wait Flow Control** | Sender transmits one frame and waits for an ACK before sending the next. | Simple stop-and-wait ARQ |
| **Sliding Window Flow Control** | Sender can transmit multiple frames (up to window size N) before needing an ACK. | Go-Back-N ARQ, Selective Repeat ARQ |

## 1.4 Intuitive Analogy: The Restaurant Kitchen 🍽️

> [!NOTE]
> **Restaurant Analogy (Stop-and-Wait):** Imagine a single waiter (sender) in a tiny restaurant. He takes one order to the kitchen, then **waits** at the kitchen counter (cannot serve other tables) until the chef (receiver) finishes that one dish and hands it back. Only then does he return to the table. This is slow but foolproof — the chef is never overwhelmed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conveyor Belt Analogy (Sliding Window):** Now imagine a busy buffet line. The chef places **multiple dishes** (frames) on a conveyor belt (the window) of fixed length. The waiter picks them up as he can, and sends a single "refill" signal when the belt has space for more. The chef never waits for each individual confirmation — he just keeps the belt full. This is much faster, and the belt's length is the **window size**.

## 1.5 Two Sub-Functions of Data Link Layer

The Data Link Layer actually performs **two** closely related but distinct functions:
1. **Flow Control** — prevent the sender from outrunning the receiver *(today's topic)*.
2. **Error Control** — detect and recover from lost, duplicated, or corrupted frames using ARQ techniques.

Both functions are often implemented together, but in the KTU 2024 scheme, they are conceptually separated for exam purposes.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Stop-and-Wait Flow Control — Operational Logic

Stop-and-Wait is the **simplest** form of flow control. The sender transmits one frame, then **stops** and **waits** for a positive acknowledgement (ACK) from the receiver before transmitting the next frame.

### Step-by-Step Operation:

1. The sender transmits a single data frame, say `F_i`, and starts a **timer**.
2. The sender **stops** transmitting and waits.
3. The receiver receives `F_i`, checks for errors (using CRC/checksum), and accepts it.
4. The receiver sends back a small **ACK (Acknowledgement)** frame to the sender.
5. The sender receives the ACK, cancels the timer, and proceeds to send the next frame `F_{i+1}`.
6. If the **timer expires** before an ACK arrives, the sender **retransmits** `F_i` (this is the ARQ aspect).
7. If a **NAK (Negative Acknowledgement)** arrives, the sender retransmits the same frame.

### Why "Stop and Wait"?

The name is self-explanatory: the sender **stops** after sending one frame and **waits** for a confirmation. The channel is **idle** most of the time — which is the major drawback.

### Formal Parameters for Stop-and-Wait:

- **Transmission time ($T_t$):** Time to push all bits of a frame onto the medium.
- **Propagation time ($T_p$):** Time for one bit to travel from sender to receiver.
- **Total cycle time:** $T_t + T_p + T_p + T_t = 2T_p + 2T_t = 2(T_p + T_t)$ (assuming ACK is instantaneous in length for simplicity).
- If ACK is non-negligible, the ACK transmission time is $T_a$, so the total is $2T_t + 2T_p + T_a$.

## 2.2 Sliding Window Flow Control — Operational Logic

In the sliding window protocol, the sender can transmit **multiple frames** before waiting for an acknowledgement. The maximum number of frames that can be sent is governed by the **window size**.

### Concept of the "Window":

Both sender and receiver maintain a conceptual **window** of sequence numbers:

- **Sender's window:** Defines the range of sequence numbers it can transmit. The upper edge is advanced when an ACK is received; the lower edge is advanced when a frame is sent.
- **Receiver's window:** Defines the range of acceptable sequence numbers. The window slides as frames are received and ACKed.

### Two Main Variants (KTU 2024 High-Priority):

| Variant | Receiver Behavior on Out-of-Order Frame | Complexity |
|---------|------------------------------------------|------------|
| **Go-Back-N ARQ** | **Discards** the out-of-order frame and all subsequent frames; resends from the lost frame onwards. | Lower (single timer) |
| **Selective Repeat ARQ** | **Buffers** the out-of-order frame in memory; resends only the specific lost frame later. | Higher (multiple timers) |

### Step-by-Step Operation of Sliding Window (Generic):

1. The sender is given a window size of **N** (e.g., N = 4 in a 3-bit sequence numbering).
2. The sender transmits frames 0, 1, 2, 3 **back-to-back** without waiting.
3. As each ACK arrives (e.g., ACK 0, ACK 1, ...), the window **slides** forward, freeing up the next sequence number.
4. The sender can now transmit a new frame using the newly freed sequence number.
5. The receiver's job is to deliver frames in order to the upper layer and send cumulative or selective ACKs.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cheat Sheet — Master these formulas for the KTU exam.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Description |
|---|-----------|---------|-------------|
| 1 | **Transmission time** | $T_t = \dfrac{L}{R}$ | $L$ = frame length in bits, $R$ = data rate in bps |
| 2 | **Propagation time** | $T_p = \dfrac{d}{v}$ | $d$ = distance, $v$ = propagation speed |
| 3 | **Parameter $a$** | $a = \dfrac{T_p}{T_t}$ | Ratio of propagation to transmission time |
| 4 | **Bandwidth-Delay Product (BDP)** | $B = R \times T_p$ | Number of bits "in-flight" in the channel |
| 5 | **Stop-and-Wait Efficiency** | $U = \dfrac{1}{1 + 2a}$ | Fraction of time channel is used for data |
| 6 | **Stop-and-Wait Throughput** | $S = \dfrac{L}{T_t + 2T_p} \times \dfrac{1}{1}$ | Effective bits per second |
| 7 | **Sliding Window Efficiency** | $U = \dfrac{N}{1 + 2a}$ | Valid only when $N \geq 2a+1$ |
| 8 | **Sliding Window Throughput** | $S = \dfrac{N \cdot L}{T_t + 2T_p}$ | When $N \geq 2a+1$ |
| 9 | **Maximum Window Size (No ACK loss)** | $W_{\max} = 2a + 1$ | Minimum $N$ to keep pipe full |
| 10 | **Min Sequence Number Bits (S&W)** | $k = 1$ | Only 0 and 1 are needed |
| 11 | **Min Sequence Number Bits (Go-Back-N)** | $k = N$ | Up to $2^k - 1$ sequence numbers |
| 12 | **Min Sequence Number Bits (Selective Repeat)** | $k = 2N$ | Up to $2^k - 1$ sequence numbers |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Stop-and-Wait** is used in **Bluetooth SCO links**, **simple serial protocols** (e.g., XMODEM), and **hardware flow control (XON/XOFF)** in UARTs.
- **Sliding Window** is the foundation of **TCP** (with a 16-bit or larger window), **HDLC**, **PPP**, and **802.11** MAC acknowledgements.
- The **efficiency formula $U = N/(1+2a)$** is the mathematical heart of why satellite links (large $T_p$) and high-speed LANs (small $T_t$) have very different optimum window sizes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** On a geostationary satellite link with $T_p \approx 250$ ms, if $T_t = 1$ ms, then $a = 250$. To keep the channel busy, the window size must be at least $2(250) + 1 = 501$ frames. This is why TCP on satellite links requires a **large receive window** (RFC 1323 window scaling).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Transmission Time ($T_t$)

### Given:
- A frame of length $L$ bits
- A data rate of $R$ bits per second

### Logical Steps:
The **transmission time** is the time required for the sender to push all the bits of one frame onto the physical medium. If the medium can carry $R$ bits every second, then to send $L$ bits, we divide the total bits by the rate.

$$
T_t = \dfrac{\text{Number of bits}}{\text{Bits transmitted per second}} = \dfrac{L}{R}
$$

The unit of $T_t$ is **seconds**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Propagation Time ($T_p$)

### Given:
- Distance between sender and receiver: $d$ meters
- Speed of signal in the medium: $v$ meters/second

### Logical Steps:
Propagation time is the time it takes for **one bit** to travel from the sender's antenna to the receiver's antenna. This is purely a function of distance and signal speed.

$$
T_p = \dfrac{\text{Distance}}{\text{Signal speed}} = \dfrac{d}{v}
$$

For copper cable, $v \approx 2 \times 10^8$ m/s. For optical fiber, $v \approx 2 \times 10^8$ m/s. For free-space radio, $v = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Stop-and-Wait Efficiency ($U$)

### Setup:
Consider a single complete cycle of Stop-and-Wait:
1. Sender transmits one data frame: time $= T_t$
2. Frame propagates to receiver: time $= T_p$
3. Receiver immediately sends ACK (assume ACK is zero length for simplicity): time $= 0$
4. ACK propagates back to sender: time $= T_p$

### Total Cycle Time:
$$
T_{\text{cycle}} = T_t + T_p + T_p = T_t + 2T_p
$$

### Useful (Data) Time:
The channel is only used for useful data during $T_t$. The rest of the time, it carries either the ACK or is idle.

$$
T_{\text{useful}} = T_t
$$

### Efficiency Definition:
Efficiency is the ratio of useful time to total cycle time.

$$
U = \dfrac{T_{\text{useful}}}{T_{\text{cycle}}} = \dfrac{T_t}{T_t + 2T_p}
$$

### Substituting the Parameter $a$:
Recall that $a = \dfrac{T_p}{T_t}$, so $T_p = a \cdot T_t$.

$$
U = \dfrac{T_t}{T_t + 2(a \cdot T_t)} = \dfrac{T_t}{T_t(1 + 2a)} = \dfrac{1}{1 + 2a}
$$

### Final Result:
$$
U_{\text{S\&W}} = \dfrac{1}{1 + 2a}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** This is one of the most frequently asked derivations. You **must** show all four steps: cycle time → useful time → ratio → substitution. Skipping the substitution step costs full marks.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Sliding Window Efficiency ($U$)

### Setup:
With a window of size $N$, the sender can transmit $N$ frames back-to-back before the first ACK arrives.

### Time to Transmit $N$ Frames:
$$
T_{\text{tx, N}} = N \cdot T_t
$$

### Time Until First ACK Arrives (Same as One Frame Cycle):
$$
T_{\text{cycle}} = T_t + 2T_p
$$

### Efficiency:
The sender is busy for $N \cdot T_t$ during the cycle, but the channel cycle is $T_t + 2T_p$. The ratio gives efficiency.

$$
U = \dfrac{N \cdot T_t}{T_t + 2T_p}
$$

### Substituting $T_p = a \cdot T_t$:

$$
U = \dfrac{N \cdot T_t}{T_t + 2a \cdot T_t} = \dfrac{N}{1 + 2a}
$$

### Case Analysis:
| Condition | Result | Interpretation |
|-----------|--------|----------------|
| $N < 2a+1$ | $U = \dfrac{N}{1+2a} < 1$ | Window is the bottleneck |
| $N \geq 2a+1$ | $U = 1$ (100%) | Channel is the bottleneck; fully utilized |
| $N \gg 2a+1$ | $U = 1$ | Cannot exceed 100% efficiency |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** Once $N \geq 2a+1$, increasing the window size further **does not** increase efficiency. The channel is already fully utilized. This is the basis of **TCP's window scaling** — beyond a point, a larger window offers no gain.

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Throughput in Stop-and-Wait

Throughput $S$ is the number of useful bits transmitted per second.

### Definition:
In one cycle of duration $T_t + 2T_p$, we deliver $L$ useful bits (one frame).

$$
S = \dfrac{L}{T_t + 2T_p}
$$

### Substituting $T_t = L/R$:

$$
S = \dfrac{L}{\dfrac{L}{R} + 2T_p} = \dfrac{L \cdot R}{L + 2R \cdot T_p}
$$

### Maximum Throughput:
When $T_p \to 0$ (or $a \to 0$):

$$
S_{\max} = R
$$

The maximum theoretical throughput equals the raw channel rate — but only if the channel is fully utilized.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Comparison

| Property | Stop-and-Wait | Go-Back-N | Selective Repeat |
|----------|---------------|-----------|------------------|
| Window size (sender) | 1 | N (≥ 1) | N (≥ 1) |
| Window size (receiver) | 1 | 1 | N |
| Min sequence numbers | 2 | $N+1$ | $2N$ |
| Wasted bandwidth on error | Whole frame | All N frames | Just 1 frame |
| Receiver complexity | Very low | Low | High (buffer needed) |
| Link utilization (low $a$) | Good | Good | Good |
| Link utilization (high $a$) | **Poor** | Good | Good |
| Number of timers | 1 | 1 | N |

## 3.7 Numerical Worked Example (Full KTU Style)

### Problem:
A channel has a data rate of $R = 4$ kbps and a one-way propagation delay of $T_p = 20$ ms. Frame size is $L = 200$ bits. Compute:
(a) Transmission time
(b) Parameter $a$
(c) Stop-and-Wait efficiency
(d) Sliding Window efficiency for $N = 4$

### Part (a): Transmission Time

$$
T_t = \dfrac{L}{R} = \dfrac{200 \text{ bits}}{4000 \text{ bps}}
$$

$$
T_t = 0.05 \text{ s} = 50 \text{ ms}
$$

### Part (b): Parameter $a$

$$
a = \dfrac{T_p}{T_t} = \dfrac{20 \text{ ms}}{50 \text{ ms}} = 0.4
$$

### Part (c): Stop-and-Wait Efficiency

$$
U_{\text{S\&W}} = \dfrac{1}{1 + 2a} = \dfrac{1}{1 + 0.8} = \dfrac{1}{1.8} \approx 0.5556
$$

$$
U_{\text{S\&W}} \approx 55.56\%
$$

### Part (d): Sliding Window Efficiency for $N = 4$

$$
U_{\text{SW}} = \dfrac{N}{1 + 2a} = \dfrac{4}{1.8} \approx 2.222
$$

Since efficiency cannot exceed 1, the window is large enough to keep the channel **fully busy** (100% utilization). The actual utilization is **capped at 100%** because $4 \geq 2(0.4) + 1 = 1.8$.

$$
U_{\text{SW}} = 1.0 \; (100\%)
$$

### Verification of Throughput:
$$
S_{\text{S\&W}} = U \cdot R = 0.5556 \times 4000 = 2222.2 \text{ bps}
$$

$$
S_{\text{SW}} = 1.0 \times 4000 = 4000 \text{ bps}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Stop-and-Wait Sequence Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant S as Sender
    participant R as Receiver
    S->>R: Data Frame 0
    Note over S: Sender starts timer T
    Note over S: Sender STOPS transmitting
    R->>S: ACK 0
    Note over S: Timer cancelled
    S->>R: Data Frame 1
    Note over S: Sender starts timer T
    Note over S: Sender STOPS transmitting
    R->>S: ACK 1
    Note over S: Timer cancelled
    S->>R: Data Frame 2
    Note over R: Frame 2 LOST in transit
    Note over S: Timer expires
    S->>R: Data Frame 2 (retransmit)
    R->>S: ACK 2
```

## 4.2 Sliding Window Sender/Receiver Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph SENDERSIDE["SENDER SIDE"]
        direction TB
        appLayer["Application Layer Data"]
        swBuf["Sender Window Buffer N=4"]
        seqNum["Sequence Number Generator"]
        txm["Transmitter to Physical Layer"]
        appLayer --> swBuf
        swBuf --> txm
        seqNum --> swBuf
    end

    subgraph PHYCHANNEL["PHYSICAL CHANNEL"]
        direction LR
        medium["Bit Stream on the Wire"]
    end

    subgraph RECEIVERSIDE["RECEIVER SIDE"]
        direction TB
        rxml["Receiver from Physical Layer"]
        rwBuf["Receiver Window Buffer"]
        ackGen["ACK Generator"]
        deliver["Delivers in-order frames to upper layer"]
        rxml --> rwBuf
        rwBuf --> deliver
        rwBuf --> ackGen
    end

    txm --> medium
    medium --> rxml
    ackGen --> medium
    medium --> swBuf
```

## 4.3 Sliding Window Timeline Diagram

```mermaid
graph TB
    t0["T=0: Send F0"]
    t1["T=Tt: F0 fully transmitted"]
    t2["T=Tt+Tp: F0 reaches receiver"]
    t3["T=Tt+2Tp: ACK 0 reaches sender"]
    t4["T=Tt+2Tp+Tt: F1 fully transmitted"]
    t5["T=2Tt+2Tp: F1 reaches receiver"]
    t6["T=2Tt+3Tp: ACK 1 reaches sender"]

    t0 --> t1 --> t2 --> t3 --> t4 --> t5 --> t6
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Timeline:**
> - The **shaded region** (between $T_t$ and $T_t + 2T_p$) is the **idle gap** — the sender is waiting for an ACK.
> - The **stop-and-wait** channel is idle for $2T_p$ seconds per cycle.
> - With sliding window, the sender fills this gap with more frames.

## 4.4 Window Sliding Mechanism (Go-Back-N)

```mermaid
graph LR
    stateA["Initial: Window = 0,1,2,3 sent"]
    stateB["After ACK 0: Window = 1,2,3,4"]
    stateC["After ACK 1: Window = 2,3,4,5"]
    stateD["After ACK 2: Window = 3,4,5,6"]
    stateE["Frame 4 LOST - Sender resends 4,5,6"]

    stateA --> stateB --> stateC --> stateD
    stateD --> stateE
    stateE --> stateF["After all ACKs: Window keeps sliding"]
```

## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Flow Control Stack

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph APP["APPLICATION LAYER"]
        dataSrc["Data Source"]
    end

    subgraph DLL["DATA LINK LAYER - FLOW CONTROL MODULE"]
        fc["Flow Control Engine"]
        win["Window State Manager"]
        seq["Sequence Number Allocator"]
        ack["Acknowledgement Processor"]
        timer["Retransmission Timer"]
    end

    subgraph PHY["PHYSICAL LAYER"]
        bits["Bit Stream Transmitter"]
    end

    dataSrc -->|Application data| fc
    fc -->|Request seq num| seq
    seq -->|Allocated seq num| fc
    fc -->|Frame with seq num| bits
    bits -->|Transmitted bits| medium((Channel))
    medium -->|Received bits| rxml2["Receiver Input"]
    rxml2 -->|Frame| ack
    ack -->|ACK frame| bits
    ack -->|Update window| win
    win -->|Slide window| fc
    fc -->|Deliver data| dataSink["Application Layer Data Sink"]
    timer -->|Timeout event| fc
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Define Flow Control. Why is it needed at the Data Link Layer?**

### Model Answer (3 marks):
**Definition (1 mark):** Flow control is a Data Link Layer mechanism that regulates the rate of data transmission from the sender to the receiver, ensuring that the sender does not transmit data faster than the receiver can process it.

**Why needed (2 marks):** The receiver has a finite buffer. If the sender is faster, the receiver's buffer may overflow, leading to **frame loss** and retransmissions. Flow control prevents this by introducing a feedback mechanism (ACK/NAK) from the receiver to the sender, ensuring the sender knows when the receiver is ready for more data.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Compare Stop-and-Wait and Sliding Window flow control in terms of efficiency.**

### Model Answer (3 marks):
- **Stop-and-Wait** has an efficiency of $U = \dfrac{1}{1+2a}$. It uses a single frame window (N=1), so the channel is idle during the ACK wait time. It is **inefficient for high-latency, high-bandwidth links** (large $a$).
- **Sliding Window** has an efficiency of $U = \dfrac{N}{1+2a}$ (capped at 1). It uses a window of N frames and keeps the channel busy, achieving **full utilization** when $N \geq 2a+1$.
- Stop-and-Wait is **simpler** but slow; Sliding Window is **more complex** but far more efficient on long-distance or high-speed links.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** A very common mistake students make is to forget that the ACK also takes **propagation time** to travel back. If the question says "compute total time for one cycle," you **must** include both forward propagation AND return propagation. Missing $T_p$ for the ACK return costs 2 to 3 marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Modified]

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** Explain the operation of **Stop-and-Wait flow control** with a neat timing diagram. Derive the expression for the efficiency of the protocol in terms of the parameter $a = T_p / T_t$.

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** A satellite link has a data rate of $R = 1$ Mbps and a one-way propagation delay of $T_p = 250$ ms. The frame size is $L = 125$ bytes. Calculate:
1. The transmission time $T_t$
2. The parameter $a$
3. The efficiency using Stop-and-Wait
4. The minimum window size required to achieve 100% efficiency in a Sliding Window protocol.

---

### Model Answer for Question A:

#### Part (a) [7 marks]:

**Operation (3 marks):**
Stop-and-Wait flow control works as follows:
1. The sender transmits one data frame and starts a timer.
2. The sender **stops** and waits for an acknowledgement.
3. The receiver checks the frame for errors. If correct, it sends an ACK back; if not, it discards and may send a NAK.
4. Upon receiving the ACK, the sender transmits the next frame.
5. If the timer expires before an ACK arrives, the sender **retransmits** the same frame.

**Timing Diagram (2 marks):**
```
Sender:   |--F0--|--idle------|----F1----|--idle------|
Receiver: |----F0 received----|----------|----F1 received----
Time:     0      Tt          Tt+2Tp     2Tt+2Tp     3Tt+2Tp
```
The channel is busy for $T_t$ (data) and $T_p$ (ACK return propagation) — total active time = $T_t + T_p$ out of a total cycle of $T_t + 2T_p$.

**Derivation of Efficiency (2 marks):**
- Total cycle time: $T_{\text{cycle}} = T_t + 2T_p$
- Useful time: $T_{\text{useful}} = T_t$
- Efficiency: $U = \dfrac{T_t}{T_t + 2T_p}$
- Substituting $a = T_p / T_t$ (so $T_p = aT_t$):

$$
U = \dfrac{T_t}{T_t + 2aT_t} = \dfrac{1}{1 + 2a}
$$

**Final Result:** $U = \dfrac{1}{1 + 2a}$

---

#### Part (b) [7 marks]:

**Step 1: Compute Transmission Time $T_t$ (2 marks)**

Convert the frame size to bits: $L = 125 \text{ bytes} = 125 \times 8 = 1000$ bits.

$$
T_t = \dfrac{L}{R} = \dfrac{1000 \text{ bits}}{1 \times 10^6 \text{ bps}} = 1 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 1 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Parameter $a$ (1 mark)**

$$
a = \dfrac{T_p}{T_t} = \dfrac{250 \text{ ms}}{1 \text{ ms}} = 250
$$

**Step 3: Compute Stop-and-Wait Efficiency (2 marks)**

$$
U_{\text{S\&W}} = \dfrac{1}{1 + 2a} = \dfrac{1}{1 + 500} = \dfrac{1}{501} \approx 0.001996 \approx 0.2\%
$$

**Step 4: Minimum Window Size for 100% Efficiency (2 marks)**

For the channel to be fully utilized:

$$
N \geq 2a + 1
$$

$$
N \geq 2(250) + 1 = 501
$$

**The minimum window size is $N = 501$ frames.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** On a geostationary satellite link, Stop-and-Wait is **catastrophically inefficient** (only 0.2% utilization). A sliding window of at least 501 frames is required to fully utilize the 1 Mbps channel.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** Explain the **Sliding Window flow control** mechanism with a diagram. Compare it with the Stop-and-Wait protocol.

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** A system uses a Sliding Window protocol with a window size of $N = 7$. The frame size is $L = 1000$ bits, the link bandwidth is $R = 50$ kbps, and the one-way propagation delay is $T_p = 5$ ms. Calculate:
1. The transmission time $T_t$
2. The parameter $a$
3. The efficiency of the protocol
4. The throughput in bits per second.

---

### Model Answer for Question B:

#### Part (a) [7 marks]:

**Sliding Window Mechanism (4 marks):**
The Sliding Window protocol allows the sender to transmit multiple frames (up to a window size $N$) before waiting for an acknowledgement. Both sender and receiver maintain a **window** of sequence numbers:
- The **sender's window** lists the frames that can be sent.
- The **receiver's window** lists the frames that can be accepted.
- As ACKs arrive, the windows **slide** forward, allowing new frames to be sent.

**Diagram (2 marks):**

```
Sender Window: [ F0  F1  F2  F3  F4  F5  F6 ]   N=7
                --------------------------->  (slides right as ACKs arrive)

Receiver Window: [ F0  F1  F2  F3  F4  F5  F6 ]
```

**Comparison with Stop-and-Wait (1 mark):**

| Feature | Stop-and-Wait | Sliding Window |
|---------|---------------|----------------|
| Window size | 1 | N |
| Efficiency at high $a$ | Very poor | High (up to 100%) |
| Complexity | Low | Higher |
| Channel utilization | Underutilized | Fully utilized when $N \geq 2a+1$ |

---

#### Part (b) [7 marks]:

**Step 1: Transmission Time $T_t$ (1 mark)**

$$
T_t = \dfrac{L}{R} = \dfrac{1000 \text{ bits}}{50{,}000 \text{ bps}} = 0.02 \text{ s} = 20 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 2: Parameter $a$ (1 mark)**

$$
a = \dfrac{T_p}{T_t} = \dfrac{5 \text{ ms}}{20 \text{ ms}} = 0.25
$$

**Step 3: Efficiency of Sliding Window (3 marks)**

$$
U = \dfrac{N}{1 + 2a} = \dfrac{7}{1 + 2(0.25)} = \dfrac{7}{1.5} = 4.667
$$

Since efficiency cannot exceed 1, we cap it:

$$
U = 1.0 \; (100\%)
$$

This makes sense because $N = 7 \geq 2a + 1 = 1.5$, so the channel is fully busy.

**Step 4: Throughput in bits per second (2 marks)**

$$
S = U \times R = 1.0 \times 50{,}000 \text{ bps} = 50{,}000 \text{ bps} = 50 \text{ kbps}
$$

Since efficiency is 100%, throughput equals the raw channel bandwidth.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert bytes to bits.** Frame size is almost always given in **bytes** in KTU questions, but the formula needs **bits**. Always multiply by 8.
> 2. **Forgetting to add the ACK propagation time.** A complete cycle is $T_t + 2T_p$, not $T_t + T_p$. The ACK travels **back** and also has a propagation delay.
> 3. **Capping efficiency at 1.** Sliding window efficiency $N/(1+2a)$ can exceed 1 mathematically, but physically you cannot exceed 100%. Always cap it.
> 4. **Confusing Go-Back-N with Selective Repeat.** Go-Back-N resends **all** frames after a loss; Selective Repeat resends **only the missing frame**. This is a 7-mark differentiator in Part B.
> 5. **Wrong minimum window formula.** It is $N \geq 2a + 1$, **not** $N \geq 2a$ and **not** $N \geq a + 1$.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize these for the exam:**

- ☐ **Flow Control** = rate-matching between sender and receiver; prevents receiver buffer overflow.
- ☐ **Stop-and-Wait**: send 1 frame, wait for ACK. Window size = 1. Efficiency = $\dfrac{1}{1+2a}$.
- ☐ **Sliding Window**: send up to $N$ frames, pipeline the channel. Efficiency = $\dfrac{N}{1+2a}$, capped at 1.
- ☐ **Parameter $a$** = $T_p / T_t$. High $a$ (satellite) = bad for Stop-and-Wait.
- ☐ **Minimum window for 100% efficiency** = $2a + 1$.
- ☐ **Go-Back-N** = discard out-of-order frames; sender has window N, receiver has window 1. Needs $N+1$ sequence numbers.
- ☐ **Selective Repeat** = buffer out-of-order frames; both sides have window N. Needs $2N$ sequence numbers.
- ☐ **Bandwidth-Delay Product** = $R \times T_p$ = number of bits "in flight" = pipe capacity.
- ☐ **Transmission Time** $T_t = L/R$. **Propagation Time** $T_p = d/v$.
- ☐ **TCP uses Sliding Window** with cumulative ACKs and dynamic window adjustment.
- ☐ **One complete Stop-and-Wait cycle** = $T_t + 2T_p$ (NOT $T_t + T_p$).
- ☐ **Sender window slides** when ACK is received; **receiver window slides** when in-order frame is delivered.
- ☐ **Stop-and-Wait only needs 2 sequence numbers** (0 and 1). Go-Back-N needs $N+1$. Selective Repeat needs $2N$.
- ☐ **Stop-and-Wait = HDLC Asynchronous Balanced Mode (sub-mode)**; **Sliding Window = HDLC Normal Response Mode**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Error Control: Error Detection, Checksum, Bit Stuffing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT522 - COMPUTER COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Error Control: Error Detection, Checksum, Bit Stuffing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Error Control?

In digital data communication, the **bit stream** transmitted across a physical medium (copper, fibre, wireless) is never perfectly safe. Electrical noise, thermal agitation, cross-talk, fading, and impulse interference from switching circuits can flip a transmitted `0` to `1` or vice-versa. **Error Control** is the collective set of techniques used at the **Data Link Layer (DLL)** of the OSI/TCP-IP model to **detect** and optionally **correct** such bit-level corruption, ensuring that the receiver reconstructs the *exact* bit sequence the sender transmitted.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECPT522, Module 2):**
> *"Error Control refers to the mechanisms employed to detect and correct errors that occur during the transmission of data frames over an unreliable communication channel, primarily through the addition of redundant bits, frame sequencing, and acknowledgment protocols."*

The three primary sub-functions are:
1. **Error Detection** – recognise that an error has occurred (covered today).
2. **Error Correction** – locate and fix the corrupted bits (Hamming Code, FEC).
3. **Retransmission (ARQ)** – request the sender to re-transmit the frame (Stop-and-Wait, Go-Back-N, Selective Repeat).

Our focus today is on the first pillar — **Error Detection** — with special emphasis on **Checksum** and **Bit Stuffing**, which are board-favourite topics.

## 1.2 Types of Transmission Errors

| Error Type | Description | Typical Cause |
|---|---|---|
| **Single-Bit Error** | Only **1** bit in a given data unit is flipped | Very short noise burst, cosmic ray hit |
| **Burst Error** | A contiguous cluster of $n$ bits is corrupted ($n \geq 2$) | Lightning, impulse noise, deep fading |
| **Random / Multiple Errors** | Scattered, non-contiguous bit flips | Severe channel degradation |

> [!NOTE]
> In real links (DSL, Wi-Fi, LTE), **burst errors** dominate. A good detector must therefore have strong burst-detection capability, not just single-bit sensitivity.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The Postal Money Order

Imagine you post a **₹500 Money Order** to your grandmother. The amount is written in **words** ("Five Hundred Only") and in **digits** ("500/-"). When it arrives:

- If the **words** are tampered, the digits still protect the value.
- If the **digits** are tampered, the words still hold.
- If **both** are tampered, the post office rejects the order as "suspicious".

This is exactly what **redundancy** does in data communication. The sender transmits the *data* plus *extra check bits* computed mathematically from the data. The receiver recomputes the check and compares. A mismatch ⇒ error detected.

## 1.4 Why Redundancy?

Error detection is fundamentally a problem of **distance** in a code-space. If every valid codeword differs from every other valid codeword in **at least $d_{min}$** bit positions, then up to $d_{min} - 1$ errors can be *detected* and up to $\lfloor(d_{min} - 1)/2\rfloor$ errors can be *corrected*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No detection scheme is 100% reliable.** All error-detection codes are *probabilistic* — they only guarantee detection of error patterns within their *Hamming distance capability*. Some exotic multi-bit errors can still slip through undetected.
